4. Urate-lowering therapy if: >=2 flares/year, tophi, urate nephropathy, renal stones
5. Treat to target: serum urate <6mg/dL
6. Lifestyle: limit alcohol/purines, weight loss, hydration
meds:
Acute: Indomethacin 50mg TID x5-7d or Naproxen 500mg BID
Colchicine 1.2mg then 0.6mg 1h later (within 36h of onset)
Prednisone 30-40mg daily x5d (if NSAIDs contraindicated)
Intra-articular triamcinolone 10-40mg
Prophylaxis: Allopurinol 100mg daily (titrate to urate <6)
Febuxostat 40-80mg daily
Colchicine 0.6mg daily during ULT initiation
ddx: Septic arthritis, Pseudogout (CPPD), Cellulitis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Reactive arthritis, Trauma
labs:
Joint aspiration (gold standard): negatively birefringent needle-shaped MSU crystals
Cell count (10,000-70,000 WBC, neutrophil predominant)
Gram stain/culture (rule out septic arthritis)
Serum uric acid (may be normal during acute flare)
BMP (renal function)
CBC, ESR/CRP
Joint X-ray (chronic: punched-out erosions, overhanging edges)
Dual-energy CT (advanced)
notes:
Serum urate often NORMAL during acute flare - do not use to rule out gout
Joint aspiration is gold standard but clinical diagnosis acceptable
Allopurinol: start low (100mg), go slow, titrate q2-4wks to target urate <6
Allopurinol flare prophylaxis: colchicine 0.6mg daily for 3-6 months
HLA-B*5801 testing in Southeast Asian, African American patients before allopurinol (SJS risk)
description: A form of inflammatory arthritis caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joints, resulting from elevated uric acid levels. Classically affects the big toe.

name: Septic Arthritis
signs:
Acute monoarticular arthritis (knee most common)
Severe pain with any movement
Joint effusion
Warmth and erythema
Fever
Refusal to bear weight or move joint
Systemic illness
treatment:
1. Joint aspiration BEFORE antibiotics (but do not delay if septic)
2. Empiric IV antibiotics
3. Surgical drainage/washout (especially hip, prosthetic joints)
4. Serial aspirations or arthroscopic washout
5. Orthopedic/surgical consultation
6. Duration: typically 2-4 weeks IV (varies)
meds:
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (empiric, covers MRSA)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (add for gram-negative coverage)
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (if MSSA confirmed)
Ceftazidime 2g IV q8h (if Pseudomonas suspected - IVDU)
Gonococcal: Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM daily
ddx: Gout, Pseudogout, Reactive arthritis, Rheumatoid arthritis flare, Lyme arthritis, Trauma with hemarthrosis, Osteomyelitis
labs:
Joint aspiration (MANDATORY): cell count >50,000 WBC (usually >100k), >75% PMNs
Gram stain (positive in 50-75%)
Synovial fluid culture
Blood cultures x2
CBC, ESR, CRP
BMP
Gonococcal NAAT (urethral, cervical, pharyngeal, rectal)
Joint X-ray (baseline)
MRI (if concern for adjacent osteomyelitis)
notes:
Most common: S. aureus (adults), N. gonorrhoeae (young sexually active adults)
Gonococcal: often migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, dermatitis before settling in one joint
Prosthetic joint infection: lower WBC threshold (>1,100)
Synovial WBC >50,000 with >75% PMNs = septic until proven otherwise
Do NOT delay antibiotics for aspiration if sepsis present
Lyme arthritis: large joint (knee), may have lower WBC counts
description: A joint infection, usually bacterial, that can rapidly destroy cartilage and bone if not urgently drained and treated with antibiotics. A surgical emergency.

name: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
signs:
High fever (>104F)
Muscle rigidity (lead-pipe)
Altered mental status
Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis)
Elevated CK
Leukocytosis
Metabolic acidosis
Tremor
treatment:
1. Stop offending agent immediately
2. Aggressive cooling
3. IV fluids
4. Dantrolene for severe rigidity
5. Bromocriptine or amantadine
6. Benzodiazepines for agitation
7. ICU admission
8. Monitor for rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, DIC
meds:
Dantrolene 1-2.5mg/kg IV q6h (max 10mg/kg/day)
Bromocriptine 2.5mg PO/NGT q8h
Lorazepam 1-2mg IV q6h (agitation/rigidity)
IV NS aggressive hydration
Sodium bicarbonate if rhabdomyolysis
ddx: Serotonin syndrome, Malignant hyperthermia, Lethal catatonia, Meningitis/encephalitis, Thyroid storm, Anticholinergic toxicity, Heat stroke
labs:
CK (markedly elevated, often >1000)
BMP (AKI from rhabdomyolysis)
CBC (leukocytosis)
LFTs
Coags (DIC)
Urinalysis (myoglobinuria)
Lactate
ABG/VBG
Iron (low)
Blood cultures (r/o infection)
notes:
Caused by dopamine antagonists: haloperidol, droperidol, metoclopramide, all antipsychotics
Onset: days to weeks after starting or dose increase
NMS vs Serotonin syndrome: NMS = rigidity, slow onset, bradyreflexia; SS = clonus, rapid onset, hyperreflexia
NMS vs Malignant hyperthermia: MH = succinylcholine/inhalational anesthetics, masseter rigidity
Mortality 5-20% with treatment
Reintroduce antipsychotic cautiously (different class) after 2+ weeks
description: A rare, life-threatening reaction to antipsychotic (neuroleptic) medications causing severe muscle rigidity, high fever, altered consciousness, and autonomic instability.

name: Serotonin Syndrome
signs:
Clonus (spontaneous, inducible, ocular)
Agitation/restlessness
Diaphoresis
Hyperthermia
Tachycardia
Hyperreflexia
Tremor
Muscle rigidity (late)
Diarrhea
Dilated pupils
treatment:
1. Stop all serotonergic agents
2. Benzodiazepines for agitation
3. Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist)
4. Active cooling if hyperthermic
5. IV fluids
6. Avoid paralytics unless intubation needed
7. ICU for severe cases
8. Usually resolves within 24-72h
meds:
Cyproheptadine 12mg PO/NGT then 4mg q2h PRN (max 32mg/day)
Lorazepam 1-2mg IV q15-30min PRN
IV NS hydration
Avoid dantrolene (not effective in SS)
External cooling measures
ddx: NMS, Anticholinergic toxicity, Malignant hyperthermia, Sympathomimetic overdose, Meningitis, Thyroid storm
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (Hunter criteria)
CK (may be elevated)
BMP
CBC
LFTs
Coags
Urinalysis
Lactate
Tox screen
notes:
Hunter criteria: serotonergic agent + ANY of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, ocular clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, tremor + hyperreflexia, temp >38 + ocular/inducible clonus
Common drugs: SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol, triptans, linezolid, methylene blue, dextromethorphan, MDMA
MAOI + SSRI = most dangerous combination
Key differentiator from NMS: clonus and hyperreflexia (NMS has rigidity and hyporeflexia)
description: A potentially life-threatening condition caused by excess serotonin activity in the nervous system, usually from drug interactions. Characterized by clonus, agitation, and hyperthermia.

name: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN
signs:
Prodrome: fever, malaise, URI symptoms 1-3 days before rash
Target-like lesions evolving to bullae
Flaccid blisters, Nikolsky sign positive
Mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital)
Skin pain/tenderness
Epidermal detachment
SJS: <10% BSA; TEN: >30% BSA
treatment:
1. Stop offending drug immediately
2. Transfer to burn center/ICU
3. Supportive care (wound care, fluid replacement, pain management)
4. Ophthalmology consult (corneal damage)
5. Cyclosporine may reduce progression (evidence emerging)
6. Avoid systemic corticosteroids (controversial)
7. SCORTEN for prognosis
meds:
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
Pain management: morphine, acetaminophen
Cyclosporine 3-5mg/kg/day (controversial but growing evidence)
Silver-based wound dressings
Ophthalmologic drops/lubricants
Avoid sulfonamides, corticosteroids debated
ddx: Erythema multiforme, DRESS syndrome, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Pemphigus vulgaris, Autoimmune bullous diseases, Acute GVHD
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Skin biopsy (full-thickness epidermal necrosis)
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Blood cultures (secondary infection)
Photo documentation
SCORTEN score at day 1 and day 3
notes:
Most common culprits: allopurinol, sulfonamides, carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, NSAIDs (oxicam), antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins)
SCORTEN: age >40, malignancy, BSA >10%, tachycardia, BUN >28, glucose >252, bicarb <20
HLA-B*5801: allopurinol SJS risk (Southeast Asian, African American)
HLA-B*1502: carbamazepine SJS risk (Southeast Asian)
Ocular complications in 50-90%: symblepharon, corneal scarring, dry eye
description: A severe, life-threatening skin reaction usually triggered by medications, where the skin and mucous membranes blister and detach. TEN involves >30% body surface area.

name: Pre-eclampsia / Eclampsia
signs:
HTN (>=140/90 after 20wk gestation)
Proteinuria
Headache
Visual changes (scotomata)
RUQ/epigastric pain
Edema (facial)
Hyperreflexia/clonus
Seizures (eclampsia)
Pulmonary edema
HELLP: hemolysis, elevated LFTs, low platelets
treatment:
1. Delivery is definitive treatment
2. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
3. Antihypertensives for severe HTN (>=160/110)
4. >=37wk: deliver
5. <37wk with severe features: deliver after stabilization
6. <37wk without severe features: expectant management with close monitoring
7. Betamethasone if <34wk (fetal lung maturity)
meds:
Magnesium sulfate 4-6g IV bolus then 1-2g/h infusion
Labetalol 20mg IV then 40mg then 80mg q10min
Hydralazine 5-10mg IV q20min
Nifedipine 10-20mg PO q20-30min
Betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses 24h apart (if <34wk)
Calcium gluconate 1g IV (magnesium antidote)
ddx: Gestational HTN, Chronic HTN, HELLP syndrome, TTP/HUS, Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, SLE flare, Pheochromocytoma
labs:
BP monitoring
24h urine protein or protein/creatinine ratio
CBC (platelets, hemolysis)
BMP (creatinine, uric acid)
LFTs (AST/ALT)
LDH
Peripheral smear (schistocytes)
Coags
Fetal monitoring (NST, BPP, umbilical artery Doppler)
notes:
Severe features: SBP >=160, DBP >=110, platelets <100k, Cr >1.1, LFTs >2x normal, pulmonary edema, cerebral/visual symptoms
Eclampsia: seizures in pre-eclampsia setting - give MgSO4 first, then deliver
MgSO4 monitoring: reflexes (lose at 10), respiratory rate (lose at 15), cardiac arrest (>25)
HELLP: can occur without significant HTN or proteinuria
Postpartum pre-eclampsia: up to 6 weeks after delivery
description: A pregnancy complication after 20 weeks with new-onset hypertension and organ damage. Eclampsia adds seizures. The only definitive treatment is delivery.

name: Clostridioides difficile Infection
signs:
Watery diarrhea (10-15 stools/day)
Lower abdominal pain/cramping
Fever
Nausea
Anorexia
Leukocytosis (often very high, WBC >15k)
Dehydration
Abdominal distension
Toxic megacolon (severe)
treatment:
1. Stop inciting antibiotic if possible
2. Non-severe initial: fidaxomicin (preferred) or vancomycin PO
3. Severe (WBC >15k or Cr >1.5): vancomycin PO
4. Fulminant: vancomycin PO + PR + IV metronidazole
5. Surgical consultation for toxic megacolon
6. Fecal microbiota transplant for recurrent episodes
7. Contact precautions, hand washing with soap and water (not alcohol gel)
meds:
Fidaxomicin 200mg PO BID x10d (preferred, less recurrence)
Vancomycin 125mg PO QID x10d
Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h (add for fulminant)
Vancomycin retention enema 500mg in 100mL NS q6h (fulminant with ileus)
Bezlotoxumab 10mg/kg IV x1 (anti-toxin B, prevents recurrence in high-risk)
ddx: Infectious diarrhea (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter), IBD flare, Ischemic colitis, Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (non-C diff), CMV colitis
labs:
C. diff PCR (NAAT) or GDH/EIA (2-step testing)
CBC (leukocytosis, sometimes leukemoid >40k)
BMP (AKI from dehydration)
Lactate
Albumin
CT abdomen if severe (colon wall thickening, megacolon)
Abdominal X-ray (toxic megacolon: >6cm)
notes:
Risk factors: antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, cephalosporins), PPI, hospitalization, age >65
Severity: non-severe (WBC <15k and Cr <1.5), severe (WBC >=15k or Cr >=1.5), fulminant (hypotension, shock, ileus, megacolon)
Do NOT test for cure - PCR stays positive
Do NOT use anti-motility agents (loperamide)
Recurrence rate 20-25% after first episode
Spores survive alcohol-based sanitizer - use soap and water
description: A bacterial infection of the colon caused by C. difficile, producing toxins that damage the intestinal lining. Usually follows antibiotic use that disrupts normal gut flora.

name: Meningitis - Viral
signs:
Headache
Fever
Photophobia
Neck stiffness (often milder than bacterial)
Nausea/vomiting
Malaise
Myalgias
Rash (enterovirus)
Generally less toxic-appearing than bacterial
treatment:
1. Supportive care (most cases self-limited)
2. Empiric bacterial coverage until CSF results if any doubt
3. Analgesics/antipyretics
4. IV acyclovir if HSV suspected (encephalitis risk)
5. Hydration
6. Most recover in 7-10 days
meds:
Acetaminophen 1g PO q6h
Ibuprofen 600mg PO q8h
Acyclovir 10mg/kg IV q8h (if HSV suspected)
IV fluids for hydration
Ondansetron 4mg IV PRN nausea
ddx: Bacterial meningitis, HSV encephalitis, Lyme meningitis, TB meningitis, Fungal meningitis, SAH, Migraine
labs:
LP: lymphocytic pleocytosis (WBC 50-1000), normal glucose, normal-mildly elevated protein
CSF HSV PCR
CSF enterovirus PCR
CSF Gram stain/culture (to rule out bacterial)
CBC, BMP
Blood cultures
HIV test
Lyme serologies if endemic area
notes:
Most common: enteroviruses (summer/fall)
HSV-2: can cause recurrent (Mollaret) meningitis
Early bacterial meningitis can mimic viral - when in doubt, treat empirically
CSF findings: bacterial has WBC >1000 (PMNs), low glucose, high protein
Parechovirus: common cause in infants
Most viral meningitis resolves without sequelae
description: Inflammation of the meninges caused by a virus (most commonly enteroviruses). Generally less severe than bacterial meningitis and usually self-limiting.

name: Acute Liver Failure
signs:
Jaundice
Coagulopathy (INR >1.5)
Hepatic encephalopathy
RUQ tenderness
Ascites
Hypoglycemia
Cerebral edema
Multi-organ failure
treatment:
1. ICU admission
2. N-acetylcysteine (even non-APAP causes)
3. Correct coagulopathy only if active bleeding
4. Lactulose for encephalopathy
5. ICP monitoring/management
6. Treat underlying cause
7. Liver transplant evaluation emergently
8. Infection surveillance
meds:
NAC IV protocol (same as APAP overdose)
Lactulose 30mL q2h titrate to 2-3 BM/day
Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV (cerebral edema)
Hypertonic saline 23.4% for ICP crisis
FFP/cryoprecipitate only if actively bleeding
D10 infusion for hypoglycemia
ddx: APAP toxicity, Viral hepatitis (A, B), Drug reaction (DILI), Wilson disease, Autoimmune hepatitis, Budd-Chiari, HELLP/AFLP, Mushroom poisoning, Ischemic hepatitis
labs:
LFTs (AST/ALT often >1000)
PT/INR (key prognostic marker)
BMP, CBC
Ammonia
APAP level
Viral hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg, HBc IgM, HCV RNA)
Ceruloplasmin, 24h urine copper (Wilson)
Autoimmune markers (ANA, ASMA, IgG)
Lactate
Arterial ammonia
Factor V level (prognostic)
notes:
Definition: acute liver injury + coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + encephalopathy in patient WITHOUT pre-existing liver disease
Hyperacute (<7d): better prognosis (APAP, ischemic)
Acute (7-21d): viral hepatitis
Subacute (21d-26wk): drug reactions, Wilson
Kings College Criteria for transplant listing
APAP: pH <7.3, or INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + Grade 3-4 encephalopathy
description: Rapid deterioration of liver function in a patient without pre-existing liver disease, with coagulopathy and encephalopathy developing over days to weeks.

name: Diabetic Foot Ulcer / Osteomyelitis
signs:
Non-healing foot ulcer
Wound drainage/purulence
Periwound erythema
Swelling
Fever (if systemic)
Probe-to-bone positive
Decreased sensation (neuropathy)
Pedal pulses diminished (PAD)
treatment:
1. Wound assessment and classification (Wagner, UT classification)
2. Offloading (total contact cast, therapeutic footwear)
3. Debridement of necrotic tissue
4. Antibiotics for infection
5. Vascular assessment (ABI, consider revascularization)
6. Tight glucose control
7. Surgery/amputation if extensive
8. Bone biopsy for osteomyelitis confirmation
meds:
Mild: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875mg BID or TMP-SMX DS + Amoxicillin-clavulanate
Moderate: Ampicillin-sulbactam 3g IV q6h or Ertapenem 1g IV daily
Severe: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h + Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV
Osteomyelitis: 6 weeks antibiotics (guided by bone culture)
ddx: Cellulitis without ulcer, Charcot neuroarthropathy, Gout, Venous stasis ulcer, Arterial insufficiency ulcer, Necrotizing fasciitis
labs:
CBC, BMP, HbA1c
ESR, CRP (elevated in osteomyelitis)
Wound culture (deep tissue or bone, NOT superficial swab)
Blood cultures if systemic
Probe-to-bone test (89% specific for osteomyelitis)
X-ray foot (may be normal early)
MRI (gold standard for osteomyelitis)
ABI/vascular studies
notes:
Probe-to-bone: if you can touch bone through the ulcer, osteomyelitis very likely
ESR >70 strongly suggests osteomyelitis
MRI sensitivity >90% for osteomyelitis
Bone biopsy/culture is gold standard for guiding antibiotic therapy
S. aureus most common pathogen in osteomyelitis
Multidisciplinary approach: ID, vascular, podiatry, wound care, endocrine
description: A chronic wound on the foot of a diabetic patient, often complicated by bone infection (osteomyelitis), resulting from neuropathy, poor circulation, and impaired healing.

name: Pneumonia - Hospital Acquired
signs:
New fever
New or worsening cough
Purulent secretions
New infiltrate on CXR
Leukocytosis or leukopenia
Decreased oxygenation
Onset >=48h after admission
treatment:
1. Empiric antibiotics covering Pseudomonas and MRSA
2. De-escalate based on cultures at 48-72h
3. Duration: 7 days (guided by procalcitonin trending)
4. Avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum coverage
5. Elevate HOB 30-45 degrees
6. DVT prophylaxis
7. Stress ulcer prophylaxis
meds:
Anti-pseudomonal: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h OR Cefepime 2g IV q8h OR Meropenem 1g IV q8h
Anti-MRSA: Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h OR Linezolid 600mg IV q12h
Adjust per cultures and sensitivities
Nebulized antibiotics for MDR gram-negatives (adjunct)
ddx: Atelectasis, PE, ARDS, CHF, Aspiration pneumonitis, Drug fever
labs:
CXR or CT chest
Sputum culture/Gram stain (before antibiotics)
Blood cultures x2
CBC with differential
BMP
ProCalcitonin (guide duration)
ABG if hypoxemic
Consider bronchoscopy with BAL for ICU patients
notes:
HAP: >=48h after admission, not intubated
VAP: >=48h after intubation
Risk factors for MDR: prior IV abx in 90d, >=5d hospitalization before onset, septic shock, ARDS, prior MRSA/Pseudomonas
Double cover Pseudomonas only if risk factors for MDR AND septic shock
VAP bundle: HOB elevation, daily sedation vacation, DVT/PUD prophylaxis, daily readiness to extubate assessment
description: A lung infection that develops 48 or more hours after hospital admission, often involving drug-resistant organisms like MRSA and Pseudomonas.

name: Acute Limb Ischemia
signs:
6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesias, Paralysis, Poikilothermia (cold)
Sudden onset leg pain
Absence of distal pulses
Cool pale limb
Mottling
Muscle tenderness
Sensory loss
Motor weakness (late, ominous)
treatment:
1. Immediate anticoagulation with heparin
2. Vascular surgery consultation STAT
3. Rutherford classification guides treatment
4. Viable (I): anticoagulate, arteriography
5. Threatened (II): revascularization (embolectomy, bypass, endovascular)
6. Irreversible (III): amputation
7. Monitor for reperfusion syndrome/compartment syndrome
meds:
Heparin 80u/kg IV bolus then 18u/kg/h
Thrombolytics (catheter-directed tPA) for selected cases
Analgesia: morphine/fentanyl IV
NS hydration (prevent rhabdomyolysis post-reperfusion)
Sodium bicarbonate (if reperfusion injury)
ddx: Chronic limb-threatening ischemia, DVT (phlegmasia), Compartment syndrome, Aortic dissection with limb malperfusion, Cholesterol embolization
labs:
ABI (may be unmeasurable)
CT angiography or conventional angiography
Duplex ultrasound
CBC, BMP, coags
CK (reperfusion rhabdomyolysis)
Lactate
Type and crossmatch
ECG (atrial fibrillation as embolic source)
notes:
Most common cause: embolism (AFib) or in-situ thrombosis (atherosclerosis)
Golden period: 6 hours to revascularize before irreversible damage
Rutherford: I = viable, IIA = marginally threatened, IIB = immediately threatened, III = irreversible
Motor deficit = IIB or III = most urgent
Reperfusion injury: hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, myoglobinuria, compartment syndrome
Watch for reperfusion-induced cardiac arrest from hyperkalemia
description: Sudden decrease in blood flow to an extremity (usually a leg) threatening its viability. Typically caused by an embolism or in-situ thrombosis. A surgical emergency.

name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
signs:
Thunderclap headache (worst headache of life)
Sudden onset, maximal at onset
Neck stiffness
Photophobia
Nausea/vomiting
Loss of consciousness (brief)
Seizures
Focal deficits (CN III palsy)
Sentinel headache (weeks before)
treatment:
1. CT head without contrast STAT
2. LP if CT negative but high suspicion
3. CTA to identify aneurysm
4. Secure aneurysm (endovascular coiling or surgical clipping)
5. Nimodipine for vasospasm prevention
6. EVD for hydrocephalus
7. ICU monitoring
8. Manage blood pressure
9. Seizure prophylaxis (short-term)
meds:
Nimodipine 60mg PO q4h x21 days
Labetalol or nicardipine for BP control (target SBP <160 pre-securing, <180 post)
Fosphenytoin or levetiracetam (seizure prophylaxis)
Aminocaproic acid or TXA (short-term if delay securing aneurysm)
Mannitol/hypertonic saline for ICP
Analgesics for headache
ddx: Migraine, Tension headache, Meningitis, ICH, Cervical artery dissection, Posterior reversible encephalopathy (PRES), Thunderclap headache (benign)
labs:
CT head (>95% sensitive within 6h)
LP: xanthochromia, elevated opening pressure, RBCs (non-clearing)
CTA head (aneurysm identification)
Conventional angiography (gold standard)
CBC, BMP, coags
Troponin (neurogenic stunned myocardium)
ECG (diffuse T-wave inversions, QT prolongation)
notes:
CT sensitivity: ~98% at 6h, ~93% at 12h, ~50% at 5 days
Hunt-Hess grade: I (mild HA) to V (coma) - prognostic
Fisher grade: based on CT blood pattern - predicts vasospasm risk
Vasospasm: days 4-14 post-SAH, monitor with transcranial Doppler
Triple H therapy (hypertension, hypervolemia, hemodilution) being replaced by induced hypertension alone
Rebleeding risk highest in first 24h
description: Bleeding into the space between the brain and its surrounding membranes, most commonly from a ruptured aneurysm. Classically presents with sudden thunderclap headache.

name: Tension Pneumothorax
signs:
Severe respiratory distress
Hypotension/shock
Tracheal deviation (away from affected side)
Absent breath sounds (affected side)
Distended neck veins
Tachycardia
Cyanosis
Subcutaneous emphysema
Cardiac arrest (PEA)
treatment:
1. CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS - do NOT delay for imaging
2. Immediate needle decompression: 14-16g needle, 2nd ICS MCL (or 4th/5th ICS AAL)
3. Follow immediately with chest tube: 5th ICS anterior axillary line
4. High-flow oxygen
5. Fluid resuscitation if hypotensive
meds:
Supplemental oxygen 100% NRB
IV fluid bolus
Pain management after decompression
Chest tube: 28-32Fr
ddx: Massive PE, Cardiac tamponade, Hemothorax, Simple pneumothorax, MI, Anaphylaxis
labs:
This is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Post-decompression CXR
ABG
Point-of-care US (absent lung sliding, lung point)
notes:
Tension PTX = air enters pleural space but cannot escape (one-way valve)
Causes: trauma, mechanical ventilation, central line placement, lung biopsy, CPR
Needle decompression is temporizing - MUST place chest tube after
5th ICS AAL may be more reliable than 2nd ICS MCL for decompression (less adipose tissue)
In cardiac arrest: bilateral finger thoracostomies
description: A life-threatening form of pneumothorax where a one-way valve mechanism traps air in the pleural space, progressively compressing the heart and opposite lung.

name: Rhabdomyolysis
signs:
Muscle pain/weakness/tenderness
Dark tea/cola-colored urine (myoglobinuria)
Swelling of affected muscles
Fever
Nausea/vomiting
Confusion (if AKI/electrolyte disturbance)
Oliguria/anuria (AKI)
Abdominal pain
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (NS 200-300mL/h)
2. Target UOP 200-300mL/h
3. Monitor and correct electrolytes aggressively
4. Treat hyperkalemia emergently if present
5. Avoid nephrotoxins
6. Dialysis if AKI with refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload
7. Compartment syndrome monitoring
8. Treat underlying cause
meds:
NS 1-2L/h initially then 200-300mL/h (massive volumes may be needed)
Sodium bicarbonate 150mEq in 1L D5W (alkalinize urine to pH >6.5, controversial)
Calcium gluconate 1g IV if hyperkalemic with ECG changes
Insulin 10u + D50 (K shifting)
Mannitol 0.5g/kg IV (osmotic diuresis, controversial)
ddx: AKI from other causes, Myositis, DVT, Compartment syndrome, DKA, Malignant hyperthermia, NMS
labs:
CK (>5x normal, often >10,000; severe >100,000)
BMP (hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia early then hypercalcemia, AKI)
Urinalysis (dipstick positive for blood but no RBCs = myoglobin)
Urine myoglobin
LDH, uric acid
Coags (DIC risk)
Lactate
ABG/VBG (metabolic acidosis)
notes:
CK peaks at 24-72h, may take 5+ days to normalize
Common causes: trauma/crush, prolonged immobilization, seizures, drugs (statins, cocaine, MDMA), extreme exertion, heat stroke, NMS
AKI risk increases with CK >15,000-20,000
Hypocalcemia: calcium deposits in damaged muscle - avoid IV calcium unless symptomatic or hyperkalemic with ECG changes
Late hypercalcemia: as calcium mobilizes from healing muscle
Compartment syndrome: maintain high index of suspicion
description: Breakdown of damaged skeletal muscle, releasing its contents (myoglobin, potassium, CK) into the bloodstream, which can cause kidney failure and fatal arrhythmias.

name: Hypothyroidism
signs:
Fatigue
Weight gain
Cold intolerance
Constipation
Dry skin/hair
Bradycardia
Peripheral edema
Delayed reflexes
Depression
Menorrhagia
Hoarse voice
Myxedema
treatment:
1. Levothyroxine replacement
2. Start low in elderly/cardiac patients
3. Take on empty stomach, 30-60min before breakfast
4. Monitor TSH q6-8 weeks until stable, then annually
5. Adjust dose in pregnancy (increase 25-30%)
meds:
Levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg/day (full replacement)
Start 25-50mcg daily in elderly or cardiac
Increase by 25mcg q6-8wk to target TSH
Pregnancy: increase dose immediately by 25-30%
ddx: Depression, Anemia, CHF, CKD, Sleep apnea, Chronic fatigue syndrome
labs:
TSH (elevated in primary, low/normal in secondary)
Free T4 (low)
TPO antibodies (Hashimoto)
Lipid panel (often dyslipidemia)
CBC (anemia)
notes:
Hashimoto most common cause in developed world
Subclinical: elevated TSH, normal free T4 - treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic
Myxedema coma: severe hypothyroidism emergency
Drug interactions: calcium, iron, PPI reduce absorption - separate by 4h
Biotin supplements interfere with thyroid lab assays
description: Underactive thyroid gland producing insufficient thyroid hormone, slowing metabolism and causing fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and cognitive slowing.

name: Hyperthyroidism / Graves Disease
signs:
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Heat intolerance
Tremor
Tachycardia/palpitations
Anxiety/irritability
Diarrhea
Exophthalmos (Graves)
Pretibial myxedema (Graves)
Thyroid bruit
Onycholysis
Menstrual irregularity
treatment:
1. Antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred)
2. Beta-blocker for symptom control
3. Radioactive iodine ablation (definitive)
4. Surgery (thyroidectomy) for large goiter/compressive symptoms
5. Ophthalmology referral for Graves ophthalmopathy
meds:
Methimazole 10-30mg PO daily
PTU 100-200mg TID (first trimester pregnancy only)
Propranolol 20-40mg TID-QID
Atenolol 25-100mg daily
Radioactive iodine I-131 (definitive)
Prednisone for moderate-severe ophthalmopathy
ddx: Anxiety disorder, Pheochromocytoma, Thyroiditis (subacute, painless), Toxic multinodular goiter, Toxic adenoma, Factitious thyrotoxicosis
labs:
TSH (suppressed)
Free T4 and T3 (elevated)
TSI or TRAb (Graves specific)
Radioactive iodine uptake and scan
CBC (check before starting methimazole)
LFTs
notes:
Graves: diffuse uptake on RAIU scan; Toxic nodule: focal hot nodule
Methimazole risk: agranulocytosis (0.2-0.5%) - check WBC if fever/sore throat
PTU risk: hepatotoxicity - only use in first trimester or thyroid storm
Graves ophthalmopathy: may worsen with RAI - give prednisone prophylaxis if moderate
Subclinical hyperthyroidism: low TSH, normal T3/T4 - treat if TSH <0.1 or symptomatic
description: Overactive thyroid gland producing excess thyroid hormone, accelerating metabolism and causing weight loss, rapid heartbeat, tremor, and heat intolerance.

name: Hyponatremia
signs:
Often asymptomatic if mild/chronic
Nausea/vomiting
Headache
Confusion
Lethargy
Seizures (severe)
Coma (severe)
Respiratory arrest
Muscle cramps
Falls in elderly
treatment:
1. Classify: hypovolemic, euvolemic, hypervolemic
2. Assess severity and chronicity
3. Severe symptoms (seizures, coma): hypertonic saline 3% NaCl
4. Chronic: correct slowly (<=8 mEq/L per 24h)
5. SIADH: fluid restriction, salt tabs, possibly tolvaptan
6. Hypovolemic: NS resuscitation
7. Hypervolemic: fluid/sodium restriction, diuretics, treat underlying
meds:
3% NaCl 100-150mL IV bolus over 10min (repeat x2 if persistent severe symptoms)
NS for hypovolemic
Fluid restriction 1-1.5L/day (SIADH)
Tolvaptan 15mg PO daily (SIADH, not liver disease)
Furosemide + salt tabs (SIADH refractory to fluid restriction)
Demeclocycline 300-600mg BID (SIADH, rarely used)
ddx:
Pseudohyponatremia (hyperlipidemia, hyperproteinemia)
Hypovolemic: diuretics, vomiting, diarrhea, adrenal insufficiency
Euvolemic: SIADH, hypothyroidism, cortisol deficiency
Hypervolemic: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, CKD
labs:
Serum Na, serum osmolality
Urine osmolality, urine Na
BMP
TSH, cortisol (rule out hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency)
LFTs, albumin
Uric acid (low in SIADH)
CXR, CT head (SIADH causes)
notes:
Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/CPM): risk if correcting >8-10 mEq/L in 24h in chronic hyponatremia
High risk for ODS: Na <105, alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease, hypokalemia
SIADH criteria: euvolemic, low serum osm, inappropriately concentrated urine (>100), urine Na >40, normal thyroid/adrenal
Correction rate: <=8 mEq/L per 24h for chronic; can be faster for acute (<48h)
If overcorrected: D5W +/- DDAVP to lower sodium back down
description: Abnormally low sodium concentration in the blood (<135 mEq/L), which can cause brain swelling. Must be corrected slowly to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.

name: Acute Coronary Syndrome - Unstable Angina
signs:
Chest pain at rest or crescendo pattern
New-onset angina with minimal exertion
Similar presentation to NSTEMI
No troponin elevation
ECG may show ST depression, T-wave inversion, or be normal
Dyspnea
Diaphoresis
treatment:
Same as NSTEMI approach:
1. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor)
2. Anticoagulation
3. Risk stratification (TIMI, GRACE)
4. Stress test or coronary angiography based on risk
5. Beta-blocker, statin, nitrates
6. Medical optimization
meds:
Aspirin 325mg then 81mg daily
Clopidogrel 300-600mg load then 75mg daily or Ticagrelor 180mg then 90mg BID
Heparin or enoxaparin
Metoprolol 25-50mg PO BID
Atorvastatin 80mg daily
Nitroglycerin SL or IV
ddx: NSTEMI, Stable angina, GERD, Costochondritis, PE, Anxiety, Aortic dissection
labs:
Serial troponin (negative by definition for UA)
12-lead ECG (serial)
CBC, BMP, lipids
BNP
CXR
Stress test or coronary angiography
notes:
Unstable angina + NSTEMI = NSTE-ACS
With high-sensitivity troponin, true unstable angina is less commonly diagnosed
TIMI risk score guides invasive vs conservative strategy
Difference from NSTEMI: no troponin elevation
High-risk features: recurrent pain, ST changes, hemodynamic instability, high TIMI/GRACE score
description: Chest pain from reduced coronary blood flow at rest or with minimal exertion, without heart muscle damage (normal troponin). A warning sign of impending heart attack.

name: Mesenteric Ischemia
signs:
Severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam
Nausea/vomiting
Diarrhea (may be bloody)
Abdominal distension
Bowel sounds initially hyperactive then absent
Peritonitis (late)
Shock (late)
Lactic acidosis
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
2. Broad-spectrum antibiotics
3. Anticoagulation with heparin
4. Emergent CTA
5. Surgical embolectomy or revascularization
6. Endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis for selected cases
7. Bowel resection if nonviable bowel
8. Second-look laparotomy in 24-48h
meds:
Heparin 80u/kg bolus then 18u/kg/h
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
NS/LR aggressive resuscitation
Vasodilators (papaverine) intra-arterial in some cases
Avoid vasopressors if possible (worsen ischemia)
ddx: SBO, Pancreatitis, AAA rupture, Volvulus, Diverticulitis, Perforated viscus, MI, Aortic dissection
labs:
Lactate (elevated, often >2)
CTA abdomen (filling defect in SMA/SMV, bowel wall changes)
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP (metabolic acidosis)
LDH (elevated)
D-dimer
ABG (metabolic acidosis)
Amylase/lipase
notes:
Pain out of proportion to exam is classic and should raise suspicion
Acute arterial (SMA embolism most common, often AFib)
Acute venous (MVT: hypercoagulable states)
Non-occlusive (low flow: shock, vasopressors, cardiac failure)
Chronic: postprandial pain, food fear, weight loss (intestinal angina)
Mortality 60-80% if bowel necrosis develops
AFib is most common source of embolism to SMA
description: Reduced blood flow to the intestines, causing bowel ischemia and potentially necrosis. Classically presents with severe abdominal pain out of proportion to physical exam findings.

name: Toxic Megacolon
signs:
Abdominal distension
Abdominal pain/tenderness
Fever >38.6C
Tachycardia >120
Leukocytosis >10.5k
Anemia
Dehydration
Altered mental status
Decreased bowel sounds
Rebound/guarding (perforation)
treatment:
1. NPO, NGT decompression
2. IV fluids and electrolyte correction
3. IV antibiotics (broad spectrum)
4. Stop anti-motility agents (opioids, anticholinergics, loperamide)
5. Serial abdominal X-rays q12h
6. Knee-chest position, rolling q2h (decompressive)
7. Surgical consultation: colectomy if no improvement in 24-72h or perforation
meds:
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h or Meropenem 1g IV q8h
Vancomycin 500mg PO/PR QID (if C. diff)
Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h (if C. diff)
IV fluids aggressively
Blood transfusion if anemic
Avoid opioids, anticholinergics
ddx: SBO, Ogilvie syndrome (colonic pseudo-obstruction), Volvulus, Ischemic colitis, Perforated viscus
labs:
Abdominal X-ray (transverse colon >6cm or cecum >9cm)
CBC (leukocytosis, anemia)
BMP
Lactate
Blood cultures
C. diff testing
CT abdomen (if perforation suspected)
Serial KUB
notes:
Definition: radiographic colon dilation + systemic toxicity
Most common causes: IBD (especially UC), C. diff, ischemic colitis
Perforation risk increases with colon diameter and duration
Indicators for surgery: perforation, uncontrolled hemorrhage, progressive dilation despite 48-72h medical therapy
Avoid colonoscopy and barium enema in acute toxic megacolon
Mortality: 20% without perforation, 40%+ with perforation
description: A life-threatening complication where the colon becomes severely dilated (>6cm) with systemic toxicity, risking perforation. Most commonly from IBD or C. difficile.

name: Pulmonary Hypertension
signs:
Progressive dyspnea on exertion
Fatigue
Chest pain
Syncope/presyncope
Peripheral edema
JVD
Loud P2 (pulmonic component of S2)
RV heave
Tricuspid regurgitation murmur
Hepatomegaly
Ascites
treatment:
1. Classify WHO Group (1-5) - treatment differs
2. Group 1 (PAH): pulmonary vasodilators
3. Group 2 (left heart): treat underlying cardiac disease
4. Group 3 (lung disease): treat underlying lung disease, O2
5. Group 4 (CTEPH): pulmonary endarterectomy, riociguat
6. Diuretics for fluid overload
7. Anticoagulation (especially Group 1, Group 4)
8. Supplemental O2
9. Lung transplant for refractory
meds:
Sildenafil 20mg TID or Tadalafil 40mg daily (PDE5i)
Ambrisentan 5-10mg daily (ERA)
Bosentan 62.5-125mg BID (ERA)
Epoprostenol IV continuous (prostacyclin - severe)
Treprostinil SC/IV/inhaled
Riociguat 1-2.5mg TID (sGC stimulator, Group 4)
Furosemide for fluid management
Warfarin (selected patients)
ddx: COPD, CHF, PE (chronic thromboembolic), Interstitial lung disease, Sleep apnea, Valvular heart disease, Connective tissue disease
labs:
Echo (RVSP estimate, RV dilation/dysfunction)
Right heart catheterization (gold standard: mPAP >=20mmHg)
PFTs and DLCO
V/Q scan (rule out CTEPH)
CT chest
6-minute walk test
BNP/NT-proBNP
ANA, HIV, LFTs (connective tissue disease, portopulmonary)
Sleep study if suspected OSA
HRCT
notes:
WHO Groups: 1-PAH, 2-Left heart, 3-Lung disease, 4-CTEPH, 5-Multifactorial
Right heart cath defines PH: mPAP >=20mmHg
Pre-capillary: PAWP <=15; Post-capillary: PAWP >15
Vasoreactivity testing during RHC (if Group 1): CCB if positive
Avoid pregnancy (30-50% mortality)
Epoprostenol: never abruptly discontinue (rebound PH crisis)
Prognosis varies widely by group and functional class
description: Elevated blood pressure in the pulmonary arteries (mean >20mmHg), forcing the right side of the heart to work harder and eventually leading to right heart failure.

name: Acute Pericardial Effusion
signs:
Dyspnea (most common)
Chest pain/pressure
Muffled heart sounds
Tachycardia
Friction rub (may disappear as fluid accumulates)
JVD
Ewart sign (dullness below left scapula)
Pulsus paradoxus
Hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Echo-guided pericardiocentesis if hemodynamic compromise
2. NSAIDs + colchicine if inflammatory
3. Drain if large/symptomatic or concern for bacterial/TB
4. Pericardial window for recurrent
5. Treat underlying cause
6. Send pericardial fluid for analysis
meds:
Ibuprofen 600mg TID + Colchicine 0.5mg BID (if inflammatory)
Pericardiocentesis for drainage
Intrapericardial triamcinolone (prevent recurrence)
Antibiotics if purulent
Anti-TB therapy if tuberculous
ddx: Cardiac tamponade, CHF, Constrictive pericarditis, Pleural effusion, MI, PE
labs:
Echo (effusion size, tamponade physiology)
ECG (low voltage, electrical alternans)
CXR (enlarged cardiac silhouette if >250mL)
Pericardial fluid: cell count, protein, LDH, glucose, cytology, culture, ADA, PCR
Blood: CBC, BMP, ESR/CRP
TSH, ANA, HIV
TB testing
notes:
Rate of accumulation matters more than size
Rapid: 150-200mL can cause tamponade
Slow: 1-2L can accumulate without tamponade
Causes: viral/idiopathic, malignancy, uremia, bacterial, TB, autoimmune, post-MI (Dressler), post-cardiac surgery, hypothyroidism
ADA >40 suggests TB in high-prevalence areas
Hemorrhagic: malignancy, TB, uremia, post-procedure
description: Abnormal fluid accumulation in the pericardial sac surrounding the heart. Can be harmless if small and slow, or fatal if rapid (tamponade).

name: Ventricular Tachycardia
signs:
Palpitations
Dizziness/lightheadedness
Syncope
Chest pain
Dyspnea
Hypotension
Wide complex tachycardia on ECG
AV dissociation
Cannon A waves in JVP
Cardiac arrest (pulseless VT)
treatment:
1. Pulseless VT: defibrillation per ACLS protocol
2. Unstable with pulse: synchronized cardioversion
3. Stable monomorphic VT: IV amiodarone or procainamide
4. Polymorphic VT (normal QT): treat as VF - defibrillation + amiodarone
5. Torsades de Pointes: IV magnesium, isoproterenol, overdrive pacing
6. Correct electrolytes (K, Mg)
7. ICD evaluation for secondary prevention
meds:
Amiodarone 150mg IV over 10min then 1mg/min x6h then 0.5mg/min
Procainamide 20-50mg/min until arrhythmia suppressed (max 17mg/kg)
Lidocaine 1-1.5mg/kg IV bolus then 1-4mg/min
Magnesium 2g IV over 5-20min (Torsades)
Isoproterenol 2-10mcg/min (Torsades)
Metoprolol if ischemia-related
ddx: SVT with aberrancy, Pre-excited tachycardia (WPW), Hyperkalemia, Artifact, Ventricular paced rhythm
labs:
12-lead ECG (wide QRS >120ms, rate >100)
BMP (K, Mg, Ca)
Troponin
Magnesium level
Drug levels (digoxin, QT-prolonging meds)
Echo (structural heart disease)
Continuous telemetry
Electrophysiology study (EP study)
notes:
Wide complex tachycardia: treat as VT until proven otherwise
Brugada criteria for differentiating VT from SVT with aberrancy
AV dissociation, fusion beats, capture beats = VT
Common causes: ischemia/MI, structural heart disease, electrolyte abnormalities, drugs
Torsades: polymorphic VT + prolonged QT interval
QT-prolonging drugs: antiarrhythmics, antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones), antipsychotics, methadone
ICD indicated for survivors of VT/VF arrest (secondary prevention)
description: A dangerously fast heart rhythm originating in the ventricles (>100 bpm with wide QRS). Can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest.

name: Aortic Stenosis
signs:
Exertional dyspnea
Angina
Syncope/presyncope
Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur (RUSB, radiates to carotids)
Diminished and delayed carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus)
Narrow pulse pressure
S4 gallop
Heart failure (late)
treatment:
1. Symptomatic severe AS: valve replacement (TAVR or SAVR)
2. Asymptomatic severe: monitor, exercise testing, intervene if EF drops
3. Medical management limited (does not change natural history)
4. Avoid excessive preload/afterload reduction
5. Balloon valvuloplasty as bridge to definitive therapy
6. TAVR preferred in intermediate-high surgical risk
meds:
Limited medical therapy:
Cautious diuretics for fluid overload
Avoid vasodilators (nitrates, ACEi) in severe AS - can cause syncope
Statins do NOT slow progression
Beta-blockers cautiously if needed
IE prophylaxis for prosthetic valves
ddx: HOCM, Subaortic membrane, Mitral regurgitation, Aortic sclerosis (no gradient), VSD
labs:
Echo (valve area, mean gradient, peak velocity)
Severe: AVA <1.0cm2, mean gradient >40mmHg, peak velocity >4m/s
ECG (LVH, LAE)
CXR (calcified aortic valve, cardiomegaly)
CT calcium scoring
Cardiac catheterization (if discrepancy)
BNP
notes:
Classic triad: angina, syncope, heart failure
Once symptomatic, prognosis poor without intervention:
Angina: 5yr survival; Syncope: 3yr; Heart failure: 2yr
Bicuspid aortic valve: most common cause in <70; Degenerative calcific in >70
Low-flow low-gradient AS: dobutamine stress echo to differentiate true-severe from pseudo-severe
TAVR: transcatheter aortic valve replacement - preferred route for most patients now
description: Narrowing of the aortic valve opening, obstructing blood flow from the heart to the body. Usually from age-related calcification or a congenital bicuspid valve.

name: Nephrotic Syndrome
signs:
Severe peripheral edema (pitting)
Periorbital edema
Anasarca
Foamy urine (proteinuria)
Weight gain
Fatigue
Dyspnea (pleural effusions)
Ascites
Hyperlipidemia
Infection susceptibility
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause
2. ACEi or ARB (reduce proteinuria)
3. Sodium restriction <2g/day
4. Diuretics for edema (loop +/- thiazide)
5. Statin for hyperlipidemia
6. Anticoagulation if high VTE risk (especially membranous)
7. Immunosuppression based on biopsy results
8. Pneumococcal vaccination
meds:
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily or Losartan 50-100mg daily
Furosemide 40-80mg PO BID + Metolazone 2.5-5mg (if resistant)
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Albumin 25% IV + furosemide (severe edema with low albumin)
Prednisone 1mg/kg/day (minimal change disease)
Cyclophosphamide, rituximab, tacrolimus (based on histology)
ddx: Nephritic syndrome, CHF, Cirrhosis, Protein-losing enteropathy, Malnutrition, Myxedema
labs:
Urinalysis (3-4+ protein, lipiduria, oval fat bodies)
24h urine protein >3.5g/day (or spot protein/Cr >3.5)
Serum albumin (<3g/dL)
Lipid panel (elevated)
BMP (renal function)
Complement levels (C3, C4)
ANA, anti-PLA2R (membranous), Hep B/C, HIV
Renal biopsy (definitive for classification)
notes:
Nephrotic vs Nephritic: Nephrotic = massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia; Nephritic = hematuria, HTN, mild proteinuria, RBC casts
Most common in adults: Membranous nephropathy, FSGS, Minimal change disease
Most common in children: Minimal change disease
VTE risk: loss of antithrombin III, especially membranous
SBP risk: loss of immunoglobulins
Anti-PLA2R positive = primary membranous
description: A kidney disorder causing massive protein loss in the urine (>3.5g/day), leading to severe swelling, low blood albumin, high cholesterol, and increased clotting risk.

name: Urolithiasis / Renal Colic
signs:
Severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin
Hematuria (gross or microscopic)
Nausea/vomiting
Restlessness (cannot find comfortable position)
CVA tenderness
Urinary frequency/urgency
Dysuria
Testicular/labial pain (referred)
treatment:
1. Pain management (NSAIDs first-line + opioids if needed)
2. Hydration
3. Alpha-blocker for medical expulsive therapy (stones 5-10mm)
4. <5mm: likely to pass spontaneously (>90%)
5. 5-10mm: medical expulsive therapy, may need intervention
6. >10mm or failed passage: urologic intervention (SWL, ureteroscopy, PCNL)
7. Emergent: infected obstructed stone = urology STAT
meds:
Ketorolac 30mg IV/15mg IM (first-line analgesic)
Ibuprofen 600mg PO q8h
Morphine 0.1mg/kg IV or Hydromorphone 0.5-1mg IV PRN
Tamsulosin 0.4mg PO daily (medical expulsive therapy)
Nifedipine 30mg PO daily (alternative MET)
Ondansetron 4mg IV PRN
ddx: Pyelonephritis, Appendicitis, Ovarian torsion, Ectopic pregnancy, AAA, Musculoskeletal back pain, Renal infarction
labs:
Urinalysis (hematuria in 85%, r/o infection)
Urine culture
BMP (creatinine, calcium)
CBC (leukocytosis if infected)
CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (gold standard)
US (first-line in pregnancy, children)
Strain urine for stone analysis
24h urine collection (recurrent stone formers)
notes:
Stone composition: Calcium oxalate (80%), Uric acid (10%), Struvite (5-10%), Cystine (1%)
Infected obstructed stone = EMERGENCY - sepsis risk, needs emergent decompression (stent or nephrostomy)
Uric acid stones: radiolucent on X-ray, seen on CT
Struvite: staghorn calculi, associated with urease-producing organisms (Proteus)
Prevention: hydration (>2.5L/day), dietary modification based on stone type
Recurrence rate ~50% within 5-10 years without prevention
description: Kidney stones — hard mineral deposits that form in the kidneys and cause severe colicky pain when passing through the urinary tract.

name: Spinal Cord Compression
signs:
Back pain (often first symptom)
Weakness/paralysis below level of compression
Sensory level
Bowel/bladder dysfunction (urinary retention, incontinence)
Saddle anesthesia (cauda equina)
Hyperreflexia below lesion (UMN) or hyporeflexia (LMN/cauda equina)
Babinski sign positive
treatment:
1. MRI whole spine STAT
2. High-dose dexamethasone if malignant compression
3. Neurosurgical consultation
4. Radiation oncology consultation for malignant
5. Surgical decompression (emergency if progressive deficit)
6. Foley catheter if urinary retention
7. DVT prophylaxis
8. Treat underlying cause
meds:
Dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg IV q6h (malignant compression)
Pain management: scheduled acetaminophen + opioids PRN
Gabapentin 300mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (DVT prophylaxis after surgery)
ddx: Transverse myelitis, Epidural abscess, Epidural hematoma, Disc herniation, GBS, Multiple sclerosis, Spinal infarction
labs:
MRI whole spine with contrast (gold standard)
Plain X-rays (may show lytic/blastic lesions, loss of pedicle)
CT myelography if MRI contraindicated
CBC, BMP, ESR/CRP
PSA, SPEP (if unknown primary)
Post-void residual (urinary retention)
Blood cultures if abscess suspected
notes:
Oncologic emergency: malignant cord compression, typically metastatic (lung, breast, prostate, renal, myeloma)
Cauda equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, bilateral leg weakness/pain - surgical emergency
Surgical decompression within 24-48h gives best outcomes
If cannot get MRI within 4h and high suspicion, start dexamethasone empirically
Epidural abscess: fever + back pain + neuro deficit = MRI STAT (S. aureus most common)
description: Pressure on the spinal cord from tumor, abscess, hematoma, or disc herniation that can cause permanent paralysis and loss of bladder/bowel function if not urgently treated.

name: Diabetic Emergencies - Hypoglycemia
signs:
Tremor
Diaphoresis
Palpitations
Anxiety
Hunger
Confusion
Slurred speech
Seizures
Loss of consciousness
Focal neurologic deficits (may mimic stroke)
Coma
treatment:
1. Conscious patient: oral glucose (15-20g fast-acting carb)
2. Unconscious/unable to swallow: IV dextrose or IM glucagon
3. Recheck glucose q15min
4. Once glucose >70, give complex carb/meal
5. Identify and treat cause
6. Adjust diabetes medications
7. Observe: sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia may recur (long half-life)
meds:
D50 (50% dextrose) 25-50mL IV (12.5-25g glucose)
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC/IN (if no IV access)
Oral glucose tablets 15-20g
D10 infusion for sustained hypoglycemia
Octreotide 50mcg SC q6-8h (sulfonylurea-induced, refractory)
ddx: Stroke, Seizure disorder, Syncope, Intoxication, Sepsis, Adrenal insufficiency, Insulinoma
labs:
Point-of-care glucose STAT
BMP
Insulin level, C-peptide, proinsulin (if cause unclear)
Sulfonylurea screen
Cortisol
LFTs
Beta-hydroxybutyrate
notes:
Whipple triad: symptoms of hypoglycemia + low glucose + resolution with glucose correction
Sulfonylureas: long-acting, may cause prolonged hypoglycemia - observe 24h, octreotide helpful
Hypoglycemia unawareness: recurrent hypoglycemia blunts counter-regulatory response
Non-diabetic hypoglycemia workup: insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, sulfonylurea screen during episode
High C-peptide + high insulin = endogenous (insulinoma, sulfonylurea)
Low C-peptide + high insulin = exogenous insulin
description: Dangerously low blood sugar (glucose <70 mg/dL), most often caused by diabetes medications. Can cause seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain damage if prolonged.

name: Pneumocystis Pneumonia (PCP)
signs:
Progressive dyspnea over days-weeks
Dry nonproductive cough
Fever
Tachypnea
Hypoxemia (often out of proportion to exam)
Crackles (may be subtle)
Weight loss
Malaise
treatment:
1. TMP-SMX (first-line)
2. Adjunctive corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >35
3. Supplemental oxygen
4. Start ART if HIV-positive (may wait 2 weeks to reduce IRIS risk)
5. Duration: 21 days for HIV, 14 days for non-HIV
6. Secondary prophylaxis until CD4 >200 for 3+ months
meds:
TMP-SMX 15-20mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided q6-8h x21d
Prednisone 40mg BID x5d, then 40mg daily x5d, then 20mg daily x11d (if PaO2 <70)
Alternatives: Pentamidine 4mg/kg IV daily, Atovaquone 750mg PO BID, Clindamycin 600mg IV q6h + Primaquine 30mg PO daily
Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS 1 tab daily
ddx: Community-acquired pneumonia, TB, CMV pneumonitis, Lymphocytic interstitial pneumonitis, Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma, Fungal pneumonia
labs:
CXR (bilateral diffuse GGO/interstitial infiltrates)
CT chest (GGOs, crazy paving)
LDH (often markedly elevated)
ABG (hypoxemia, elevated A-a gradient)
Beta-D-glucan (elevated, sensitive)
Induced sputum or BAL for PCP PCR/DFA
CD4 count
HIV viral load
notes:
CD4 <200 = risk for PCP (most common AIDS-defining illness)
Can also occur in non-HIV immunosuppression (transplant, chronic steroids, chemotherapy)
LDH elevation correlates with disease severity
Beta-D-glucan: >80pg/mL supports diagnosis (also elevated in other fungal infections)
CXR may be normal in early disease
Pneumothorax is a known complication
Spontaneous pneumothorax in HIV patient = think PCP
description: A serious fungal lung infection caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii, occurring almost exclusively in immunocompromised patients, especially those with HIV and CD4 <200.

name: Tuberculosis
signs:
Chronic cough >2-3 weeks
Hemoptysis
Night sweats
Weight loss
Fever (low grade)
Fatigue
Anorexia
Lymph node enlargement
Apical lung findings (crackles, decreased BS)
treatment:
1. Isolate (airborne precautions, negative pressure room)
2. Active TB: 4-drug regimen (RIPE) x2mo then INH+RIF x4mo
3. MDR-TB: individualized based on sensitivities
4. Latent TB: INH x9mo or Rifampin x4mo or INH+Rifapentine x12 weekly doses
5. DOT (directly observed therapy) recommended
6. Report to public health
meds:
Isoniazid (INH) 5mg/kg (max 300mg) daily
Rifampin 10mg/kg (max 600mg) daily
Pyrazinamide 25mg/kg daily
Ethambutol 15-20mg/kg daily
Pyridoxine (B6) 25-50mg daily (prevent INH neuropathy)
Latent: INH 300mg daily x9mo + B6
ddx: Lung cancer, Pneumonia, MAC, Fungal infection (histo, cocci, blasto), Sarcoidosis, NTM
labs:
CXR (upper lobe cavitary lesion, infiltrate, effusion, miliary pattern)
Sputum AFB smear x3 (early morning)
Sputum mycobacterial culture (gold standard, takes weeks)
Sputum GeneXpert MTB/RIF (rapid PCR, 2 hours)
QuantiFERON-TB Gold or PPD (latent TB screening)
CBC, CMP, HIV test
LFTs (baseline before treatment)
notes:
Airborne transmission - N95 respirator required
Cavitary disease: most infectious
Miliary TB: hematogenous spread, tiny nodules throughout lungs
INH side effects: hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy (give B6)
Rifampin: orange discoloration of fluids, CYP450 inducer (many drug interactions)
Ethambutol: optic neuritis (check visual acuity)
Pyrazinamide: hepatotoxicity, hyperuricemia
Treat latent TB before starting anti-TNF therapy
description: A chronic bacterial infection (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) primarily affecting the lungs, spread through airborne droplets. Can remain latent for years before becoming active.

name: Mitral Regurgitation
signs:
Holosystolic murmur at apex
Radiates to axilla
Dyspnea on exertion
Orthopnea
Fatigue
Palpitations
S3 gallop
Laterally displaced PMI
treatment:
1. Medical management for chronic compensated MR
2. Surgery (repair preferred over replacement) for severe symptomatic MR
3. Surgery if asymptomatic with EF 30-60% or LVESD >=40mm
4. MitraClip for high surgical risk patients
5. Afterload reduction and diuretics
meds:
ACE inhibitors (lisinopril 5-40mg daily)
Diuretics (furosemide 20-80mg daily)
Beta-blockers if AFib
Anticoagulation if AFib (apixaban 5mg BID)
Hydralazine if ACEi intolerant
ddx: Aortic stenosis, VSD, Tricuspid regurgitation, Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, MVP
labs:
TTE with Doppler (severity grading, EF, LA size)
TEE (detailed valve anatomy pre-surgery)
ECG (LAE, AFib, LVH)
CXR (cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion)
BNP
Cardiac MRI if echo inconclusive
notes:
Primary (degenerative, endocarditis, rheumatic) vs Secondary (ischemic, dilated CM)
Severe MR: regurgitant volume >=60mL, EROA >=0.4cm2, vena contracta >=0.7cm
Acute MR (chordal rupture, papillary muscle rupture): surgical emergency
Repair has better outcomes than replacement - refer to experienced center
description: A condition where the mitral valve doesn't close tightly, allowing blood to flow backward into the left atrium during each heartbeat.

name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
signs:
Dyspnea on exertion
Orthopnea
Fatigue
Peripheral edema
JVD
S3 gallop
Laterally displaced PMI
Mitral/tricuspid regurgitation murmurs
Hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Standard GDMT for HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i
2. Diuretics for volume overload
3. ICD if EF <=35% despite 3 months GDMT
4. CRT if LBBB + QRS >=150ms + EF <=35%
5. Treat underlying cause if identified
6. LVAD or transplant evaluation for end-stage
meds:
Sacubitril/valsartan 24/26mg BID titrate to 97/103mg BID
Carvedilol 3.125mg BID titrate to 25mg BID
Spironolactone 25mg daily
Dapagliflozin 10mg daily
Furosemide 20-160mg daily
Ivabradine 5-7.5mg BID if HR >70
ddx: Ischemic cardiomyopathy, Myocarditis, Valvular heart disease, Hypertensive cardiomyopathy, Peripartum cardiomyopathy, Alcoholic cardiomyopathy
labs:
TTE (dilated LV, reduced EF, global hypokinesis)
BNP/NT-proBNP
CBC, BMP, LFTs, TSH
Iron studies (hemochromatosis)
HIV, hepatitis panel
Cardiac MRI (fibrosis pattern)
Genetic testing if familial
notes:
Most common cause of heart transplantation
Idiopathic in ~50%, familial ~25%, alcohol, chemotherapy (doxorubicin), viral
Reversible causes: tachycardia-mediated, alcohol, thyroid, peripartum
Genetic counseling and screening of first-degree relatives recommended
description: A disease of the heart muscle where the left ventricle becomes enlarged and weakened, reducing the heart's ability to pump blood effectively.

name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
signs:
Systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva/standing
Dyspnea on exertion
Syncope or presyncope with exertion
Chest pain
Palpitations
Bisferiens pulse
S4 gallop
Double apical impulse
treatment:
1. Avoid dehydration, strenuous exercise, and Valsalva
2. Beta-blockers first-line for symptoms
3. Verapamil or diltiazem if BB intolerant
4. Disopyramide for refractory obstruction
5. Septal myectomy (surgical) or alcohol septal ablation
6. ICD for high-risk SCD patients
7. Mavacamten for obstructive HCM
meds:
Metoprolol 50-200mg daily
Verapamil 120-480mg daily (avoid with BB)
Disopyramide 100-200mg TID
Mavacamten 5-15mg daily
Avoid: digoxin, diuretics, vasodilators, ACEi in obstructive HCM
ddx: Athlete's heart, Hypertensive LVH, Aortic stenosis, Fabry disease, Amyloidosis, Friedreich ataxia
labs:
TTE (asymmetric septal hypertrophy >=15mm, SAM of mitral valve, LVOT gradient)
ECG (LVH, deep Q waves, T wave inversions)
Cardiac MRI (late gadolinium enhancement)
Holter monitor (arrhythmias)
Genetic testing (sarcomere mutations)
Exercise stress test
notes:
Most common cause of SCD in young athletes
Autosomal dominant - screen first-degree relatives
SCD risk factors: family hx SCD, syncope, NSVT, massive LVH >=30mm, abnormal BP response to exercise
Avoid competitive sports if diagnosed
SAM = systolic anterior motion of mitral valve
description: A genetic condition causing abnormal thickening of the heart muscle, which can obstruct blood flow and increase the risk of sudden cardiac death, especially in young athletes.

name: Supraventricular Tachycardia
signs:
Sudden onset palpitations
Rapid regular heart rate (150-250 bpm)
Dizziness
Chest tightness
Dyspnea
Anxiety
Neck pulsations
Polyuria (ANP release)
Abrupt termination
treatment:
1. Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage, ice water to face)
2. Adenosine 6mg rapid IV push, then 12mg if no response
3. IV diltiazem or verapamil if adenosine fails
4. Synchronized cardioversion if hemodynamically unstable
5. Catheter ablation for recurrent SVT (curative)
meds:
Adenosine 6mg IV rapid push, repeat 12mg x2
Diltiazem 0.25mg/kg IV over 2min
Verapamil 5-10mg IV over 2min
Metoprolol 5mg IV q5min x3
Flecainide 50-150mg PO BID (prevention, no structural HD)
Diltiazem 120-360mg PO daily (prevention)
ddx: Sinus tachycardia, Atrial flutter, Atrial fibrillation, VT, Anxiety/panic attack
labs:
12-lead ECG during episode (narrow QRS, regular, P waves absent or retrograde)
ECG after conversion (look for delta wave = WPW, short PR)
BMP
TSH
Holter or event monitor
EP study if recurrent
notes:
AVNRT (60%): most common type, dual AV nodal pathways
AVRT (30%): accessory pathway (WPW if manifest)
Atrial tachycardia (10%): P waves visible, may be automatic
AVOID AV nodal blockers in WPW with AFib (can cause VF)
Pill-in-pocket: flecainide 200-300mg PO for infrequent episodes
description: A rapid heart rhythm originating above the ventricles, causing episodes of sudden fast heartbeat that usually start and stop abruptly.

name: Complete Heart Block
signs:
Bradycardia (20-40 bpm)
Syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks)
Dizziness
Fatigue
Dyspnea
Hypotension
Cannon A waves in JVP
Variable intensity S1
treatment:
1. Identify and treat reversible causes (MI, drugs, infection)
2. Atropine 0.5-1mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia
3. Transcutaneous pacing if unstable
4. Transvenous temporary pacing as bridge
5. Permanent pacemaker implantation (definitive)
6. Isoproterenol infusion as temporizing measure
meds:
Atropine 0.5-1mg IV q3-5min (max 3mg) - often ineffective
Isoproterenol 2-10mcg/min IV
Dopamine 5-20mcg/kg/min IV
Epinephrine 2-10mcg/min IV
Glucocorticoids if inflammatory etiology
ddx: Second-degree AV block (Mobitz II), High vagal tone, Drug effect (BB, CCB, digoxin), Inferior MI, Lyme disease, Infiltrative disease
labs:
12-lead ECG (P waves and QRS complexes independent, AV dissociation)
Continuous telemetry
BMP (electrolytes)
TSH
Lyme titers if endemic area
Troponin (MI-related)
Digoxin level if applicable
notes:
No relationship between P waves and QRS complexes
Escape rhythm: junctional 40-60bpm (narrow QRS), ventricular 20-40bpm (wide QRS)
Reversible causes: inferior MI (usually temporary), Lyme disease, drugs
All symptomatic complete heart block needs permanent pacemaker
Congenital CHB: may be well-tolerated in young patients
description: A condition where electrical signals from the heart's upper chambers completely fail to reach the lower chambers, resulting in a dangerously slow heart rate.

name: Myocarditis
signs:
Chest pain
Dyspnea
Fever
Fatigue
Palpitations
Recent viral illness
New heart failure symptoms
Arrhythmias
Cardiogenic shock (fulminant)
treatment:
1. Supportive care and hemodynamic support
2. Standard HF therapy if reduced EF (ACEi, BB, diuretics)
3. Restrict activity for 3-6 months
4. Immunosuppression for giant cell myocarditis or autoimmune
5. Mechanical support (IABP, ECMO) for fulminant cases
6. Transplant evaluation if no recovery
meds:
Lisinopril 2.5-20mg daily
Carvedilol 3.125-25mg BID
Furosemide 20-80mg IV/PO as needed
Milrinone 0.375-0.75mcg/kg/min (cardiogenic shock)
IVIG (debated, may help in viral)
Prednisone + azathioprine (giant cell myocarditis)
ddx: Acute MI, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, PE, Pericarditis, Dilated cardiomyopathy, Sepsis-related cardiomyopathy
labs:
Troponin elevated
BNP/NT-proBNP elevated
ECG (ST changes, arrhythmias, low voltage)
Echo (wall motion abnormalities, reduced EF)
Cardiac MRI (edema, late gadolinium enhancement - Lake Louise criteria)
Endomyocardial biopsy (gold standard but rarely done)
Viral serologies
notes:
Most common cause: viral (Coxsackie B, parvovirus B19, HHV-6, SARS-CoV-2)
Giant cell myocarditis: rapidly progressive, high mortality without immunosuppression
Fulminant myocarditis: paradoxically better prognosis if survives acute phase
No competitive sports for 3-6 months after diagnosis
Cardiac MRI is best noninvasive diagnostic tool
description: Inflammation of the heart muscle, usually triggered by a viral infection, which can weaken the heart and cause arrhythmias.

name: Peripheral Arterial Disease
signs:
Intermittent claudication (leg pain with walking)
Rest pain in advanced disease
Non-healing wounds or ulcers
Diminished or absent pulses
Hair loss on extremities
Pale/cool skin
ABI <0.9
treatment:
1. Smoking cessation (most important)
2. Supervised exercise program (30-45min, 3x/week)
3. Antiplatelet therapy
4. Statin therapy
5. Cilostazol for claudication symptoms
6. Revascularization (endovascular or surgical) for critical limb ischemia or lifestyle-limiting claudication
meds:
Aspirin 81mg daily or Clopidogrel 75mg daily
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Cilostazol 100mg BID (avoid in HF)
Ramipril 2.5-10mg daily
Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID + aspirin (COMPASS trial)
ddx: Lumbar spinal stenosis (neurogenic claudication), DVT, Chronic venous insufficiency, Peripheral neuropathy, Buerger disease
labs:
Ankle-brachial index (ABI <0.9 diagnostic)
Segmental pressures
Duplex ultrasound
CTA or MRA (pre-intervention)
Lipid panel, HbA1c, BMP
Toe pressures if calcified vessels (ABI falsely elevated in diabetes)
notes:
ABI: <0.9 = PAD, <0.4 = severe/critical
Fontaine classification: I asymptomatic, II claudication, III rest pain, IV tissue loss
PAD patients have high cardiovascular mortality - screen for CAD
Rutherford classification used for surgical planning
Critical limb ischemia: rest pain >2 weeks, ulcers, or gangrene
description: Narrowing of arteries in the legs due to atherosclerosis, reducing blood flow and causing pain with walking that can progress to tissue loss.

name: Stable Angina
signs:
Substernal chest pressure/tightness with exertion
Predictable onset with consistent activity level
Relieves with rest or nitroglycerin within 5 minutes
May radiate to arm, jaw, back
Dyspnea
No pain at rest
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modification: exercise, diet, smoking cessation
2. Sublingual nitroglycerin PRN for acute episodes
3. Anti-anginal therapy: beta-blocker first-line
4. Aspirin and statin for all patients
5. Stress testing for risk stratification
6. Coronary angiography if high-risk features
7. PCI or CABG for refractory symptoms or high-risk anatomy
meds:
Aspirin 81mg daily
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Metoprolol succinate 25-200mg daily
Amlodipine 5-10mg daily
Nitroglycerin 0.4mg SL PRN (up to 3 doses q5min)
Isosorbide mononitrate 30-120mg daily
Ranolazine 500-1000mg BID (add-on)
ddx: Unstable angina, GERD, Costochondritis, Esophageal spasm, Aortic stenosis, Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Anxiety
labs:
Resting ECG (may be normal)
Stress testing (exercise or pharmacologic)
Echo (LV function, wall motion)
Lipid panel
HbA1c
BMP
Coronary CTA or angiography
notes:
CCS classification: I - ordinary activity no symptoms, II - slight limitation, III - marked limitation, IV - any activity/rest
Beta-blockers reduce mortality post-MI
Nitrate-free interval of 10-14h to prevent tolerance
Modify risk factors: HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, smoking, obesity
ICHT trial: medical therapy noninferior to PCI for stable angina
description: Predictable chest discomfort caused by reduced blood flow to the heart during physical exertion or stress, due to narrowed coronary arteries.

name: Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome
signs:
Palpitations
Dizziness
Syncope
Dyspnea
Chest discomfort
Sudden cardiac death (rare)
Delta wave on resting ECG
Short PR interval
treatment:
1. Acute orthodromic AVRT: vagal maneuvers, adenosine
2. Acute antidromic AVRT: procainamide or ibutilide
3. WPW + AFib: procainamide or cardioversion (AVOID AV nodal blockers)
4. Catheter ablation of accessory pathway (curative, first-line for recurrent)
5. Observation for asymptomatic patients (risk stratification)
meds:
Adenosine 6-12mg IV (orthodromic AVRT only)
Procainamide 20-50mg/min IV
Ibutilide 1mg IV over 10min
Flecainide 200-300mg PO (pill-in-pocket)
AVOID: digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, beta-blockers in AFib+WPW
ddx: SVT (AVNRT), Atrial flutter, Ventricular tachycardia, LGL syndrome, Mahaim fiber tachycardia
labs:
12-lead ECG (short PR <120ms, delta wave, wide QRS)
Electrophysiology study (definitive, pre-ablation)
Echo (rule out structural heart disease - Ebstein anomaly)
Holter monitor
Exercise stress test (assess for intermittent pre-excitation)
notes:
Delta wave = slurred upstroke of QRS due to ventricular pre-excitation
AV nodal blockers in WPW+AFib can cause 1:1 conduction down accessory pathway → VF
Ebstein anomaly associated with right-sided accessory pathways
Intermittent pre-excitation on Holter suggests lower risk of SCD
Ablation success rate >95%
description: A congenital heart condition where an extra electrical pathway between the upper and lower chambers causes episodes of rapid heartbeat and characteristic ECG changes.

name: Mitral Valve Prolapse
signs:
Mid-systolic click
Late systolic murmur
Often asymptomatic
Palpitations
Atypical chest pain
Anxiety
Dyspnea
Click moves earlier with standing/Valsalva
treatment:
1. Reassurance for asymptomatic patients (benign prognosis)
2. Beta-blockers for symptomatic palpitations
3. Endocarditis prophylaxis NOT recommended (unless prior endocarditis)
4. Surgery if severe mitral regurgitation develops
5. Regular echo follow-up if moderate or greater MR
meds:
Metoprolol 25-50mg BID (palpitations)
Aspirin 81mg daily (if TIA/stroke history)
Anxiolytics if significant anxiety
Endocarditis prophylaxis NOT routinely indicated
ddx: Mitral regurgitation (other causes), Tricuspid valve prolapse, Atrial septal defect, Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Panic disorder
labs:
Echo (mitral leaflet displacement >=2mm above annular plane)
ECG (usually normal, may show T wave inversions in inferior leads)
Holter monitor if palpitations
Exercise stress test if exertional symptoms
notes:
Most common valvular abnormality (2-3% of population)
Barlow syndrome = myxomatous degeneration with redundant leaflets
Risk of progression to severe MR, endocarditis, arrhythmias
Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos associated
Click earlier with maneuvers that decrease LV volume (standing, Valsalva)
description: A condition where one or both flaps of the mitral valve bulge back into the left atrium during heartbeat, usually benign but can progress to significant valve leakage.

name: Pulmonary Fibrosis
signs:
Progressive dyspnea
Dry nonproductive cough
Bibasilar inspiratory crackles (Velcro-like)
Digital clubbing
Cyanosis (late)
Rapid shallow breathing
Weight loss
treatment:
1. Antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib) for IPF
2. Supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic
3. Pulmonary rehabilitation
4. Lung transplant evaluation for progressive disease
5. Treat underlying cause if not idiopathic
6. Avoid triggers/exposures
meds:
Pirfenidone 801mg TID with food
Nintedanib 150mg BID with food
Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >88%
Proton pump inhibitor (GERD common comorbidity)
Avoid immunosuppression in IPF (PANTHER trial showed harm)
ddx: COPD, CHF, Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, Sarcoidosis, Asbestosis, Drug-induced lung disease, Connective tissue disease-associated ILD
labs:
PFTs (restrictive: reduced FVC, reduced DLCO, normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio)
HRCT (UIP pattern: bibasilar honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, reticulation)
CXR (bilateral reticular opacities)
6-minute walk test
ABG
ANA, RF, anti-CCP (rule out CTD-ILD)
Surgical lung biopsy if HRCT atypical
notes:
IPF: most common idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, median survival 3-5 years
UIP pattern on HRCT: definite IPF diagnosis without biopsy (in right clinical context)
Acute exacerbation: rapid decline, bilateral GGO on CT, high mortality
Multidisciplinary discussion recommended for diagnosis
Pirfenidone and nintedanib slow FVC decline but don't cure
description: A chronic lung disease where the tissue deep in the lungs becomes thick, stiff, and scarred over time, making it progressively harder to breathe.

name: Sarcoidosis
signs:
Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
Dyspnea and cough
Erythema nodosum
Fever and fatigue
Arthralgia
Uveitis
Skin plaques/papules
Cranial nerve palsies
Hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Observation for mild disease (many spontaneously remit)
2. Systemic corticosteroids for significant organ involvement
3. Steroid-sparing agents for chronic disease
4. Topical steroids for skin/eye disease
5. Treat hypercalcemia if present
meds:
Prednisone 20-40mg daily x4-6 weeks then taper over 6-12 months
Methotrexate 10-15mg weekly (steroid-sparing)
Azathioprine 50-200mg daily
Hydroxychloroquine 200mg BID (skin/joint)
Infliximab 3-5mg/kg IV (refractory)
Mycophenolate 1-1.5g BID
ddx: Lymphoma, Tuberculosis, Berylliosis, Histoplasmosis, Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, HIV-associated lymphadenopathy
labs:
CXR (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy)
CT chest
PFTs (restrictive or obstructive pattern)
ACE level (elevated in 60% but nonspecific)
Calcium (hypercalcemia in 10%)
24hr urine calcium
Biopsy: noncaseating granulomas
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Ophthalmologic exam
notes:
Staging: 0-no involvement, I-bilateral hilar LAD, II-LAD+infiltrates, III-infiltrates only, IV-fibrosis
Stage I: 60-80% spontaneous remission
Lofgren syndrome: erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar LAD + arthritis + fever (excellent prognosis)
African Americans: more severe, chronic disease
Cardiac sarcoid: arrhythmias, heart block - needs cardiac MRI
description: An inflammatory disease that causes clusters of immune cells (granulomas) to form in various organs, most commonly the lungs and lymph nodes.

name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
signs:
Loud snoring
Witnessed apneas
Excessive daytime sleepiness
Morning headaches
Difficulty concentrating
Nocturia
Obesity
Large neck circumference (>17in men, >16in women)
Crowded oropharynx
treatment:
1. CPAP is first-line treatment
2. Weight loss (can be curative if mild)
3. Positional therapy (avoid supine sleeping)
4. Oral appliance (mandibular advancement device) for mild-moderate
5. Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) if CPAP intolerant
6. Hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire device)
7. Avoid sedatives and alcohol before sleep
meds:
CPAP titration per sleep study
No pharmacologic treatment is first-line
Modafinil 200mg daily (residual sleepiness despite CPAP)
Topical nasal steroids (nasal congestion)
Avoid: benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol at bedtime
ddx: Central sleep apnea, Narcolepsy, Hypothyroidism, Depression, Obesity hypoventilation syndrome, Periodic limb movement disorder
labs:
Polysomnography (gold standard) - AHI defines severity
Home sleep apnea test (HSAT) for uncomplicated cases
Epworth Sleepiness Scale
TSH (rule out hypothyroidism)
ABG if obesity hypoventilation suspected
CBC (polycythemia)
notes:
AHI: 5-15 mild, 15-30 moderate, >30 severe
Increased risk: HTN, AFib, stroke, MI, type 2 DM, MVA
CPAP compliance: goal >4hr/night on >70% of nights
Preoperative screening: STOP-BANG questionnaire
Refractory HTN: consider OSA as underlying cause
description: A sleep disorder where the throat muscles intermittently relax and block the airway during sleep, causing repeated breathing interruptions and poor sleep quality.

name: Lung Abscess
signs:
Productive cough (foul-smelling/putrid sputum)
Fever and night sweats
Weight loss
Pleuritic chest pain
Hemoptysis
Dental disease (aspiration risk)
Clubbing (chronic)
treatment:
1. Prolonged antibiotic therapy (6-8 weeks minimum)
2. Postural drainage
3. CT-guided percutaneous drainage if antibiotics fail
4. Bronchoscopy to rule out obstruction
5. Surgical resection for refractory cases
6. Dental evaluation and treatment
meds:
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h then 300mg PO QID
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Metronidazole 500mg IV/PO q8h (with penicillin)
Moxifloxacin 400mg daily (alternative)
ddx: Cavitary lung cancer, TB, Pulmonary infarction, Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Septic emboli, Fungal infection
labs:
CXR (cavity with air-fluid level)
CT chest (better characterization)
Sputum culture and gram stain
Blood cultures
CBC (leukocytosis, anemia)
Bronchoscopy (rule out obstructing lesion)
notes:
Most common cause: aspiration of oropharyngeal contents (anaerobes)
Risk factors: alcoholism, poor dentition, dysphagia, seizures, altered consciousness
Primary: aspiration-related, usually right lower lobe or posterior segments
Secondary: post-obstructive (tumor), hematogenous (endocarditis)
Most respond to antibiotics alone; surgery <10% of cases
description: A localized area of pus and dead tissue within the lung, usually caused by bacterial infection from aspiration, forming a cavity visible on imaging.

name: Bronchiectasis
signs:
Chronic productive cough (large volume purulent sputum)
Recurrent respiratory infections
Hemoptysis
Dyspnea
Crackles and rhonchi
Clubbing
Wheezing
treatment:
1. Airway clearance techniques (chest physiotherapy, PEP devices)
2. Inhaled hypertonic saline for mucus mobilization
3. Antibiotics for acute exacerbations
4. Long-term macrolide therapy if frequent exacerbations (>=3/year)
5. Treat underlying cause
6. Surgical resection for localized disease with recurrent infections
7. Pulmonary rehabilitation
meds:
Azithromycin 250mg daily or 500mg 3x/week (maintenance)
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125mg BID (exacerbation)
Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID (Pseudomonas)
Inhaled tobramycin 300mg BID (chronic Pseudomonas)
Hypertonic saline 7% nebulized BID
Albuterol 2.5mg nebulized PRN
ddx: COPD, Chronic bronchitis, Cystic fibrosis, Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, NTM infection, Primary ciliary dyskinesia
labs:
HRCT chest (dilated airways, signet ring sign, tram tracking)
Sputum culture and sensitivity
PFTs (obstructive pattern)
CBC, immunoglobulin levels
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level
Sweat chloride (CF)
Aspergillus IgE and IgG (ABPA)
Ciliary biopsy if PCD suspected
notes:
Vicious cycle: infection → inflammation → airway damage → impaired clearance → more infection
Common organisms: H. influenzae, Pseudomonas (poor prognosis), S. aureus, NTM
Causes: post-infectious, CF, immune deficiency, ABPA, autoimmune, alpha-1 AT deficiency
Pseudomonas colonization: associated with worse outcomes
Massive hemoptysis: bronchial artery embolization
description: Permanent widening and damage of the airways (bronchi) that leads to chronic cough with excessive mucus production and recurrent lung infections.

name: Aspiration Pneumonia
signs:
Cough with foul-smelling sputum
Fever
Dyspnea
Tachypnea
Crackles in dependent lung zones
Hypoxia
History of aspiration event or risk factors
Insidious onset
treatment:
1. Antibiotics covering oral anaerobes and gram-negatives
2. Supplemental oxygen
3. Aspiration precautions (HOB elevation, NPO if active aspiration)
4. Speech-language pathology swallow evaluation
5. Diet modification based on swallow study
6. Treat underlying cause of aspiration
meds:
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h (if penicillin allergic)
Meropenem 1g IV q8h (if resistant organisms)
Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h + ceftriaxone 1g IV daily
ddx: Community-acquired pneumonia, Chemical pneumonitis, Lung abscess, PE, ARDS, Pulmonary edema
labs:
CXR (infiltrate in dependent segments - RLL, posterior segments)
CT chest if complications suspected
Sputum culture
Blood cultures
CBC, BMP, lactate
ABG if hypoxic
Swallow study (videofluoroscopy or FEES)
notes:
Dependent segments: supine = superior segments of lower lobes, posterior upper lobes; upright = basilar segments of lower lobes
Risk factors: altered consciousness, dysphagia, GERD, poor dentition, NG/OG tube, post-extubation
Chemical pneumonitis (Mendelson syndrome): sterile aspiration of gastric acid, no antibiotics initially
Aspiration of foreign body: may need bronchoscopy for removal
description: A lung infection that occurs when food, saliva, liquids, or vomit is inhaled into the lungs, commonly affecting people with swallowing difficulties or altered consciousness.

name: Respiratory Failure
signs:
Dyspnea and tachypnea
Use of accessory muscles
Cyanosis
Altered mental status
Diaphoresis
Tachycardia
Tripod positioning
Paradoxical abdominal breathing
Hypoxemia (SpO2 <90%)
treatment:
1. Supplemental oxygen (target SpO2 88-92% in COPD, 92-96% otherwise)
2. Noninvasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) if appropriate
3. Endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation if NIV fails
4. Treat underlying cause
5. Lung-protective ventilation if ARDS (low tidal volume 6mL/kg IBW)
6. Prone positioning if severe ARDS
meds:
Supplemental O2 via NC, Venturi mask, NRB, or HFNC
Albuterol 2.5mg nebulized (bronchospasm)
Methylprednisolone 125mg IV (COPD/asthma exacerbation)
Antibiotics if infectious etiology
Sedation for intubated patients: propofol, fentanyl, dexmedetomidine
Neuromuscular blockade: cisatracurium (severe ARDS)
ddx: COPD exacerbation, Asthma, Pneumonia, PE, ARDS, CHF/pulmonary edema, Neuromuscular disease, Pneumothorax
labs:
ABG (Type 1: PaO2 <60mmHg; Type 2: PaCO2 >50mmHg)
Pulse oximetry
CXR
CBC, BMP, lactate
BNP (cardiac vs pulmonary)
Blood cultures if sepsis suspected
CT chest/CTA if PE suspected
notes:
Type 1 (hypoxemic): PaO2 <60mmHg on room air - VQ mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment
Type 2 (hypercapnic): PaCO2 >50mmHg - hypoventilation, increased dead space
P/F ratio: PaO2/FiO2 - <300 = ARDS, <200 = moderate, <100 = severe
ARDSNet protocol: Vt 6mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure <30cmH2O
Avoid hyperoxia: target SpO2 92-96% (88-92% in COPD/CO2 retainers)
description: A life-threatening condition where the lungs cannot adequately oxygenate the blood or remove carbon dioxide, requiring urgent respiratory support.

name: Empyema
signs:
Fever persisting despite antibiotics for pneumonia
Pleuritic chest pain
Dyspnea
Decreased breath sounds
Dullness to percussion
Weight loss
Night sweats
Leukocytosis
treatment:
1. IV antibiotics
2. Chest tube drainage (large bore preferred)
3. Intrapleural fibrinolytics if loculated
4. VATS (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery) for organized empyema
5. Open decortication for chronic organized empyema
6. Nutritional support
meds:
