Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (if MRSA risk)
Intrapleural tPA 10mg + dornase alfa 5mg BID via chest tube
ddx: Complicated parapneumonic effusion, Lung abscess, Malignant pleural effusion, TB pleuritis, Chylothorax
labs:
CT chest with contrast (enhanced pleural thickening, split pleura sign)
Thoracentesis: frank pus, pH <7.2, glucose <40, LDH >1000, positive Gram stain/culture
Blood cultures
CBC (leukocytosis)
CRP/ESR
Pleural fluid culture (anaerobes common)
notes:
Evolution: simple parapneumonic → complicated parapneumonic → empyema
Exudative → fibrinopurulent → organizing stages
Most common cause: complication of bacterial pneumonia
MIST2 trial: intrapleural tPA + dornase alfa improves drainage and reduces surgical need
If chest tube drainage inadequate after 24-48h, consider VATS
description: A collection of pus in the pleural space between the lung and chest wall, usually occurring as a complication of pneumonia.

name: Croup
signs:
Barking/seal-like cough
Inspiratory stridor
Hoarseness
Low-grade fever
Runny nose (prodrome)
Worse at night
Subglottic narrowing (steeple sign on XR)
Respiratory distress in severe cases
treatment:
1. Single dose dexamethasone for all patients
2. Nebulized epinephrine for moderate-severe stridor at rest
3. Observe 2-4 hours after epinephrine (rebound possible)
4. Cool mist (traditional, limited evidence)
5. Minimize agitation
6. Hospitalize if persistent stridor at rest or respiratory distress
meds:
Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg PO/IM x1 (max 10mg)
Nebulized racemic epinephrine 0.5mL of 2.25% in 3mL NS
Nebulized L-epinephrine 0.5mL/kg of 1:1000 (max 5mL)
Prednisolone 1-2mg/kg if dexamethasone unavailable
Budesonide 2mg nebulized (alternative to dexamethasone)
ddx: Epiglottitis, Foreign body aspiration, Bacterial tracheitis, Retropharyngeal abscess, Peritonsillar abscess, Angioedema
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (labs rarely needed)
AP neck XR: steeple sign (subglottic narrowing)
Pulse oximetry
Avoid throat examination if epiglottitis suspected
Viral testing (parainfluenza) rarely changes management
notes:
Most common cause: parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3)
Age 6 months - 3 years most affected, peak in fall
Westley croup score: stridor, retractions, air entry, cyanosis, consciousness
Single dose dexamethasone is the most important intervention
Recurrent croup: consider subglottic stenosis, hemangioma
description: A common childhood respiratory infection that causes swelling around the vocal cords, resulting in a distinctive barking cough and noisy breathing.

name: Acute Bronchitis
signs:
Cough (productive or dry) lasting 1-3 weeks
Low-grade fever
Malaise
Rhonchi or wheezing
Chest discomfort with coughing
Sore throat
Rhinorrhea
No consolidation on exam
treatment:
1. Supportive care (rest, hydration)
2. Avoid antibiotics (viral in >90% of cases)
3. Cough suppressants if needed for sleep
4. Inhaler for bronchospasm
5. NSAIDs or acetaminophen for fever/malaise
6. Honey for cough (adults and children >1yr)
meds:
Dextromethorphan 10-20mg PO q4h PRN
Guaifenesin 200-400mg PO q4h (expectorant)
Albuterol MDI 2 puffs q4-6h PRN (if wheezing)
Ibuprofen 400mg PO q6h PRN
Honey 1-2 tsp PRN (cough suppressant)
Antibiotics NOT recommended routinely
ddx: Pneumonia, Asthma exacerbation, COPD exacerbation, Pertussis, Post-nasal drip, GERD, Influenza
labs:
Usually no workup needed
CXR only if pneumonia suspected (high fever, tachycardia, focal findings)
Influenza rapid test if within 48h of symptom onset
Pertussis PCR if cough >2 weeks
ProCalcitonin may guide antibiotic decision
notes:
Viral cause >90%: rhinovirus, influenza, coronavirus, RSV
Antibiotic stewardship: acute bronchitis is #1 reason for inappropriate antibiotic prescribing
Cough may persist 2-3 weeks (post-infectious cough)
No evidence for routine use of cough suppressants, mucolytics, or bronchodilators
Consider pertussis if cough >2 weeks with paroxysms
description: A self-limited inflammation of the large airways in the lungs, usually caused by a viral infection, resulting in a cough that typically resolves within a few weeks.

name: Hemoptysis
signs:
Coughing up blood or blood-tinged sputum
Dyspnea
Chest pain
History of lung disease
Weight loss (malignancy)
Fever (infection)
Massive hemoptysis: >500mL/24h or hemodynamic instability
treatment:
1. Assess severity and hemodynamic stability
2. Protect airway (affected side down if massive)
3. Bronchoscopy for localization and potential therapy
4. Bronchial artery embolization for massive hemoptysis
5. CT angiography to identify bleeding source
6. Surgical resection (last resort for uncontrollable bleeding)
7. Treat underlying cause
meds:
Tranexamic acid 1g IV or 500mg nebulized
Cough suppressants (codeine 15-30mg PO) to reduce shearing
IV fluids and blood products as needed
Antibiotics if infectious cause
Treat coagulopathy: vitamin K, FFP, platelets
ddx: Bronchitis, Bronchiectasis, Lung cancer, TB, Pulmonary embolism, AVM, Goodpasture syndrome, Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Mitral stenosis
labs:
CXR
CT chest (with contrast/CTA if massive)
CBC, coagulation studies, type and screen
BMP, BUN (Goodpasture)
Sputum AFB, culture, cytology
Bronchoscopy (diagnostic and therapeutic)
Anti-GBM antibodies, ANCA if diffuse alveolar hemorrhage suspected
UA (pulmonary-renal syndrome)
notes:
Massive hemoptysis (>500mL/24h): life-threatening, mortality >50% if untreated
Most common causes: bronchitis, bronchiectasis, lung cancer, TB
Bronchial arteries (systemic pressure) cause 90% of massive hemoptysis
Position patient with bleeding side down to protect unaffected lung
Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage: bilateral infiltrates + anemia + hemoptysis (Goodpasture, vasculitis, SLE)
description: Coughing up blood from the respiratory tract, which can range from blood-streaked sputum to life-threatening massive bleeding requiring emergency intervention.

name: Multiple Sclerosis
signs:
Optic neuritis (vision loss, pain with eye movement)
Sensory deficits (numbness, tingling)
Lhermitte sign (electric sensation down spine with neck flexion)
Spasticity
Ataxia
Bladder dysfunction
Fatigue
Internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Uhthoff phenomenon (worsening with heat)
treatment:
1. Acute relapse: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x3-5 days
2. Plasmapheresis for steroid-refractory relapses
3. Disease-modifying therapy (DMT) to reduce relapse rate
4. Symptomatic management (spasticity, pain, fatigue, bladder)
5. Physical therapy and rehabilitation
6. Vitamin D supplementation
meds:
Methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3-5 days (relapse)
Ocrelizumab 600mg IV q6 months
Natalizumab 300mg IV q4 weeks
Dimethyl fumarate 240mg PO BID
Fingolimod 0.5mg PO daily
Baclofen 5-20mg PO TID (spasticity)
Modafinil 200mg daily (fatigue)
Oxybutynin 5mg BID (bladder)
ddx: Neuromyelitis optica (NMOSD), CNS vasculitis, Sarcoidosis, SLE cerebritis, B12 deficiency, Lyme disease, HIV, ADEM
labs:
Brain MRI (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord T2/FLAIR lesions)
CSF: oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index
Visual evoked potentials (delayed)
Anti-AQP4 (rule out NMOSD)
Anti-MOG antibodies
Vitamin B12, RPR, HIV, Lyme
Revised McDonald criteria for diagnosis
notes:
McDonald criteria: dissemination in space AND time (can be satisfied by MRI alone)
Subtypes: relapsing-remitting (85%), secondary progressive, primary progressive
Prognostic factors: male sex, older onset, motor/cerebellar symptoms, frequent early relapses
JC virus screening (PML risk with natalizumab)
Pregnancy: reduced relapse rate, increase postpartum
description: A chronic autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the protective covering (myelin) of nerve fibers in the brain and spinal cord, causing communication problems between the brain and body.

name: Parkinson's Disease
signs:
Resting tremor (pill-rolling, 4-6 Hz)
Bradykinesia
Rigidity (cogwheel)
Postural instability (late feature)
Mask-like facies
Shuffling gait
Micrographia
Hypophonia
REM sleep behavior disorder
Anosmia
treatment:
1. Carbidopa/levodopa is most effective therapy
2. Dopamine agonists for younger patients (delay levodopa)
3. MAO-B inhibitors as monotherapy or adjunct
4. COMT inhibitors to extend levodopa effect
5. Deep brain stimulation for motor fluctuations
6. Physical therapy, speech therapy, occupational therapy
meds:
Carbidopa/levodopa 25/100mg TID (titrate to effect)
Pramipexole 0.125-1.5mg TID
Ropinirole 0.25-8mg TID
Rasagiline 1mg daily (MAO-B inhibitor)
Entacapone 200mg with each levodopa dose (COMT)
Amantadine 100mg BID (dyskinesias)
Rivastigmine patch 13.3mg/24h (dementia)
ddx: Essential tremor, MSA, PSP, Corticobasal degeneration, Lewy body dementia, Drug-induced parkinsonism, Normal pressure hydrocephalus, Wilson disease
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (no definitive test)
DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT) - confirms dopaminergic deficit
Brain MRI (rule out structural causes)
Copper/ceruloplasmin (Wilson disease if young)
TSH
Response to levodopa helps confirm diagnosis
notes:
Cardinal features: TRAP - Tremor, Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability
Asymmetric onset is classic (unilateral then bilateral)
Honeymoon period: good levodopa response for ~5 years
Motor complications: wearing off, on-off fluctuations, dyskinesias
Non-motor: depression, anxiety, constipation, orthostatic hypotension, RBD, cognitive decline
description: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the brain, leading to tremor, stiffness, and difficulty with movement.

name: Alzheimer's Disease
signs:
Progressive memory loss (short-term first)
Difficulty with familiar tasks
Language problems (word-finding difficulty)
Disorientation to time and place
Poor judgment
Personality and behavioral changes
Withdrawal from activities
Sundowning (evening agitation)
treatment:
1. Cholinesterase inhibitors for mild-moderate disease
2. Memantine for moderate-severe disease
3. Cognitive stimulation and engagement
4. Caregiver education and support
5. Safety planning (driving, wandering, finances)
6. Treat behavioral symptoms (depression, agitation, sleep)
7. Advance care planning early in disease
meds:
Donepezil 5-10mg PO daily
Rivastigmine patch 4.6-13.3mg/24h
Galantamine 4-12mg PO BID
Memantine 5-10mg PO BID (moderate-severe)
SSRI for depression/anxiety (sertraline 25-100mg)
Melatonin 3-6mg for sleep
Avoid: anticholinergics, benzodiazepines
ddx: Vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, Frontotemporal dementia, Normal pressure hydrocephalus, B12 deficiency, Hypothyroidism, Depression (pseudodementia), Medication side effects
labs:
Clinical diagnosis + cognitive testing (MMSE, MoCA)
Brain MRI (hippocampal/medial temporal atrophy, rule out other causes)
Amyloid PET scan
CSF biomarkers (low Abeta-42, elevated tau, p-tau)
CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, folate, RPR
Urinalysis (delirium workup)
Neuropsychological testing
notes:
Most common cause of dementia (60-80% of cases)
Pathology: amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles
Genetics: APOE4 allele increases risk; early-onset familial: APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 mutations
Staging: preclinical → MCI due to AD → mild → moderate → severe dementia
Medication side effects: avoid anticholinergics (Beers criteria)
Lecanemab: anti-amyloid antibody (modest benefit, ARIA risk)
description: The most common form of dementia, a progressive brain disorder that slowly destroys memory, thinking skills, and eventually the ability to carry out simple tasks.

name: Transient Ischemic Attack
signs:
Sudden onset focal neurological deficit
Speech difficulty (aphasia, dysarthria)
Unilateral weakness or numbness
Visual loss (monocular = amaurosis fugax)
Ataxia
Symptoms resolve completely within 24h (usually <1h)
No evidence of infarction on imaging
treatment:
1. Emergent evaluation (treat as stroke until proven otherwise)
2. Antiplatelet therapy immediately
3. Carotid imaging (duplex US or CTA)
4. Cardiac monitoring (AFib detection)
5. Statin initiation
6. Blood pressure management
7. Carotid endarterectomy if >=70% ipsilateral stenosis
8. ABCD2 score for risk stratification
meds:
Aspirin 325mg PO immediately, then 81mg daily
Clopidogrel 75mg daily (dual antiplatelet x21 days per POINT trial)
Atorvastatin 80mg daily
Antihypertensive (after acute phase): lisinopril, amlodipine
Anticoagulation if AFib detected (apixaban 5mg BID)
ddx: Ischemic stroke, Migraine with aura, Seizure (Todd paralysis), Hypoglycemia, Conversion disorder, MS relapse, Brain tumor, Vestibular disorder
labs:
Brain MRI with DWI (rule out acute infarction)
CT head (rule out hemorrhage)
CTA head/neck or carotid duplex
ECG and cardiac monitoring (>=24h)
Echo (TTE or TEE)
CBC, BMP, glucose, lipids, HbA1c
PT/INR, PTT
ABCD2 score
notes:
ABCD2 score: Age >=60(1), BP >=140/90(1), Clinical (unilateral weakness 2, speech only 1), Duration (>=60min 2, 10-59min 1), Diabetes(1)
Score >=4: high 2-day stroke risk, consider hospitalization
Dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel) x21 days reduces 90-day stroke risk (CHANCE, POINT trials)
10-15% stroke risk within 90 days after TIA without treatment
Time-based definition replaced by tissue-based: TIA = transient symptoms + no infarction on DWI
description: A temporary episode of neurological dysfunction caused by brief interruption of blood flow to the brain, often called a 'mini-stroke,' serving as a warning sign for future stroke.

name: Epilepsy
signs:
Recurrent unprovoked seizures
Generalized tonic-clonic: loss of consciousness, stiffening, jerking
Absence: brief staring spells
Focal: motor, sensory, or autonomic symptoms
Post-ictal confusion
Tongue biting (lateral)
Urinary incontinence
Aura (focal onset)
treatment:
1. Anti-seizure medication (ASM) after 2 unprovoked seizures or 1 seizure with high recurrence risk
2. Choose ASM based on seizure type
3. Monotherapy preferred initially
4. Epilepsy surgery evaluation for drug-resistant epilepsy (failed >=2 ASMs)
5. Vagus nerve stimulation or responsive neurostimulation
6. Seizure precautions: driving restrictions, water safety
meds:
Levetiracetam 500-1500mg BID (broad-spectrum, first-line)
Lamotrigine 100-200mg BID (focal and generalized)
Valproate 500-1000mg BID (generalized - avoid in women of childbearing age)
Carbamazepine 200-600mg BID (focal only)
Oxcarbazepine 300-1200mg BID
Lacosamide 100-200mg BID
Brivaracetam 50-100mg BID
ddx: Syncope, Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES), TIA, Migraine, Hypoglycemia, Cardiac arrhythmia, Panic attack, Movement disorder
labs:
EEG (interictal epileptiform discharges)
Brain MRI (structural cause)
Prolactin level (elevated 10-20min post-seizure, helps differentiate from PNES)
BMP (electrolytes, glucose)
CBC
LFTs, drug levels for monitoring
Video-EEG monitoring (pre-surgical evaluation)
Genetic testing in select cases
notes:
Definition: >=2 unprovoked seizures >24h apart, OR 1 seizure + high recurrence risk (>=60%), OR epilepsy syndrome
Drug-resistant epilepsy: failed adequate trials of 2 ASMs
SUDEP (sudden unexpected death): risk ~1/1000 patient-years, higher with uncontrolled GTC seizures
Women: folic acid 1-4mg daily, lamotrigine/levetiracetam preferred in pregnancy
Driving: most states require 3-12 months seizure-free
description: A brain disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain, requiring long-term management with anti-seizure medications.

name: Bell's Palsy
signs:
Sudden unilateral facial weakness (upper AND lower face)
Inability to close eye on affected side
Drooping corner of mouth
Loss of nasolabial fold
Difficulty eating/drinking
Hyperacusis
Decreased taste (anterior 2/3 tongue)
Postauricular pain
treatment:
1. Oral corticosteroids within 72h of onset (most important)
2. Eye protection: artificial tears, eye patch at night
3. Antiviral added in severe cases (debated)
4. Physical therapy for facial exercises
5. Surgical decompression rarely needed
meds:
Prednisone 60-80mg daily x7 days then taper over 7 days
Valacyclovir 1g TID x7 days (severe/complete paralysis)
Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose) q1-2h while awake
Lacri-Lube ointment at night
Taping eyelid shut at night
ddx: Stroke (central facial palsy - spares forehead), Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster + vesicles), Lyme disease, Otitis media, Parotid tumor, Sarcoidosis, GBS, Cholesteatoma
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Lyme serology if endemic area or bilateral
MRI brain/IAC if atypical or no improvement in 3 weeks
Electroneurography (ENoG) if complete paralysis
No routine labs needed for typical presentation
HIV testing if risk factors
notes:
Upper vs lower motor neuron: Bell's palsy = LMN (entire half of face); stroke = UMN (spares forehead)
Recovery: 85% recover completely within 3 months
House-Brackmann grading scale (I-VI)
Ramsay Hunt: facial palsy + vesicular eruption on ear + hearing loss (worse prognosis)
Bilateral facial palsy: think Lyme, GBS, sarcoidosis, HIV
Incomplete eye closure: risk of corneal ulceration
description: Sudden weakness or paralysis of the muscles on one side of the face due to inflammation of the facial nerve, usually temporary and thought to be triggered by viral infections.

name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
signs:
Severe, electric shock-like facial pain
Unilateral, along trigeminal nerve distribution (V2/V3 most common)
Brief paroxysms (seconds to 2 minutes)
Trigger zones (touching face, chewing, talking, brushing teeth)
Pain-free intervals between attacks
No neurological deficit on exam
treatment:
1. Carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine (first-line)
2. Add baclofen or lamotrigine if partial response
3. Microvascular decompression (surgery for refractory)
4. Gamma knife radiosurgery
5. Percutaneous procedures (balloon compression, glycerol rhizolysis)
6. MRI to rule out secondary causes
meds:
Carbamazepine 100-400mg BID (first-line)
Oxcarbazepine 150-600mg BID
Baclofen 10-20mg TID (adjunct)
Lamotrigine 200-400mg daily
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (less effective)
Phenytoin 200-400mg daily (acute IV loading option)
ddx: Dental pathology, TMJ disorder, Cluster headache, Post-herpetic neuralgia, MS (bilateral TN in young patient), Glossopharyngeal neuralgia, Sinusitis, Tumor
labs:
Brain MRI with thin cuts through posterior fossa (rule out tumor, MS, vascular loop)
MRA (identify vascular compression)
No routine labs needed
Dental evaluation to rule out odontogenic pain
Carbamazepine: check HLA-B*1502 in Asian descent (SJS risk), monitor CBC and sodium
notes:
Classic TN: caused by neurovascular compression (usually SCA)
Secondary TN: caused by MS, tumor, AVM
V2 (maxillary) and V3 (mandibular) most commonly affected
Bilateral TN: consider MS (especially in young patients)
Carbamazepine: monitor CBC (agranulocytosis), sodium (hyponatremia), LFTs
Microvascular decompression: 80% long-term pain relief
description: A chronic pain condition affecting the trigeminal nerve in the face, causing sudden, severe, shock-like pain episodes typically triggered by everyday activities like eating or touching the face.

name: Cluster Headache
signs:
Severe unilateral periorbital/temporal pain
Autonomic features: lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea
Partial Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis)
Restlessness/agitation during attack
Attacks 15-180 minutes
Circadian pattern (often wake from sleep)
1-8 attacks per day during cluster period
treatment:
1. Acute: high-flow oxygen 12-15L/min via NRB x15-20min
2. Sumatriptan SC for acute attacks
3. Transitional: prednisone burst, occipital nerve block
4. Preventive: verapamil (first-line)
5. Galcanezumab for episodic cluster headache
6. Avoid alcohol during cluster period
meds:
Oxygen 100% 12-15L/min via NRB (acute, first-line)
Sumatriptan 6mg SC (acute)
Zolmitriptan 5mg nasal spray (acute)
Verapamil 240-960mg daily in divided doses (prevention)
Prednisone 60mg daily x5 days then taper (transitional)
Lithium 300mg BID-TID (prevention)
Galcanezumab 300mg SC monthly x3
ddx: Migraine, Trigeminal neuralgia, Paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA, Temporal arteritis, Pheochromocytoma, Carotid dissection
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Brain MRI (rule out secondary causes: pituitary tumor, cavernous sinus lesion)
ESR if >50yo (temporal arteritis)
ECG before verapamil
Lithium level, TSH, BMP if on lithium
PSG if sleep apnea suspected (comorbid)
notes:
Male predominance 3:1, onset 20-40 years
Episodic: cluster periods 2 weeks-3 months with remission >=3 months
Chronic: no remission >3 months (10-15%)
Verapamil: get ECG at baseline and with dose increases (heart block risk)
Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia (TAC) family
Indometacin-responsive: paroxysmal hemicrania (not cluster)
Key difference from migraine: restlessness vs. wanting to lie still
description: An extremely painful headache disorder that occurs in cyclical patterns (clusters), causing severe one-sided head pain with eye watering and nasal congestion.

name: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
signs:
Gait apraxia (magnetic/shuffling gait - earliest and most responsive to treatment)
Urinary incontinence
Dementia (subcortical pattern)
Classic triad: wet, wacky, wobbly
Symptoms develop over months
Ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy
treatment:
1. High-volume lumbar puncture (30-50mL CSF removal) as diagnostic/therapeutic trial
2. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt placement (definitive)
3. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (select cases)
4. Physical therapy for gait rehabilitation
5. Cognitive rehabilitation
6. Shunt monitoring and adjustment
meds:
No primary pharmacologic treatment
Acetazolamide 250-500mg BID (temporizing, limited evidence)
Treat comorbid conditions
Avoid anticholinergics (worsen cognition)
Manage UTI if incontinence-related infections
ddx: Alzheimer's disease, Vascular dementia, Parkinson's disease, Cervical spondylotic myelopathy, B12 deficiency, Depression, Urological causes of incontinence
labs:
Brain MRI (ventriculomegaly, Evans index >0.3, DESH pattern, tight high convexity)
High-volume LP (gait improvement after removing 30-50mL CSF = positive tap test)
CSF pressure (normal or high-normal: <18cmH2O)
Continuous lumbar drainage trial (72h)
Cisternography (less used)
Neuropsychological testing
Timed Up and Go test (before and after LP)
notes:
Triad: gait disturbance (earliest, most responsive to shunt) → urinary incontinence → dementia
Evans index = max width of frontal horns / max internal skull diameter (>0.3 = ventriculomegaly)
Best shunt candidates: known cause (SAH, meningitis), short symptom duration, gait predominant
Shunt complications: subdural hematoma, infection, over-drainage, obstruction
Gait improvement post-LP predicts shunt response (80% positive predictive value)
description: A brain condition where cerebrospinal fluid accumulates in the ventricles despite normal pressure readings, causing the classic triad of walking difficulty, urinary incontinence, and memory problems.

name: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
signs:
Progressive muscle weakness (asymmetric)
Muscle atrophy and fasciculations (LMN signs)
Hyperreflexia and spasticity (UMN signs)
Dysarthria and dysphagia (bulbar onset)
No sensory loss
Emotional lability (pseudobulbar affect)
Respiratory failure (late)
treatment:
1. Riluzole (modest survival benefit)
2. Edaravone (may slow functional decline)
3. Multidisciplinary care (neurology, pulm, PT/OT/SLP, nutrition, palliative)
4. BiPAP for respiratory support
5. PEG tube for nutrition when dysphagia progresses
6. Communication devices
7. Palliative care and advance directives early
meds:
Riluzole 50mg PO BID
Edaravone 60mg IV daily x14 days in 28-day cycles
Tofersen (SOD1 mutation only)
Dextromethorphan/quinidine 20/10mg BID (pseudobulbar affect)
Baclofen 10-20mg TID (spasticity)
Lorazepam 0.5-1mg SL (end-of-life dyspnea)
Glycopyrrolate 1-2mg TID (sialorrhea)
ddx: Cervical myelopathy, Multifocal motor neuropathy, Myasthenia gravis, Kennedy disease, Inclusion body myositis, Primary lateral sclerosis, MS
labs:
EMG/NCV (active and chronic denervation in multiple regions, normal sensory)
Brain and spine MRI (rule out structural causes)
CBC, CMP, TSH, B12
Creatine kinase (mildly elevated)
PFTs with MIP/MEP (respiratory monitoring)
Genetic testing (SOD1, C9orf72 if familial)
Anti-GM1 antibodies (rule out MMN)
notes:
El Escorial criteria: UMN + LMN signs in multiple regions, progressive spread, no alternative explanation
Median survival 3-5 years from symptom onset
Bulbar onset: worse prognosis (2-3 years)
FVC <50% predicted: consider BiPAP
5-10% familial; C9orf72 repeat expansion most common genetic cause
Sensory involvement EXCLUDES ALS - look for alternative diagnosis
description: A progressive neurodegenerative disease that destroys motor neurons controlling voluntary muscles, leading to increasing paralysis while typically leaving mental function intact.

name: Encephalitis
signs:
Altered mental status
Fever
Seizures
Focal neurological deficits
Headache
Behavioral/personality changes
Movement disorders
Language dysfunction
Memory impairment (temporal lobe involvement)
treatment:
1. Empiric acyclovir immediately (do not wait for results)
2. Supportive care: airway, seizure management, ICP monitoring
3. Dexamethasone for autoimmune encephalitis
4. Immunotherapy for autoimmune: IVIG, plasma exchange, rituximab
5. Seizure prophylaxis
6. ICU care for severe cases
meds:
Acyclovir 10mg/kg IV q8h x14-21 days (HSV empiric)
Levetiracetam 500-1500mg BID (seizure prophylaxis)
Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg IV q6h (bacterial/autoimmune)
IVIG 0.4g/kg/day x5 days (autoimmune)
Rituximab 375mg/m2 weekly x4 (refractory autoimmune)
Mannitol 1g/kg IV (elevated ICP)
ddx: Meningitis, Brain abscess, Status epilepticus, Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR), Metabolic encephalopathy, Toxic ingestion, CNS vasculitis, Prion disease
labs:
Brain MRI (temporal lobe involvement suggests HSV)
CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose, HSV PCR
EEG (PLEDs in HSV, extreme delta brush in anti-NMDAR)
CSF viral panel (HSV, VZV, enterovirus, arbovirus)
Autoimmune antibodies: anti-NMDAR, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2
HIV test
Blood cultures
CBC, BMP, LFTs
notes:
HSV encephalitis: most common sporadic cause, temporal lobe predilection, mortality >70% untreated
Start acyclovir empirically in ALL suspected encephalitis - do not delay for LP/MRI
Anti-NMDAR encephalitis: young women, psychiatric symptoms, movement disorder, autonomic instability (check for ovarian teratoma)
Arboviral: West Nile, Eastern equine, Japanese encephalitis (geographic/seasonal)
Autoimmune encephalitis increasingly recognized - check antibody panels
description: Inflammation of the brain itself, most commonly caused by viral infections like herpes simplex, leading to altered consciousness, seizures, and potentially permanent brain damage if not treated promptly.

name: Essential Tremor
signs:
Bilateral action/postural tremor (hands most common)
Frequency 4-12 Hz
Improves with alcohol
Head tremor (titubation)
Voice tremor
No resting tremor component
Absence of other neurological findings
Family history common
Worsens with stress, fatigue, caffeine
treatment:
1. Reassurance if mild and not functionally limiting
2. Propranolol or primidone (first-line)
3. Topiramate or gabapentin (second-line)
4. Botulinum toxin for head/voice tremor
5. Deep brain stimulation (VIM thalamus) for refractory disabling tremor
6. MRI-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy
meds:
Propranolol 60-320mg daily (long-acting preferred)
Primidone 25mg at bedtime, titrate to 250mg TID
Topiramate 25-200mg BID
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID
Alprazolam 0.25-0.5mg TID (short-term, situational)
Botulinum toxin injections (head/voice tremor)
ddx: Parkinson's disease (resting tremor), Physiologic tremor (enhanced), Cerebellar tremor, Drug-induced tremor, Hyperthyroidism, Wilson disease, Dystonic tremor
labs:
Clinical diagnosis - no definitive test
TSH (rule out hyperthyroidism)
Ceruloplasmin, copper (Wilson disease if <40yo)
DaTscan (to differentiate from Parkinson's if unclear)
Brain MRI (rule out structural cause)
LFTs if starting primidone
notes:
Most common movement disorder (estimated 4-5% of population)
Autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance
Key differentiator from PD: action/postural (ET) vs resting tremor (PD), bilateral (ET) vs asymmetric (PD)
Alcohol responsiveness supports diagnosis but don't recommend as treatment
DBS of VIM nucleus: 70-90% tremor reduction
Often misdiagnosed as Parkinson's disease
description: The most common movement disorder, causing involuntary rhythmic shaking primarily of the hands during voluntary movements, often running in families and typically worsening slowly over time.

name: Crohn's Disease
signs:
Abdominal pain (RLQ common)
Chronic diarrhea (often non-bloody)
Weight loss
Fatigue
Perianal disease (fistulas, abscesses, skin tags)
Mouth ulcers
Skip lesions on endoscopy
Cobblestone mucosa
Extraintestinal: arthritis, erythema nodosum, uveitis
treatment:
1. Induction: corticosteroids or biologics
2. Maintenance: immunomodulators or biologics
3. Step-up vs top-down approach based on severity
4. Surgery for complications (strictures, fistulas, abscesses)
5. Nutritional support
6. Smoking cessation (worsens Crohn's)
7. Perianal disease: antibiotics + biologics + surgery
meds:
Budesonide 9mg daily (ileal/right colon)
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper (moderate-severe)
Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg daily
Methotrexate 25mg SC weekly
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV at 0,2,6 weeks then q8wk
Adalimumab 160mg SC then 80mg then 40mg q2wk
Vedolizumab 300mg IV at 0,2,6 wks then q8wk
Ustekinumab 6mg/kg IV then 90mg SC q8wk
ddx: Ulcerative colitis, IBS, Celiac disease, Intestinal TB, Appendicitis, Diverticulitis, Lymphoma, Behcet disease
labs:
Colonoscopy with biopsies (skip lesions, transmural inflammation, granulomas)
CRP/ESR elevated
Fecal calprotectin (>250 suggests active inflammation)
CBC (anemia)
Albumin (malnutrition)
CT/MR enterography (small bowel involvement, strictures)
Vitamin B12, folate, iron, vitamin D levels
TBST before biologics
notes:
Can affect any part of GI tract (mouth to anus), most common in terminal ileum
Transmural inflammation → fistulas, strictures, abscesses
Skip lesions distinguish from UC (continuous involvement)
Smoking worsens Crohn's (opposite of UC)
Screen for TB and hepatitis B before starting biologics
Colonoscopy surveillance for CRC starting 8 years after diagnosis
description: A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the digestive tract, causing transmural inflammation that leads to abdominal pain, diarrhea, and complications like fistulas and strictures.

name: Ulcerative Colitis
signs:
Bloody diarrhea
Rectal urgency and tenesmus
Abdominal cramping (LLQ)
Fever (severe flares)
Weight loss
Extraintestinal: primary sclerosing cholangitis, arthritis, pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum
treatment:
1. Mild-moderate: 5-ASA (mesalamine) oral + rectal
2. Moderate-severe: corticosteroids for induction, biologics/immunomodulators for maintenance
3. Refractory: cyclosporine or infliximab
4. Toxic megacolon: IV steroids, antibiotics, surgical consult
5. Colectomy is curative
6. CRC surveillance colonoscopy q1-2 years after 8 years
meds:
Mesalamine 2.4-4.8g daily (oral) + suppository/enema
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper
Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg daily
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV
Adalimumab 160mg then 80mg then 40mg q2wk
Tofacitinib 10mg BID induction then 5mg BID
Vedolizumab 300mg IV
Cyclosporine 2mg/kg IV (acute severe refractory)
ddx: Crohn's disease, Infectious colitis (C. diff, CMV, E. coli), Ischemic colitis, Radiation colitis, Diverticulitis, IBS, Colorectal cancer
labs:
Colonoscopy with biopsies (continuous inflammation from rectum, pseudopolyps, loss of haustra)
Stool studies (rule out infection, C. diff)
CRP/ESR
Fecal calprotectin
CBC (anemia, leukocytosis)
Albumin
LFTs and ANCA/ASCA (distinguish from Crohn's)
p-ANCA positive in 65-70% UC
Abdominal XR (toxic megacolon screening)
notes:
Always starts at rectum, extends proximally in continuous pattern
Mucosal/submucosal inflammation (unlike transmural in Crohn's)
Toxic megacolon: colon >6cm, systemic toxicity → emergent colectomy
PSC association: 5% of UC patients, increases CRC risk further
Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) is curative
CRC risk: cumulative 2% at 10yr, 8% at 20yr, 18% at 30yr
description: A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that causes inflammation and ulcers along the inner lining of the large intestine and rectum, typically presenting with bloody diarrhea.

name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
signs:
Heartburn (retrosternal burning)
Regurgitation
Dysphagia
Chronic cough
Hoarseness
Dental erosion
Chest pain (non-cardiac)
Worse after meals, lying down
Relieved by antacids
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modifications: weight loss, elevate HOB, avoid triggers
2. PPI trial (first-line for moderate-severe)
3. H2 blockers for mild symptoms or on-demand use
4. EGD if alarm symptoms or refractory
5. Anti-reflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) for refractory GERD
6. Step-down PPI therapy after 8 weeks
meds:
Omeprazole 20mg daily (or BID if severe)
Pantoprazole 40mg daily
Famotidine 20mg BID (H2 blocker)
Calcium carbonate/antacids PRN
Sucralfate 1g QID (mucosal protection)
Baclofen 10mg TID (reduces transient LES relaxations)
ddx: Peptic ulcer disease, Eosinophilic esophagitis, Esophageal cancer, Achalasia, Coronary artery disease, Functional dyspepsia, Gastroparesis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (PPI trial is diagnostic and therapeutic)
EGD: erosive esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, stricture
Biopsies if Barrett suspected
24h pH monitoring (gold standard for quantifying reflux)
Esophageal manometry (pre-surgical)
H. pylori testing
notes:
Alarm symptoms (need EGD): dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia, vomiting, age >60 new onset
Barrett esophagus: intestinal metaplasia → dysplasia → adenocarcinoma risk
Long-term PPI risks: C. diff, osteoporosis, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, CKD (try to use lowest dose/shortest duration)
H. pylori: test and treat if found
Avoid: caffeine, alcohol, chocolate, fatty foods, mint, late meals
description: A chronic digestive condition where stomach acid frequently flows back into the esophagus, causing heartburn, regurgitation, and potential damage to the esophageal lining.

name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
signs:
Epigastric pain/burning
Gastric ulcer: pain worsened by eating
Duodenal ulcer: pain relieved by eating, worse 2-3h after meals
Nausea
Bloating
Melena or hematemesis (if bleeding)
Weight loss
Perforation: sudden severe pain, rigidity
treatment:
1. PPI therapy (first-line)
2. H. pylori eradication if positive
3. Stop NSAIDs/aspirin if possible
4. EGD for bleeding ulcer (epinephrine injection, clips, cautery)
5. Surgery for perforation, refractory bleeding, obstruction
6. Repeat EGD for gastric ulcers to confirm healing and rule out malignancy
meds:
Omeprazole 40mg daily x4-8 weeks
H. pylori triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin 500mg BID + amoxicillin 1g BID x14 days
Bismuth quadruple: PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline x14 days
Sucralfate 1g QID
Misoprostol 200mcg QID (NSAID ulcer prevention)
IV PPI 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr (bleeding ulcer)
ddx: GERD, Gastric cancer, Gastritis, Functional dyspepsia, Pancreatitis, Biliary colic, Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, Mesenteric ischemia
labs:
EGD with biopsies (gold standard)
H. pylori testing: stool antigen, urea breath test, or biopsy-based
CBC (anemia if chronic bleeding)
BMP
Fasting gastrin level if recurrent/refractory (Zollinger-Ellison)
CXR if perforation suspected (free air under diaphragm)
CT abdomen (perforation, complications)
notes:
H. pylori causes ~60% of gastric and ~90% of duodenal ulcers
NSAIDs: second most common cause
Confirm H. pylori eradication 4 weeks after treatment completion
Gastric ulcers: always biopsy (rule out malignancy)
Zollinger-Ellison: refractory/multiple ulcers, diarrhea, fasting gastrin >1000
Forrest classification for bleeding ulcers predicts rebleeding risk
description: Open sores that develop on the inner lining of the stomach or upper small intestine, most commonly caused by H. pylori infection or NSAID use.

name: Diverticulitis
signs:
LLQ abdominal pain
Fever
Nausea/vomiting
Change in bowel habits (constipation or diarrhea)
Abdominal tenderness and guarding
Palpable mass (phlegmon or abscess)
Rectal bleeding (usually mild)
treatment:
1. Uncomplicated: outpatient antibiotics or observation
2. Complicated (abscess, perforation, fistula): hospitalization, IV antibiotics
3. CT-guided percutaneous drainage for abscess >3cm
4. Surgery for perforation with peritonitis, failure of medical management, fistula
5. Elective sigmoid colectomy after recovery from complicated episode
6. Colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after resolution
meds:
Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID + metronidazole 500mg TID x7-10 days (outpatient)
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125mg BID (alternative)
Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h (inpatient)
Acetaminophen 1g q6h (avoid NSAIDs)
Mesalamine (prevention - limited evidence)
High-fiber diet after resolution
ddx: Colon cancer, IBD, Appendicitis (right-sided), IBS, Ischemic colitis, Ovarian pathology, UTI, PID
labs:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (pericolonic fat stranding, wall thickening, abscess, free air)
CBC (leukocytosis)
CRP elevated
Urinalysis (rule out UTI, detect colovesical fistula)
Blood cultures if septic
Colonoscopy after resolution (rule out malignancy - NOT during acute episode)
notes:
Most common: left-sided (sigmoid) in Western countries; right-sided more common in Asian populations
Hinchey classification: I-pericolic abscess, II-pelvic abscess, III-purulent peritonitis, IV-fecal peritonitis
Recent evidence: uncomplicated diverticulitis may not require antibiotics (DIABOLO trial)
Prevention: high-fiber diet, exercise, normal weight
Colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after to rule out underlying malignancy (found in 1-2%)
description: Inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) that can form in the walls of the large intestine, most commonly causing left lower abdominal pain and fever.

name: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis
signs:
Abdominal pain and tenderness
Fever
Altered mental status
Worsening ascites
Ileus
Hypotension
May be subtle or asymptomatic in cirrhotics
treatment:
1. Diagnostic paracentesis in ALL cirrhotics admitted with ascites
2. Empiric IV antibiotics immediately if PMN >250/mm3
3. IV albumin to prevent hepatorenal syndrome
4. Repeat paracentesis at 48h to confirm improvement
5. Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis after first episode
6. Liver transplant evaluation
meds:
Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h x5-7 days (first-line)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (alternative)
Albumin 1.5g/kg day 1 + 1g/kg day 3
Norfloxacin 400mg daily (secondary prophylaxis)
TMP/SMX DS daily (prophylaxis alternative)
Ciprofloxacin 500mg daily (prophylaxis)
ddx: Secondary peritonitis (perforation), Peritoneal carcinomatosis, Tuberculous peritonitis, Pancreatitis, Hepatorenal syndrome
labs:
Paracentesis: cell count (PMN >250/mm3 = diagnostic), culture (blood culture bottles at bedside), protein, albumin, glucose, LDH
SAAG >1.1 (portal hypertension-related ascites)
Blood cultures
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Lactate
UA (rule out UTI as source)
notes:
Diagnostic threshold: ascitic fluid PMN >250/mm3 (start antibiotics before culture results)
Most common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus
Secondary peritonitis clues: polymicrobial, protein >1g/dL, glucose <50, LDH > serum
Albumin reduces hepatorenal syndrome from 30% to 10% (Sort trial)
Prophylaxis indications: prior SBP, ascitic protein <1.5 + renal dysfunction or liver failure, GI bleed
description: A bacterial infection of the ascitic fluid in the abdomen that occurs in patients with liver disease, often presenting subtly but requiring urgent antibiotic treatment.

name: Celiac Disease
signs:
Chronic diarrhea (steatorrhea)
Abdominal bloating and pain
Weight loss
Iron deficiency anemia
Fatigue
Dermatitis herpetiformis
Osteoporosis
Failure to thrive (children)
Peripheral neuropathy
Dental enamel defects
treatment:
1. Strict lifelong gluten-free diet (wheat, barley, rye)
2. Dietitian consultation
3. Correct nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D)
4. DEXA scan for osteoporosis
5. Monitor adherence with serologies
6. Repeat biopsy if symptoms persist despite diet
7. Screen first-degree relatives
meds:
Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325mg daily)
Calcium 1200mg + Vitamin D 1000IU daily
Folate 1mg daily
Multivitamin
Dapsone 25-50mg daily (dermatitis herpetiformis)
No pharmacologic treatment for celiac itself - diet only
Budesonide (refractory celiac, short-term)
ddx: IBS, IBD, Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO), Tropical sprue, Lactose intolerance, Whipple disease, Pancreatic insufficiency, Giardiasis
labs:
tTG-IgA (tissue transglutaminase - best screening test)
Total IgA (IgA deficiency causes false-negative tTG)
Endomysial antibody (EMA) - confirmatory
Small bowel biopsy (villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytes) - gold standard
CBC (iron deficiency anemia)
Iron studies, B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium
DEXA scan
HLA-DQ2/DQ8 (99% have one - good negative predictive value)
notes:
Must be on gluten-containing diet during testing (false negatives if already GF)
HLA-DQ2 (95%) or DQ8 (5%) - absence essentially rules out celiac
Associated: Type 1 DM, thyroid disease, Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, IgA deficiency
Refractory celiac: persistent symptoms/villous atrophy despite strict GF diet >12 months
Dermatitis herpetiformis: pathognomonic skin manifestation, granular IgA at dermal papillae
Small increased risk of intestinal T-cell lymphoma and small bowel adenocarcinoma
description: An autoimmune disorder where eating gluten triggers an immune response that damages the small intestine's lining, impairing nutrient absorption and causing digestive symptoms.

name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
signs:
Recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation
Altered bowel habits (diarrhea, constipation, or mixed)
Bloating and distension
Mucus in stool
Symptoms worse with stress
No nocturnal symptoms
No weight loss or rectal bleeding (alarm features absent)
treatment:
1. Reassurance and patient education
2. Dietary modifications (low FODMAP diet)
3. Fiber supplementation for IBS-C
4. Antispasmodics for pain
5. Loperamide for IBS-D
6. Psychological therapy (CBT, gut-directed hypnotherapy)
7. Treat associated anxiety/depression
meds:
Psyllium fiber 5-10g daily (IBS-C)
Dicyclomine 10-20mg QID PRN (antispasmodic)
Loperamide 2-4mg PRN (IBS-D)
Linaclotide 290mcg daily (IBS-C)
Rifaximin 550mg TID x14 days (IBS-D)
Alosetron 0.5-1mg BID (severe IBS-D, women only)
Amitriptyline 10-25mg at bedtime (pain)
SSRI (comorbid anxiety/depression)
ddx: IBD, Celiac disease, Colorectal cancer, Microscopic colitis, SIBO, Lactose intolerance, Endometriosis, Hyperthyroidism
labs:
Diagnosis of exclusion using Rome IV criteria
CBC, CRP/ESR (normal in IBS)
Celiac serologies (tTG-IgA)
Fecal calprotectin (normal in IBS, elevated in IBD)
TSH
Stool studies if diarrhea-predominant
Colonoscopy if alarm features or age >45
Hydrogen breath test (lactose intolerance, SIBO)
notes:
Rome IV criteria: recurrent abdominal pain >=1 day/week x3 months, associated with >=2 of: related to defecation, change in frequency, change in form
Subtypes: IBS-D (diarrhea), IBS-C (constipation), IBS-M (mixed), IBS-U (unsubtyped)
Alarm features requiring investigation: >50yo onset, rectal bleeding, weight loss, nocturnal symptoms, family hx CRC/IBD, anemia
Low FODMAP diet effective in 50-70% but restrictive
Gut-brain axis dysfunction is central to pathophysiology
description: A common functional gastrointestinal disorder causing recurrent abdominal pain and altered bowel habits without any identifiable structural or biochemical abnormality.

name: Gastroparesis
signs:
Nausea and vomiting (often of undigested food)
Early satiety
Postprandial fullness
Bloating
Abdominal pain
Weight loss
Erratic blood glucose (diabetics)
Succussion splash
treatment:
1. Dietary modifications (small, frequent, low-fat, low-fiber meals)
2. Prokinetic agents
3. Antiemetics for symptom relief
4. Optimize glycemic control (diabetic gastroparesis)
5. Gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra) for refractory cases
6. Pyloric interventions (G-POEM, pyloroplasty)
7. Jejunostomy tube if unable to maintain nutrition
meds:
Metoclopramide 5-10mg PO/IV TID (max 12 weeks, risk of tardive dyskinesia)
Domperidone 10mg TID (not FDA-approved, available via expanded access)
Erythromycin 250mg IV/PO TID (short-term, tachyphylaxis)
Ondansetron 4-8mg TID PRN
Promethazine 12.5-25mg PO/PR q6h PRN
Nortriptyline 10-25mg at bedtime (pain/nausea)
ddx: Gastric outlet obstruction (mechanical), Cyclic vomiting syndrome, Functional dyspepsia, Eating disorders, SMA syndrome, Rumination syndrome, Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome
labs:
Gastric emptying scintigraphy (>10% retained at 4h = abnormal) - gold standard
EGD (rule out mechanical obstruction)
Small bowel follow-through
HbA1c (diabetic control)
TSH
BMP
CBC
Wireless motility capsule (SmartPill)
Antroduodenal manometry (specialized centers)
notes:
Most common causes: diabetic (30%), post-surgical (13%), idiopathic (35%)
Stop medications that delay gastric emptying before testing: opioids, GLP-1 agonists, anticholinergics
4h gastric emptying study: gold standard (standardized low-fat egg white meal)
Metoclopramide: FDA black box warning for tardive dyskinesia - limit to 12 weeks
GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide) can cause/worsen gastroparesis
G-POEM: endoscopic pyloromyotomy, emerging treatment option
description: A condition where the stomach takes too long to empty its contents due to impaired muscle contractions, causing nausea, vomiting, and bloating, most often associated with diabetes.

name: Lyme Disease
signs:
Erythema migrans (bull's-eye rash)
Fever, chills, fatigue
Arthralgia and myalgia
Headache
Lyme arthritis (large joints, especially knee)
Facial palsy (early disseminated)
Heart block (Lyme carditis)
Radiculopathy
treatment:
1. Early localized: oral doxycycline x10-21 days
2. Early disseminated (carditis, neuro): IV ceftriaxone x14-28 days
3. Lyme arthritis: oral doxycycline x28 days
4. Facial palsy without meningitis: oral doxycycline
5. Prevention: tick checks, DEET, permethrin-treated clothing
meds:
Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x10-21 days (first-line)
Amoxicillin 500mg PO TID x14-21 days (pregnancy, children <8)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily x14-28 days (neuro/cardiac)
Cefuroxime axetil 500mg PO BID x14-21 days
NSAIDs for arthritis pain
ddx: Viral exanthem, Cellulitis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Reactive arthritis, Bell's palsy, MS, Fibromyalgia, Rocky Mountain spotted fever
labs:
Two-tier testing: ELISA then Western blot confirmation
CBC, CMP
ECG (heart block in carditis)
CSF analysis if CNS involvement (lymphocytic pleocytosis, intrathecal antibody)
Joint aspiration if arthritis (inflammatory fluid, PCR)
PCR of skin biopsy or synovial fluid
notes:
Ixodes tick must be attached >36-48h to transmit
Erythema migrans is pathognomonic - treat clinically without waiting for serology
Serology may be negative in first 2 weeks (seroconversion lag)
Post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome: persistent symptoms, no evidence for prolonged antibiotics
Endemic areas: Northeast and upper Midwest US
Prophylaxis: single dose doxycycline 200mg within 72h of tick removal in endemic areas
description: A tick-borne bacterial infection caused by Borrelia burgdorferi that can affect the skin, joints, heart, and nervous system if not treated early with antibiotics.

name: Herpes Zoster
signs:
Painful vesicular rash in dermatomal distribution
Prodromal pain/tingling before rash
Unilateral, does not cross midline
Grouped vesicles on erythematous base
Allodynia
Hutchinson sign (nose tip = V1, risk of eye involvement)
Ramsay Hunt syndrome (ear + facial palsy)
treatment:
1. Antivirals within 72h of rash onset (or if new lesions still forming)
2. Pain management (multimodal)
3. Ophthalmology referral if V1 involvement
4. Postherpetic neuralgia prevention and treatment
5. Vaccination (Shingrix) for prevention in adults >=50
meds:
Valacyclovir 1g PO TID x7 days (preferred)
Acyclovir 800mg PO 5x/day x7 days
Famciclovir 500mg PO TID x7 days
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (PHN)
Pregabalin 75-150mg BID (PHN)
Lidocaine 5% patch (PHN)
Capsaicin 8% patch (PHN)
Preднисone taper (debated, may reduce acute pain)
ddx: HSV, Contact dermatitis, Cellulitis, Dermatitis herpetiformis, Insect bites, Vasculitis, Drug eruption
labs:
Clinical diagnosis usually sufficient
Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells - not specific)
Direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) - VZV specific
VZV PCR (most sensitive)
Viral culture
notes:
Reactivation of latent VZV in dorsal root ganglia
PHN risk increases with age (30% of patients >60)
Disseminated zoster (>20 lesions outside primary dermatome): think immunocompromised
Shingrix vaccine: 2 doses, >90% effective, recommended age >=50 or immunocompromised >=19
HZO (herpes zoster ophthalmicus): V1 dermatome, can cause keratitis, uveitis, vision loss
Contagious to those without varicella immunity (causes chickenpox, not zoster)
description: A painful blistering skin rash caused by reactivation of the chickenpox virus (varicella-zoster) in a nerve, typically occurring in a single strip on one side of the body.

name: COVID-19
signs:
Fever and cough
Dyspnea
Fatigue
Myalgia
Anosmia/ageusia
Sore throat
Diarrhea
Hypoxemia (often silent)
Ground-glass opacities on CT
treatment:
1. Mild: supportive care, monitor for progression
2. Moderate-severe: remdesivir, dexamethasone if hypoxic
3. Critical: mechanical ventilation, prone positioning
4. Early outpatient: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) within 5 days
5. Anticoagulation for hospitalized patients
6. Vaccination for prevention
meds:
Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) 300/100mg BID x5 days (outpatient, high-risk)
Dexamethasone 6mg daily x10 days (if requiring O2)
Remdesivir 200mg IV day 1, then 100mg daily x5 days
Baricitinib 4mg daily (with remdesivir or dexamethasone)
Tocilizumab 8mg/kg IV x1 (progressive disease)
Enoxaparin prophylactic dosing
ddx: Influenza, RSV, Bacterial pneumonia, PE, CHF exacerbation, Other viral pneumonia
labs:
SARS-CoV-2 PCR (nasopharyngeal swab)
Rapid antigen test
CXR (bilateral infiltrates)
CT chest (ground-glass opacities, crazy-paving)
CBC (lymphopenia)
D-dimer, ferritin, CRP, LDH (prognostic markers)
ProCalcitonin (bacterial co-infection)
notes:
Risk factors for severe disease: age >65, obesity, diabetes, heart disease, immunocompromised
Cytokine storm: marked inflammatory response, often day 7-10
Paxlovid interactions: check CYP3A4 drug interactions (ritonavir component)
Long COVID: persistent symptoms >4 weeks post-infection
Vaccination remains most effective preventive measure
Viral rebound possible after Paxlovid treatment
description: A respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus, ranging from mild cold-like symptoms to severe pneumonia and multi-organ failure.

name: Influenza
signs:
Sudden onset fever and chills
Myalgia and body aches
Headache
Dry cough
Sore throat
Fatigue
Rhinorrhea
Nausea/vomiting (more common in children)
treatment:
1. Antivirals within 48h of symptom onset
2. Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics)
3. Hospitalize if severe or high-risk
4. Annual vaccination for prevention
5. Monitor for secondary bacterial pneumonia
meds:
Oseltamivir 75mg PO BID x5 days
Baloxavir marboxil 40-80mg PO x1 dose
Peramivir 600mg IV x1 dose
Acetaminophen 1g q6h or Ibuprofen 400mg q6h PRN
Avoid aspirin in children (Reye syndrome)
ddx: COVID-19, RSV, Common cold, Bacterial pneumonia, Strep pharyngitis, Mononucleosis
labs:
Rapid influenza diagnostic test (RIDT)
Influenza PCR (most sensitive)
CXR if pneumonia suspected
CBC
Metabolic panel
ProCalcitonin (distinguish viral vs bacterial)
notes:
Types A (pandemic potential) and B (seasonal)
High-risk groups: age >65, <5, pregnant, immunocompromised, chronic diseases
Complications: pneumonia (primary viral or secondary bacterial), myocarditis, encephalitis, rhabdomyolysis
Treat within 48h for best benefit, but still treat hospitalized patients regardless of timing
Annual vaccination: reformulated each year for circulating strains
description: A contagious respiratory viral infection causing fever, body aches, and respiratory symptoms, with potential for serious complications especially in high-risk populations.

name: Toxic Shock Syndrome
signs:
High fever (>=102F)
Diffuse macular erythroderma (sunburn-like rash)
Hypotension (SBP <90)
Desquamation 1-2 weeks later (palms and soles)
Multi-organ involvement: vomiting/diarrhea, myalgia, confusion, renal failure
Strawberry tongue
Conjunctival hyperemia
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
2. Remove source (tampon, wound packing)
3. IV antibiotics: clindamycin + vancomycin or nafcillin
4. Vasopressors if refractory hypotension
5. IVIG for refractory cases
6. Surgical debridement of wound if source
meds:
Clindamycin 900mg IV q8h (inhibits toxin production)
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (if MSSA confirmed)
Normal saline boluses (often need 10-20L/day)
Norepinephrine if vasopressors needed
IVIG 1g/kg day 1, then 0.5g/kg days 2-3
ddx: Meningococcemia, Necrotizing fasciitis, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Drug reaction (DRESS, SJS), Kawasaki disease, Scarlet fever, Rocky Mountain spotted fever
labs:
Blood cultures (often negative in staph TSS)
Wound cultures
CBC (leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia)
BMP (elevated creatinine)
CPK (elevated)
LFTs (elevated bilirubin, transaminases)
Coagulation studies
Urinalysis (sterile pyuria)
notes:
Staphylococcal TSS: S. aureus TSST-1 toxin, associated with tampon use, wound packing, nasal packing
Streptococcal TSS: Group A Strep, usually from soft tissue infection, higher mortality
Clindamycin is critical: suppresses toxin production at ribosomal level
Staph TSS diagnostic criteria: fever, rash, hypotension, >=3 organ systems, desquamation
Recurrence risk 30-40% in menstrual TSS - advise against tampon use
description: A rare, life-threatening condition caused by bacterial toxins (usually Staphylococcus or Streptococcus) that triggers a massive inflammatory response with fever, rash, and dangerously low blood pressure.

name: Syphilis
signs:
Primary: painless chancre (firm, round ulcer)
Secondary: diffuse maculopapular rash (including palms/soles), condylomata lata, mucous patches, lymphadenopathy
Latent: asymptomatic
Tertiary: gummas, aortitis, tabes dorsalis
Neurosyphilis: headache, confusion, Argyll Robertson pupils
treatment:
1. Primary/secondary/early latent (<1yr): single IM benzathine penicillin G
2. Late latent/unknown duration: 3 weekly IM benzathine penicillin G doses
3. Neurosyphilis: IV aqueous penicillin G x10-14 days
4. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction: NSAIDs (warn patient)
5. Screen and treat sexual partners
6. Test for HIV (co-infection common)
meds:
Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM x1 (primary/secondary)
Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM weekly x3 (late latent/tertiary)
Aqueous penicillin G 3-4 million units IV q4h x10-14 days (neurosyphilis)
Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x14-28 days (penicillin allergy, non-pregnant)
Ceftriaxone 1-2g IM/IV daily x10-14 days (alternative)
ddx: HSV, Chancroid, Lymphogranuloma venereum, Drug eruption, Pityriasis rosea, Psoriasis, HIV acute retroviral syndrome
labs:
Non-treponemal: RPR or VDRL (screening, follow quantitative titers)
Treponemal: FTA-ABS or TP-PA (confirmatory, remains positive for life)
Reverse sequence screening: treponemal test first, then non-treponemal
Darkfield microscopy (chancre)
CSF analysis if neurosyphilis suspected (VDRL, protein, cell count)
HIV testing
notes:
Painless chancre distinguishes from herpes (painful) and chancroid (painful, purulent)
Secondary syphilis rash on palms and soles is classic - few diseases do this (also RMSF, endocarditis)
Jarisch-Herxheimer: fever, headache, myalgia within 24h of treatment (spirochete lysis)
Argyll Robertson pupils: accommodate but don't react to light (prostitute's pupil)
Congenital syphilis: screen all pregnant women, treat to prevent transmission
4-fold decline in RPR titer = adequate treatment response
description: A sexually transmitted bacterial infection caused by Treponema pallidum that progresses through distinct stages, from painless sores to widespread rash to potentially serious heart and brain complications if untreated.

name: Pneumocystis Pneumonia
signs:
Progressive dyspnea over days to weeks
Dry nonproductive cough
Fever
Hypoxemia (especially with exertion)
Tachypnea
Clear lung exam initially
Oral thrush often present
treatment:
1. TMP-SMX is first-line treatment
2. Adjunctive corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >35
3. Alternative agents if sulfa allergy
4. Supplemental oxygen
5. Prophylaxis when CD4 <200 in HIV patients
meds:
TMP-SMX 15-20mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided TID x21 days
Prednisone 40mg BID x5d, then 40mg daily x5d, then 20mg daily x11d (if hypoxic)
Pentamidine 4mg/kg IV daily (alternative)
Atovaquone 750mg PO BID (mild disease alternative)
Dapsone 100mg + TMP 15mg/kg daily
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h + primaquine 30mg daily
ddx: Bacterial pneumonia, TB, CMV pneumonitis, Kaposi sarcoma, Lymphoma, Fungal pneumonia, COVID-19
labs:
CXR (bilateral diffuse interstitial/GGO infiltrates)
CT chest (ground-glass opacities)
ABG (hypoxemia, elevated A-a gradient)
LDH elevated (>500 suggests PJP)
Induced sputum or BAL with DFA or PCR for Pneumocystis
CD4 count
Beta-glucan (elevated)
HIV viral load
notes:
Classic presentation in HIV with CD4 <200
Subacute onset over weeks (unlike bacterial pneumonia)
LDH correlates with disease severity and prognosis
Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS daily or dapsone 100mg daily when CD4 <200
Non-HIV immunocompromised: more acute/fulminant presentation
Pneumocystis jirovecii (previously called P. carinii - name changed but PCP abbreviation kept)
description: A serious fungal lung infection caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii that primarily affects people with weakened immune systems, especially those with HIV/AIDS and very low CD4 counts.

name: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
signs:
Fever
Headache (severe)
Rash: starts on wrists/ankles then spreads centrally (including palms/soles)
Myalgia
Nausea/vomiting
Abdominal pain
Confusion
Petechiae (late, indicates severe disease)
Edema
treatment:
1. Start doxycycline immediately based on clinical suspicion
2. Do NOT wait for confirmatory testing
3. Supportive care: IV fluids, monitor for complications
4. ICU admission for severe cases
5. Doxycycline is drug of choice for ALL ages including children
meds:
Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV BID until afebrile x3 days (minimum 5-7 days)
Doxycycline in children: 2.2mg/kg BID (do NOT withhold due to age)
Chloramphenicol (only if true doxycycline allergy - inferior outcomes)
ddx: Meningococcemia, Ehrlichiosis, Lyme disease, Measles, Drug eruption, Toxic shock syndrome, Endocarditis, Secondary syphilis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis - treat empirically (don't wait for results)
Rickettsia rickettsii IgG/IgM (acute and convalescent, 2-3 week delay)
Skin biopsy with immunohistochemistry
CBC (thrombocytopenia, leukopenia or leukocytosis)
BMP (hyponatremia, elevated creatinine)
LFTs (elevated transaminases)
Coagulation studies (DIC possible)
notes:
Most lethal tick-borne illness in US - mortality 20-25% without treatment
Classic triad: fever, headache, rash (but rash absent in 10-15% early on)
Rash centripetal spread: ankles/wrists → trunk (opposite of most exanthems)
Do NOT delay treatment waiting for serology - high mortality with delayed treatment
Doxycycline safe in children for short courses (AAP recommends regardless of age)
Dermacentor tick (American dog tick, Rocky Mountain wood tick) is vector
description: A potentially fatal tick-borne bacterial infection caused by Rickettsia rickettsii that causes fever, severe headache, and a characteristic rash spreading from the extremities inward.

name: Osteomyelitis
signs:
Localized bone pain and tenderness
Fever
Swelling and warmth over affected area
Reduced range of motion
Draining sinus tract (chronic)
Refusal to bear weight (children)
Open fracture or recent surgery (risk factor)
treatment:
1. Obtain bone culture before starting antibiotics if possible
2. Prolonged IV antibiotics (4-6 weeks)
3. Surgical debridement for chronic osteomyelitis or abscess
4. Remove infected hardware if present
5. Wound care and vascular assessment (diabetic foot)
6. Hyperbaric oxygen (adjunctive, limited evidence)
meds:
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (MSSA)
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (MRSA)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (gram-negative coverage)
Ciprofloxacin 750mg PO BID + rifampin 300mg PO BID (oral step-down)
Linezolid 600mg PO BID (MRSA oral option)
Metronidazole 500mg PO TID (anaerobes, diabetic foot)
ddx: Cellulitis, Septic arthritis, Gout, Fracture, Bone tumor (Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma), Charcot arthropathy, Avascular necrosis
labs:
MRI (most sensitive and specific imaging)
Bone biopsy with culture (gold standard)
Blood cultures
CBC, ESR, CRP (ESR most useful for monitoring)
Plain radiographs (changes take 10-14 days to appear)
Bone scan (if MRI contraindicated)
Probe-to-bone test (diabetic foot ulcers - positive = likely osteo)
notes:
Hematogenous: children (metaphysis of long bones), adults (vertebral bodies)
Contiguous: diabetic foot ulcers, decubitus ulcers, post-surgical
S. aureus most common organism overall
Vertebral osteomyelitis: S. aureus, TB (Pott disease), gram-negatives
Diabetic foot: polymicrobial (S. aureus, strep, gram-negatives, anaerobes)
Probe-to-bone positive predictive value ~89% in diabetic foot ulcers
description: A bone infection usually caused by bacteria reaching the bone through the bloodstream, an open wound, or spread from nearby infected tissue, requiring prolonged antibiotic treatment.

name: Infectious Mononucleosis
signs:
Fever
Severe pharyngitis with tonsillar exudates
Cervical lymphadenopathy (posterior chain)
Fatigue (profound, lasting weeks)
Splenomegaly
Hepatomegaly
Palatal petechiae
Maculopapular rash (especially after amoxicillin)
treatment:
1. Supportive care: rest, hydration, analgesics
2. Avoid contact sports for 3-4 weeks (splenic rupture risk)
3. Corticosteroids for airway compromise or severe cases
4. Return to activity guided by resolution of splenomegaly
5. Avoid amoxicillin/ampicillin (causes rash)
meds:
Acetaminophen 1g q6h PRN
Ibuprofen 400mg q6h PRN
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper (severe airway compromise only)
Avoid: amoxicillin, ampicillin (causes morbilliform rash in 70-100%)
No antiviral therapy is effective
ddx: Strep pharyngitis, CMV mononucleosis, Acute HIV, Toxoplasmosis, Lymphoma, Hepatitis, Adenovirus
labs:
Heterophile antibody test (Monospot) - may be negative first week
EBV-specific antibodies: VCA IgM (acute), VCA IgG (past/current), EBNA (past)
CBC with differential: atypical lymphocytes (>10%)
LFTs (elevated transaminases in 80%)
Peripheral smear (Downey cells)
Abdominal US if splenomegaly concern
notes:
EBV causes 90% of mono; CMV accounts for most heterophile-negative cases
Monospot: sensitivity 85% in adults, poor in children <4 and first week
Splenic rupture: rare (0.1-0.5%) but potentially fatal, avoid contact sports 3-4 weeks
Amoxicillin rash: NOT a true penicillin allergy (temporary EBV-mediated)
Complications: splenic rupture, airway obstruction, hepatitis, hemolytic anemia, Guillain-Barré
Prolonged fatigue (weeks to months) is common and does not indicate chronic EBV
description: A common viral infection caused by Epstein-Barr virus, typically affecting teenagers and young adults, causing severe sore throat, swollen lymph nodes, and prolonged fatigue.

name: Hyperthyroidism
signs:
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Heat intolerance and sweating
Tachycardia and palpitations
Tremor (fine)
Anxiety and irritability
Exophthalmos (Graves disease)
Pretibial myxedema (Graves)
Diffuse goiter
Diarrhea
Menstrual irregularities
treatment:
1. Beta-blocker for symptom control
2. Antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred, PTU in first trimester)
3. Radioactive iodine ablation (definitive)
4. Thyroidectomy (large goiter, pregnancy, ophthalmopathy)
5. Thyroid storm: PTU, iodine, propranolol, hydrocortisone, cooling
meds:
Methimazole 10-30mg daily (first-line)
Propylthiouracil 100-150mg TID (first trimester pregnancy only)
Propranolol 20-40mg TID-QID
Atenolol 25-100mg daily
Lugol solution or SSKI (thyroid storm, preoperative)
Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (thyroid storm)
ddx: Anxiety disorder, Pheochromocytoma, Factitious thyrotoxicosis, Toxic adenoma, Subacute thyroiditis, Drug-induced (amiodarone, lithium)
labs:
TSH suppressed (<0.1)
Free T4 and T3 elevated
TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) - Graves disease
Thyroid uptake and scan (diffuse uptake = Graves, focal = toxic adenoma, low = thyroiditis)
CBC before starting thionamides (baseline)
LFTs
notes:
Graves disease: most common cause, autoimmune (TRAb stimulates TSH receptor)
Methimazole: risk of agranulocytosis (0.3%) - monitor CBC, warn about sore throat/fever
PTU: hepatotoxicity risk, only use in first trimester and thyroid storm
Radioactive iodine: most patients become hypothyroid (expected outcome)
Graves ophthalmopathy: may worsen with RAI - give steroids, consider surgery instead
Subclinical hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH, normal FT4/T3 - monitor vs treat based on age/risk
description: A condition where the thyroid gland produces too much thyroid hormone, speeding up the body's metabolism and causing weight loss, rapid heartbeat, and nervousness.

name: Diabetes Mellitus Type 2
signs:
Often asymptomatic initially
Polyuria and polydipsia
Fatigue
Blurred vision
Slow-healing wounds
Recurrent infections
Acanthosis nigricans
Peripheral neuropathy
Obesity (common association)
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modification: diet, exercise, weight loss
2. Metformin (first-line pharmacotherapy)
3. Add SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA if cardiovascular/renal disease
4. Individualize HbA1c target (usually <7%)
5. Screen for complications annually: eyes, feet, kidneys
6. Cardiovascular risk reduction: statin, BP control
meds:
Metformin 500mg BID titrate to 1000mg BID
Empagliflozin 10-25mg daily (SGLT2i)
Semaglutide 0.25-1mg SC weekly (GLP-1 RA)
Insulin glargine 10u SC at bedtime (titrate)
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily
Atorvastatin 40mg daily
Aspirin 81mg daily (if cardiovascular risk)
ddx: Type 1 diabetes, LADA, Steroid-induced diabetes, Cushing syndrome, Acromegaly, Pheochromocytoma, Pancreatic disease
labs:
HbA1c >=6.5% (or FPG >=126, or 2h OGTT >=200, or random glucose >=200 with symptoms)
Fasting lipid panel
BMP (creatinine, eGFR)
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR)
HbA1c q3-6 months
Dilated eye exam annually
Foot exam annually
LFTs
notes:
ADA screening: all adults >=35, or BMI >=25 with risk factors
Cardiovascular benefit: SGLT2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) and GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide)
Renal benefit: SGLT2i and finerenone
Hypoglycemia risk: insulin, sulfonylureas (avoid in elderly)
Metformin: hold if eGFR <30, hold before iodinated contrast
Sick day rules: hold metformin, SGLT2i during acute illness
description: A chronic metabolic disorder where the body becomes resistant to insulin or doesn't produce enough, leading to elevated blood sugar levels and long-term complications affecting the eyes, kidneys, nerves, and heart.

name: Cushing's Syndrome
signs:
Central obesity with thin extremities
Moon facies
Dorsocervical fat pad (buffalo hump)
Wide purple striae
Easy bruising
Proximal muscle weakness
Hirsutism
Hypertension
Hyperglycemia
Osteoporosis
Mood changes
treatment:
1. Identify and treat cause (iatrogenic most common - taper steroids)
2. Pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease): transsphenoidal surgery
3. Adrenal adenoma/carcinoma: adrenalectomy
4. Ectopic ACTH: treat underlying tumor
5. Medical therapy while awaiting surgery or if inoperable
6. Bilateral adrenalectomy (last resort)
meds:
Ketoconazole 200-400mg BID (steroidogenesis inhibitor)
Metyrapone 250-750mg TID
Mifepristone 300-1200mg daily (glucocorticoid receptor antagonist)
Osilodrostat 2-7mg BID
Pasireotide 0.6-0.9mg SC BID (pituitary)
Cabergoline 1-3mg weekly (pituitary)
ddx: Metabolic syndrome, PCOS, Alcoholism (pseudo-Cushing), Depression, Exogenous glucocorticoid use, Simple obesity
labs:
24h urine free cortisol (elevated)
Late-night salivary cortisol (elevated)
1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol >1.8 = positive)
ACTH level: low = adrenal, high = pituitary or ectopic
High-dose dex suppression: suppresses = pituitary (Cushing disease)
Pituitary MRI
CT chest/abdomen (ectopic ACTH source)
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (distinguishes pituitary from ectopic)
notes:
Exogenous steroids are #1 cause (iatrogenic) - always ask about steroid use
Cushing disease = pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma (most common endogenous cause, 70%)
Ectopic ACTH: small cell lung cancer, carcinoid, medullary thyroid cancer
Nelson syndrome: aggressive pituitary adenoma growth after bilateral adrenalectomy
>=2 positive screening tests needed before further workup
Pseudo-Cushing: alcoholism, depression, obesity - distinguished by CRH stimulation test
description: A hormonal disorder caused by prolonged exposure to high levels of cortisol, either from the body's overproduction or from taking corticosteroid medications, causing characteristic weight gain and metabolic changes.

name: Chronic Kidney Disease
signs:
Often asymptomatic until advanced
Fatigue
Edema (periorbital, peripheral)
Hypertension
Nausea/anorexia (uremia)
Pruritus
Restless legs
Nocturia
Foamy urine (proteinuria)
Metallic taste
Pericardial friction rub (uremic pericarditis)
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (diabetes, hypertension)
2. ACEi/ARB for proteinuria and BP control
3. SGLT2 inhibitor (CKD with proteinuria)
4. Sodium and potassium restriction
5. Phosphate binders and vitamin D
6. EPO for anemia (Hgb <10)
7. Dialysis or transplant for ESRD
8. Avoid nephrotoxins
meds:
Lisinopril 5-40mg daily (proteinuria reduction)
Dapagliflozin 10mg daily (slows CKD progression)
Finerenone 10-20mg daily (diabetic CKD)
Sevelamer 800mg TID with meals (phosphate binder)
Calcitriol 0.25mcg daily
Epoetin alfa 50-300 units/kg SC 3x/week
Sodium bicarbonate 650mg TID (metabolic acidosis)
Furosemide 40-160mg daily (volume overload)
ddx: Acute kidney injury, Prerenal azotemia (dehydration), Nephrotic syndrome, Urinary obstruction, Renal artery stenosis
labs:
BMP (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarb)
eGFR (staging: G1 >90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15)
UACR (albumin-to-creatinine ratio)
CBC (anemia)
Calcium, phosphorus, PTH, vitamin D
Renal ultrasound
Lipid panel
HbA1c (diabetics)
Hep B/C, HIV (pre-transplant)
notes:
Leading causes: diabetes (40%), hypertension (25%), glomerulonephritis (10%)
ACEi/ARB: may increase creatinine up to 30% initially (acceptable, don't stop)
SGLT2i: may also cause initial eGFR dip (hemodynamic, not harmful)
Refer to nephrology at eGFR <30 or rapidly declining
AVF (arteriovenous fistula) creation when eGFR <20 (needs 2-3 months to mature)
Avoid: NSAIDs, contrast dye (if possible), aminoglycosides, excessive potassium
description: A gradual loss of kidney function over months to years, most commonly caused by diabetes and high blood pressure, that can progress to kidney failure requiring dialysis or transplantation.

name: Glomerulonephritis
signs:
Hematuria (cola/tea-colored urine)
Proteinuria
Hypertension
Edema (periorbital, lower extremity)
Oliguria
Flank pain
Fever (post-infectious)
Skin rash or joint pain (systemic disease)
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause
2. Blood pressure control (ACEi/ARB preferred)
3. Immunosuppression for autoimmune causes
4. Sodium and fluid restriction
5. Diuretics for edema
6. Dialysis if severe AKI
7. Plasmapheresis for anti-GBM, ANCA vasculitis
meds:
Lisinopril 5-40mg daily
Furosemide 40-80mg IV/PO
Methylprednisolone 1g IV x3 days then prednisone taper
Cyclophosphamide 2mg/kg daily or IV pulse (ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM)
Rituximab 375mg/m2 weekly x4 (ANCA vasculitis)
Mycophenolate 1-1.5g BID (lupus nephritis)
ddx: IgA nephropathy, Post-streptococcal GN, Lupus nephritis, ANCA-associated vasculitis, Anti-GBM disease, Membranoproliferative GN, Alport syndrome
labs:
Urinalysis (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts = pathognomonic)
Urine protein quantification
BMP (elevated creatinine)
Complement levels (C3, C4)
ANA, anti-dsDNA (lupus)
c-ANCA/p-ANCA
Anti-GBM antibodies
ASO titer (post-strep)
Hepatitis B/C serologies
Renal biopsy (definitive diagnosis)
Renal ultrasound
notes:
Nephritic syndrome: hematuria, HTN, oliguria, mild proteinuria (vs nephrotic: massive proteinuria)
Low C3: post-strep GN, MPGN, lupus, endocarditis, shunt nephritis
Normal C3: IgA nephropathy, ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM
Post-streptococcal GN: 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis, self-limited in children
IgA nephropathy: most common GN worldwide, synpharyngitic hematuria
RPGN (crescentic): medical emergency - anti-GBM, ANCA, immune complex
description: Inflammation of the tiny filters in the kidneys (glomeruli) that can cause blood and protein in the urine, high blood pressure, and kidney damage from various immune-mediated causes.

name: Renal Calculi
signs:
Severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin
Hematuria (microscopic or gross)
Nausea/vomiting
Urinary urgency and frequency
Restlessness (cannot find comfortable position)
CVA tenderness
Fever (if concurrent infection = emergency)
treatment:
1. Pain control: NSAIDs first-line (ketorolac), opioids if needed
2. Medical expulsive therapy: tamsulosin for stones 5-10mm
3. IVF hydration
4. Strain urine for stone analysis
5. Ureteroscopy or lithotripsy for stones >10mm or refractory
6. PCNL for large or staghorn stones
7. Urgent urology if fever + obstruction (obstructive pyelonephritis)
meds:
Ketorolac 30mg IV or 10mg PO q6h
Ibuprofen 600mg PO q8h
Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (medical expulsive therapy)
Ondansetron 4mg IV/PO PRN
Oxycodone 5mg PO q4h PRN (breakthrough)
HCTZ 25mg daily + potassium citrate (calcium stone prevention)
Allopurinol 100-300mg daily (uric acid stones)
ddx: Pyelonephritis, Appendicitis, Ovarian torsion, Ectopic pregnancy, AAA, Musculoskeletal pain, Renal cell carcinoma
labs:
CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (gold standard - 95% sensitivity)
Urinalysis (hematuria, crystals, pH)
BMP (creatinine)
CBC (leukocytosis if infected)
Uric acid level
Parathyroid hormone and calcium (hyperparathyroidism)
24h urine collection (after first stone for metabolic evaluation)
Stone analysis when passed
notes:
Types: calcium oxalate (80%), calcium phosphate, uric acid (radiolucent on XR), struvite (infection/staghorn), cystine
Stones <5mm: 90% pass spontaneously. 5-10mm: 50%. >10mm: usually need intervention
Fever + obstructing stone = urological emergency (urgent decompression: stent or nephrostomy)
Prevention: increase fluid intake (>2.5L/day), low sodium, normal calcium diet
Struvite stones: urease-producing bacteria (Proteus, Klebsiella) - require complete surgical removal
Cystine stones: autosomal recessive, hexagonal crystals, alkalinize urine
description: Hard mineral deposits that form in the kidneys and can cause excruciating pain as they travel through the urinary tract, commonly known as kidney stones.

name: Pyelonephritis
signs:
Fever and chills
Flank pain and CVA tenderness
Nausea/vomiting
Dysuria and urinary frequency
Malaise
Abdominal pain
May present as sepsis
treatment:
1. Outpatient: oral fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX if uncomplicated
2. Inpatient: IV antibiotics for severe, complicated, or vomiting
3. Blood cultures if septic
4. CT if no improvement in 48-72h (rule out abscess)
5. Urologic consultation if obstruction
meds:
Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID x7 days (outpatient)
TMP-SMX DS PO BID x14 days (outpatient)
Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily (inpatient)
Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h (severe)
Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV daily + ampicillin 2g IV q4h
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
ddx: Lower UTI (cystitis), Renal calculi, Appendicitis, Diverticulitis, PID, Musculoskeletal pain, Renal abscess, Renal infarction
labs:
Urinalysis with culture (pyuria, bacteriuria, WBC casts)
Blood cultures x2
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP
Lactate if sepsis concern
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (if complicated or no improvement at 48-72h)
Renal ultrasound (obstruction)
notes:
E. coli causes 75-95% of uncomplicated cases
Complicated: male, structural abnormality, obstruction, pregnancy, immunocompromised, catheter
Pregnancy: hospitalize, IV ceftriaxone - high risk for preterm labor
Perinephric abscess: suspect if no improvement after 72h antibiotics
Emphysematous pyelonephritis: gas-forming infection in diabetics, often needs nephrectomy
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis: chronic, mimics renal cell carcinoma
description: A bacterial kidney infection that causes flank pain, fever, and urinary symptoms, requiring antibiotic treatment to prevent serious complications like sepsis or kidney damage.

name: Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear
signs:
Acute knee pain and swelling
Audible pop at time of injury
Knee instability (giving way)
Positive Lachman test
Positive anterior drawer test
Positive pivot shift test
Hemarthrosis
Reduced range of motion
treatment:
1. RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) acutely
2. Knee immobilizer and crutches
3. Physical therapy for pre-habilitation
4. ACL reconstruction (young, active patients)
5. Non-operative management (older, less active)
6. Address concurrent meniscal/ligamentous injuries
meds:
NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600mg TID)
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Opioids short-term PRN
Aspiration of hemarthrosis for comfort
DVT prophylaxis post-surgery
ddx: Meniscal tear, PCL tear, MCL/LCL tear, Patellar dislocation, Tibial plateau fracture, Quadriceps/patellar tendon rupture
labs:
MRI knee (gold standard - 95% sensitivity)
XR knee (rule out fracture, Segond fracture pathognomonic for ACL tear)
Physical exam under anesthesia
Arthroscopy (diagnostic and therapeutic)
notes:
Mechanism: non-contact pivoting injury, deceleration, hyperextension
Associated injuries: meniscal tears (50%), MCL sprain, bone bruises
Unhappy triad: ACL + MCL + medial meniscus (or lateral meniscus)
ACL reconstruction: autograft (patellar tendon, hamstring, quadriceps) or allograft
Return to sport: typically 9-12 months post-reconstruction
Female athletes: 2-8x higher ACL tear risk than males
description: A tear of the anterior cruciate ligament in the knee, one of the major stabilizing ligaments, commonly occurring during sports with sudden stops, direction changes, or landing from jumps.

name: Rotator Cuff Tear
signs:
Shoulder pain (worse at night, lying on affected side)
Weakness with overhead activities
Positive drop arm test (supraspinatus)
Positive external rotation lag sign (infraspinatus)
Positive lift-off test (subscapularis)
Painful arc (60-120 degrees)
Atrophy of supraspinatus/infraspinatus
treatment:
1. Conservative first for partial tears: PT, NSAIDs, subacromial injection
2. Physical therapy: rotator cuff strengthening, scapular stabilization
3. Surgical repair for: acute traumatic tears, failed conservative management
4. Arthroscopic vs open repair
5. Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty for massive irreparable tears
meds:
Ibuprofen 600mg TID or naproxen 500mg BID
Subacromial corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40mg + lidocaine)
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Topical diclofenac gel
Limit steroid injections to 3 per year (tendon weakening)
ddx: Impingement syndrome, Adhesive capsulitis, Biceps tendinopathy, AC joint arthritis, Cervical radiculopathy, Calcific tendinitis, Labral tear
labs:
MRI shoulder (gold standard for soft tissue)
Ultrasound (operator-dependent, good for supraspinatus)
XR shoulder (rule out fracture, calcification, AC arthritis)
Arthrogram (if MRI inconclusive)
notes:
Supraspinatus most commonly torn (90%)
