Massive tear: >=2 tendons or >5cm retraction
Degenerative tears: age >40, gradual onset, may be asymptomatic
Acute traumatic: younger patients, specific injury event
Infrastructure tear: superior capsule reconstruction or reverse TSA
Return to activity after repair: 4-6 months (strengthening), full activity 6-12 months
description: A tear in one or more of the four tendons that stabilize the shoulder joint, causing pain and weakness, especially with overhead arm movements.

name: Lumbar Disc Herniation
signs:
Low back pain radiating to leg (sciatica)
Dermatomal pain distribution
Positive straight leg raise test
Numbness/tingling in affected dermatome
Weakness (foot drop if L4-L5)
Decreased reflexes (ankle jerk L5-S1)
Worse with sitting, bending, coughing
treatment:
1. Conservative management first (90% improve in 6 weeks)
2. Activity modification (avoid bed rest >2 days)
3. Physical therapy (core strengthening, McKenzie exercises)
4. Epidural steroid injection for refractory pain
5. Surgery (microdiscectomy) for: progressive neurologic deficit, cauda equina, failed 6-12 weeks conservative
6. Urgent surgery for cauda equina syndrome
meds:
NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600mg TID, naproxen 500mg BID)
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine 10mg TID short-term)
Oral prednisone taper (debated)
Epidural steroid injection (methylprednisolone 80mg)
ddx: Spinal stenosis, Piriformis syndrome, Sacroiliac joint dysfunction, Hip pathology, Peripheral neuropathy, Cauda equina syndrome, Spinal tumor, Epidural abscess
labs:
MRI lumbar spine (gold standard)
XR lumbar spine (rule out fracture, spondylolisthesis)
EMG/NCV (if diagnosis unclear, >6 weeks)
CT myelography (if MRI contraindicated)
notes:
L4-L5 (L5 root) and L5-S1 (S1 root) most common levels
L5 radiculopathy: dorsiflexion weakness, numbness lateral leg/dorsum foot
S1 radiculopathy: plantarflexion weakness, decreased ankle reflex, numbness lateral foot
Cauda equina red flags: bilateral leg symptoms, saddle anesthesia, urinary retention/incontinence, fecal incontinence → emergent MRI and surgery
Crossed straight leg raise more specific than ipsilateral
Most disc herniations resolve with conservative management
description: A condition where the soft center of a spinal disc pushes through a crack in the outer casing, pressing on nearby nerves and causing pain, numbness, or weakness radiating down the leg.

name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
signs:
Numbness/tingling in thumb, index, middle finger (median nerve)
Pain worse at night
Positive Tinel sign (tapping over carpal tunnel)
Positive Phalen test (wrist flexion 60s)
Thenar atrophy (advanced)
Decreased grip strength
Worse with repetitive hand activities
treatment:
1. Conservative: wrist splinting at night (neutral position)
2. Activity modification, ergonomic adjustments
3. Corticosteroid injection (temporary relief)
4. Surgical release if severe or failed conservative management
5. Endoscopic vs open carpal tunnel release
meds:
Wrist splint at night (neutral position)
NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400mg TID)
Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40mg into carpal tunnel)
Vitamin B6 (limited evidence)
Gabapentin 300mg TID (neuropathic pain if needed)
ddx: Cervical radiculopathy (C6-C7), Pronator syndrome, Thoracic outlet syndrome, Ulnar neuropathy, Peripheral neuropathy, De Quervain tenosynovitis, Raynaud phenomenon
labs:
Nerve conduction studies/EMG (confirm diagnosis, severity)
Clinical diagnosis often sufficient
XR wrist (rule out bony causes)
Ultrasound (median nerve swelling)
Blood glucose, TSH, RF (underlying causes)
notes:
Most common entrapment neuropathy
Median nerve: thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring finger
Risk factors: repetitive wrist motion, pregnancy, hypothyroidism, diabetes, RA, obesity
EMG/NCV: slowed sensory latency across wrist most sensitive finding
Surgical release: >90% success rate for symptom relief
Pregnancy-related CTS usually resolves postpartum
Thenar atrophy indicates severe, longstanding compression
description: Compression of the median nerve as it passes through the narrow carpal tunnel in the wrist, causing numbness, tingling, and weakness in the hand and fingers.

name: Osteoporosis
signs:
Often asymptomatic until fracture occurs
Loss of height
Kyphosis (dowager's hump)
Fragility fractures (vertebral compression, hip, wrist)
Back pain (vertebral fractures)
Dental problems
treatment:
1. Calcium 1200mg + Vitamin D 800-1000 IU daily
2. Weight-bearing exercise
3. Fall prevention
4. Bisphosphonate therapy (first-line)
5. Anabolic therapy for severe osteoporosis (teriparatide, romosozumab)
6. Treat secondary causes
meds:
Alendronate 70mg PO weekly
Risedronate 35mg PO weekly
Zoledronic acid 5mg IV annually
Denosumab 60mg SC q6 months
Teriparatide 20mcg SC daily (anabolic, max 2 years)
Romosozumab 210mg SC monthly x12 months
Calcium carbonate 600mg BID + Vitamin D3 1000IU daily
ddx: Osteomalacia, Multiple myeloma, Metastatic bone disease, Hyperparathyroidism, Paget disease, Renal osteodystrophy
labs:
DEXA scan (T-score: >-1 normal, -1 to -2.5 osteopenia, <-2.5 osteoporosis)
CBC, BMP, calcium, phosphorus
Vitamin D level (25-OH)
TSH
PTH (if calcium abnormal)
24h urine calcium
SPEP (rule out myeloma)
Celiac serologies if suspect malabsorption
Testosterone (men)
FRAX score (10-year fracture risk)
notes:
DEXA screening: women >=65, men >=70, or earlier with risk factors
FRAX: 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture - treat if >=20% or hip fracture risk >=3%
Bisphosphonate holiday after 5 years oral or 3 years IV (reassess risk)
Atypical femur fractures: associated with prolonged bisphosphonate use (>5 years)
Osteonecrosis of jaw: rare, screen dental health before starting
Denosumab: rebound vertebral fractures if discontinued - must transition to bisphosphonate
Secondary causes: glucocorticoids, hypogonadism, thyroid excess, malabsorption, anticonvulsants
description: A bone disease where decreased bone density and quality increases the risk of fractures, often called a 'silent disease' because bone loss occurs without symptoms until a fracture happens.

name: Opioid Overdose
signs:
Respiratory depression (rate <12)
Pinpoint pupils (miosis)
Altered consciousness/unresponsive
Hypotension
Bradycardia
Cyanosis
Pulmonary edema
Needle marks (IV drug use)
Hypothermia
treatment:
1. Airway management (BVM ventilation)
2. Naloxone (Narcan) - opioid antagonist
3. Repeat naloxone q2-3min as needed
4. Intubation if no response to naloxone
5. Observe 4-6h minimum (longer for long-acting opioids)
6. Naloxone infusion for recurrent sedation
7. Substance use treatment referral
meds:
Naloxone 0.04-0.4mg IV/IM/IN initial (titrate to respiratory effort, not consciousness)
Naloxone 2mg IN (intranasal, prehospital)
Repeat naloxone q2-3min (max 10mg)
Naloxone infusion: 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour
Atropine 0.5mg IV if severe bradycardia
Fluids for hypotension
ddx: Benzodiazepine overdose, Clonidine overdose, Pontine hemorrhage (pinpoint pupils), Hypoglycemia, Hypothermia, Sepsis, CO poisoning, Other sedative overdose
labs:
Point-of-care glucose
Urine drug screen
ABG/VBG
CBC, BMP
Lactate
CXR (pulmonary edema, aspiration)
ECG (QTc prolongation with methadone)
Acetaminophen and salicylate levels (co-ingestion)
notes:
Start with low naloxone dose (0.04-0.1mg) in opioid-dependent patients to avoid precipitating withdrawal
Fentanyl: may require higher cumulative naloxone doses
Long-acting opioids (methadone, extended-release): observe >=24h, may need naloxone infusion
Recurrence of respiratory depression: naloxone half-life (30-90min) << most opioids
Naloxone take-home: prescribe/dispense to all at-risk patients and families
Post-overdose: offer buprenorphine or methadone for OUD treatment
description: A life-threatening emergency caused by taking too much of an opioid drug, leading to severely slowed breathing that can cause brain damage and death without the antidote naloxone.

name: Traumatic Brain Injury
signs:
Altered consciousness (GCS <15)
Headache
Vomiting
Amnesia
Confusion
Seizures
Focal neurological deficits
Battle sign (mastoid ecchymosis)
Raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis)
CSF rhinorrhea/otorrhea (basilar skull fracture)
Cushing triad: HTN, bradycardia, irregular respirations (elevated ICP)
treatment:
1. ABCDE primary survey, c-spine precautions
2. Maintain MAP >80, CPP >60
3. CT head immediately for moderate-severe
4. ICP monitoring if GCS <=8
5. Surgical evacuation: epidural, large subdural, depressed skull fracture
6. Osmotic therapy for elevated ICP (mannitol or hypertonic saline)
7. Seizure prophylaxis x7 days for severe TBI
meds:
Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV (elevated ICP)
3% hypertonic saline 250mL IV bolus (ICP crisis)
Levetiracetam 500-1000mg IV BID x7 days (seizure prophylaxis)
Fentanyl (analgesia/sedation)
Propofol (sedation, lowers ICP)
Norepinephrine (maintain CPP)
Tranexamic acid 1g IV (within 3h if bleeding)
ddx: Stroke, Intoxication, Postictal state, Hypoglycemia, Meningitis/encephalitis, Non-accidental trauma (children)
labs:
CT head without contrast (immediate)
CT c-spine
CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, type and screen
Ethanol level, urine drug screen
ABG/lactate
Repeat CT if clinical deterioration
MRI brain (subacute evaluation)
notes:
GCS: mild 13-15, moderate 9-12, severe 3-8
Epidural hematoma: lucid interval, biconvex/lens-shaped, middle meningeal artery
Subdural hematoma: crescent-shaped, bridging veins, elderly/anticoagulated
Diffuse axonal injury: shearing forces, coma, petechial hemorrhages at gray-white junction
CPP = MAP - ICP (goal CPP >60mmHg)
Second impact syndrome: catastrophic brain swelling if second concussion before first resolves
Return to activity: graduated protocol after concussion
description: Damage to the brain caused by an external mechanical force such as a blow or jolt to the head, ranging from mild concussion to severe injury with lasting disability or death.

name: Hemorrhagic Shock
signs:
Tachycardia
Hypotension (SBP <90 late sign)
Tachypnea
Altered mental status
Pale, cool, clammy skin
Decreased urine output
Flat neck veins
Narrow pulse pressure
Delayed capillary refill
treatment:
1. Control external hemorrhage (direct pressure, tourniquet)
2. Massive transfusion protocol activation
3. Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) until hemorrhage controlled
4. Balanced resuscitation: 1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets
5. Tranexamic acid within 3h of injury
6. Emergent surgical intervention for source control
7. Resuscitative thoracotomy for penetrating trauma with cardiac arrest
meds:
PRBCs, FFP, platelets (1:1:1 ratio)
Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10min then 1g over 8h
Calcium gluconate 1g IV (prevent citrate toxicity from transfusion)
Norepinephrine only after adequate volume (last resort)
Whole blood (if available)
Cryoprecipitate 10 units (if fibrinogen <150)
ddx: Cardiogenic shock, Tension pneumothorax, Cardiac tamponade, Neurogenic shock, Septic shock, Anaphylactic shock
labs:
Type and crossmatch (or O-negative PRBCs immediately)
CBC, BMP, coagulation studies
Lactate (>4 = severe shock)
Base deficit
Thromboelastography (TEG) or ROTEM
FAST ultrasound (free fluid)
CXR, pelvic XR
ABG
notes:
ATLS Classes: I (<15% blood loss, normal vitals), II (15-30%, tachycardia), III (30-40%, hypotension), IV (>40%, lethal)
Lethal triad: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy (must prevent/correct)
Permissive hypotension: target SBP 80-90 until definitive hemorrhage control (except TBI - maintain SBP >100)
Massive transfusion: >10 units PRBCs in 24h or >4 units in 1h
Damage control surgery: control bleeding and contamination, delay definitive repair
REBOA (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of Aorta): temporizing for non-compressible torso hemorrhage
description: A life-threatening condition where severe blood loss prevents the heart from pumping enough blood to sustain the body's organs, requiring immediate hemorrhage control and blood product transfusion.

name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
signs:
Fatigue and weakness
Easy bruising and bleeding
Petechiae
Recurrent infections
Fever
Bone pain
Splenomegaly
Gum hypertrophy (monocytic subtypes)
DIC (APL)
Leukostasis symptoms if WBC very high
treatment:
1. Induction chemotherapy: 7+3 regimen (cytarabine + daunorubicin)
2. Consolidation: high-dose cytarabine or allogeneic stem cell transplant
3. APL (t(15;17)): ATRA + arsenic trioxide (curative in most)
4. Targeted therapy based on mutations (FLT3, IDH1/2)
5. Supportive care: transfusions, antibiotics, tumor lysis prevention
6. Leukapheresis for symptomatic leukostasis
meds:
Cytarabine 100-200mg/m2 continuous IV x7 days
Daunorubicin 60-90mg/m2 IV days 1-3
ATRA 45mg/m2 daily (APL)
Arsenic trioxide 0.15mg/kg IV daily (APL)
Midostaurin 50mg BID (FLT3+)
Venetoclax 400mg daily (older/unfit patients with azacitidine)
Azacitidine 75mg/m2 SC/IV x7 days (older/unfit)
ddx: ALL, MDS, CML blast crisis, Aplastic anemia, Viral infection (EBV, CMV), Vitamin B12/folate deficiency, Leukemoid reaction
labs:
CBC with differential (blasts, anemia, thrombocytopenia)
Peripheral smear (Auer rods pathognomonic)
Bone marrow biopsy (>=20% blasts diagnostic)
Flow cytometry (immunophenotyping)
Cytogenetics/FISH
Molecular testing: FLT3, NPM1, CEBPA, IDH1/2, TP53
Coagulation studies (DIC in APL)
LDH, uric acid (tumor burden)
HLA typing for potential transplant
notes:
Most common acute leukemia in adults
Auer rods: pathognomonic for AML (especially APL)
APL (PML-RARA): medical emergency - DIC common, treat with ATRA immediately on suspicion
Differentiation syndrome: fever, dyspnea, weight gain, effusions with ATRA (treat with dexamethasone)
Favorable cytogenetics: t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17) - better prognosis
Adverse: complex karyotype, TP53 mutation, FLT3-ITD high ratio
Allogeneic transplant in first remission for intermediate/adverse risk
description: A rapidly progressing cancer of the blood and bone marrow that produces abnormal white blood cells (blasts) that crowd out normal blood cells, requiring urgent chemotherapy.

name: Multiple Myeloma
signs:
Bone pain (especially back)
Pathologic fractures
Fatigue (anemia)
Recurrent infections
Renal insufficiency
Hypercalcemia symptoms (confusion, constipation, polyuria)
Weight loss
Peripheral neuropathy
Amyloidosis features
treatment:
1. Determine transplant eligibility (age, fitness)
2. Induction: triplet therapy (VRd: bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone)
3. Autologous stem cell transplant if eligible
4. Maintenance: lenalidomide
5. Bone protection: zoledronic acid or denosumab
6. Supportive: EPO for anemia, plasmapheresis for hyperviscosity
7. Radiation for bone pain/cord compression
meds:
Bortezomib 1.3mg/m2 SC weekly
Lenalidomide 25mg daily x21/28 days
Dexamethasone 40mg weekly
Zoledronic acid 4mg IV monthly
Daratumumab 16mg/kg IV (added to VRd for quadruplet)
Carfilzomib 56mg/m2 IV (relapsed)
Pomalidomide 4mg daily (relapsed)
Acyclovir 400mg BID (VZV prophylaxis with bortezomib)
ddx: MGUS, Smoldering myeloma, Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, Primary amyloidosis, Metastatic bone disease, Lymphoma, CLL
labs:
SPEP + immunofixation (M-spike)
Serum free light chains (kappa/lambda ratio)
24h urine protein electrophoresis (Bence Jones protein)
CBC (anemia)
BMP (creatinine, calcium elevated)
Beta-2 microglobulin and albumin (ISS staging)
LDH
Bone marrow biopsy (>=10% clonal plasma cells)
Skeletal survey or PET-CT or whole-body MRI
FISH: t(4;14), t(14;16), del(17p)
notes:
CRAB criteria: Calcium elevated, Renal insufficiency, Anemia, Bone lesions
SLiM-CRAB: adds biomarkers for myeloma-defining events (>=60% plasma cells, FLC ratio >=100, >1 focal MRI lesion)
ISS staging: I (B2M <3.5, albumin >=3.5), II (intermediate), III (B2M >=5.5)
Revised ISS adds LDH and high-risk FISH
Bone lesions are lytic (NOT blastic) - bone scan may be falsely negative
Renal failure: light chain cast nephropathy (myeloma kidney)
Hyperviscosity: more common in Waldenstrom than myeloma
description: A cancer of plasma cells in the bone marrow that produces abnormal antibodies, causing bone destruction, kidney damage, anemia, and immune dysfunction.

name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
signs:
Fatigue
Pallor
Dyspnea on exertion
Tachycardia
Pica (craving non-food items like ice)
Koilonychia (spoon nails)
Glossitis
Angular cheilitis
Restless legs
Pageophagia (ice craving)
treatment:
1. Oral iron supplementation (first-line)
2. IV iron if oral intolerant, malabsorption, or severe
3. Identify and treat underlying cause (GI blood loss in adults)
4. Dietary counseling (red meat, legumes, vitamin C enhances absorption)
5. Colonoscopy/EGD for unexplained IDA in adults (especially >40)
meds:
Ferrous sulfate 325mg (65mg elemental iron) PO daily-TID
Take on empty stomach with vitamin C
IV iron sucrose 200mg IV x5 doses
IV ferric carboxymaltose 750mg IV x2 doses (2 weeks apart)
IV iron dextran (test dose first, risk of anaphylaxis)
Avoid: calcium, antacids, tea with iron (decrease absorption)
ddx: Anemia of chronic disease, Thalassemia trait, Sideroblastic anemia, Lead poisoning, GI malignancy, Celiac disease
labs:
CBC: microcytic hypochromic anemia (low MCV, low MCH)
Iron studies: low ferritin (<30), low serum iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation (<20%)
Peripheral smear: microcytes, hypochromia, target cells, pencil cells
Reticulocyte count (low, then rises with treatment)
Stool guaiac/FIT (GI blood loss)
Celiac serologies
H. pylori testing
notes:
Most common nutritional deficiency worldwide
Ferritin <30 is diagnostic; ferritin 30-100 may still be IDA if inflammation present
Men and postmenopausal women: GI evaluation mandatory (colon cancer screening)
Premenopausal women: menorrhagia most common cause
Every-other-day dosing may improve absorption (hepcidin regulation)
Expect reticulocyte response in 1 week, Hgb rise in 2-4 weeks
Continue iron 3-6 months after Hgb normalizes to replete stores
description: The most common type of anemia, caused by insufficient iron to produce adequate hemoglobin, resulting in small, pale red blood cells that cannot carry enough oxygen.

name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
signs:
Chronic low back pain and stiffness (worse in morning, improves with exercise)
Reduced spinal mobility (Schober test)
Sacroiliac joint tenderness
Loss of lumbar lordosis
Chest wall pain (costovertebral involvement)
Uveitis
Enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia)
Aortic regurgitation (late)
treatment:
1. Exercise and physical therapy (cornerstone of management)
2. NSAIDs (first-line pharmacotherapy)
3. TNF inhibitors for NSAID-refractory disease
4. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab)
5. No role for methotrexate in axial disease
6. Surgical: total hip replacement if needed
meds:
Naproxen 500mg BID or Indomethacin 25-50mg TID (continuous, not PRN)
Adalimumab 40mg SC q2 weeks
Etanercept 50mg SC weekly
Secukinumab 150mg SC monthly (after loading)
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV at 0,2,6 weeks then q6-8 weeks
Sulfasalazine 1-1.5g BID (peripheral arthritis only)
ddx: Mechanical low back pain, Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH), Reactive arthritis, Psoriatic arthritis, IBD-associated spondylitis, Osteitis condensans ilii
labs:
Sacroiliac XR (bilateral sacroiliitis - may be normal early)
MRI sacroiliac joints (bone marrow edema - earliest finding)
HLA-B27 (positive in 90%, but not diagnostic alone)
CRP/ESR (may be elevated)
CBC
Spinal XR: bamboo spine (late), squaring of vertebral bodies
Echo if murmur (aortic regurgitation)
notes:
Strong HLA-B27 association (90% of AS patients)
Onset typically 15-30 years, male predominance 3:1
Modified New York criteria: clinical (pain, mobility) + radiographic sacroiliitis
Bamboo spine: complete spinal fusion (late disease, increased fracture risk)
Careful with intubation: cervical spine fusion → difficult airway
Associated conditions: anterior uveitis (40%), IBD, psoriasis, aortic valve disease
Exercise programs shown to maintain function and reduce pain more than any medication
description: A chronic inflammatory arthritis primarily affecting the spine and sacroiliac joints, causing progressive stiffness and potential spinal fusion, strongly associated with the HLA-B27 gene.

name: Giant Cell Arteritis
signs:
New-onset headache (temporal, unilateral)
Scalp tenderness
Jaw claudication
Visual disturbances (amaurosis fugax, diplopia)
Sudden vision loss (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy)
Temporary artery tenderness/nodularity
Fever and malaise
Weight loss
Polymyalgia rheumatica symptoms (50%)
treatment:
1. High-dose prednisone immediately (do NOT wait for biopsy)
2. If vision symptoms: IV methylprednisolone x3 days then oral
3. Temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks (steroids don't affect biopsy for ~2 weeks)
4. Low-dose aspirin
5. Tocilizumab for steroid-sparing
6. Slow steroid taper over 1-2 years
7. Bone protection during steroid therapy
meds:
Prednisone 40-60mg daily (uncomplicated)
Methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3 days (visual symptoms)
Aspirin 81mg daily
Tocilizumab 162mg SC weekly or 6mg/kg IV monthly (steroid-sparing)
Methotrexate 15-25mg weekly (steroid-sparing, modest benefit)
Calcium + vitamin D + bisphosphonate (bone protection)
ddx: Migraine, Tension headache, Trigeminal neuralgia, TMJ disorder, Herpes zoster (V1), Non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, Amyloidosis, Takayasu arteritis
labs:
ESR markedly elevated (often >50-100, mean ~90)
CRP elevated
CBC (normocytic anemia, thrombocytosis)
LFTs (elevated ALP)
Temporal artery biopsy (transmural inflammation with giant cells) - gold standard
Temporal artery ultrasound (halo sign)
CTA/MRA of aorta (large vessel involvement)
notes:
Medical emergency if visual symptoms - permanent blindness if untreated
Start steroids IMMEDIATELY on clinical suspicion - do not delay for biopsy
Biopsy: get contralateral side if first negative (skip lesions, 15-40% false-negative rate)
Almost never occurs before age 50 (mean onset 70s)
PMR overlap: 40-60% of GCA patients have PMR symptoms
Aortic complications: aneurysm (especially thoracic), dissection - screen with imaging
Long-term steroid complications are major concern - use tocilizumab to reduce steroid exposure
description: Inflammation of the blood vessels, particularly the temporal arteries, that can cause severe headache, scalp tenderness, and vision loss, requiring immediate steroid treatment to prevent permanent blindness.

name: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
signs:
Chronic sinusitis/rhinitis
Saddle nose deformity
Otitis media
Cough, hemoptysis
Dyspnea
Glomerulonephritis (hematuria, proteinuria)
Purpura
Peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex)
Ocular inflammation
treatment:
1. Severe/organ-threatening: rituximab or cyclophosphamide + glucocorticoids
2. Limited disease: methotrexate + glucocorticoids
3. Plasma exchange for severe renal disease or pulmonary hemorrhage
4. Maintenance: rituximab or azathioprine
5. TMP-SMX for PCP prophylaxis during immunosuppression
6. Monitor relapse: ANCA titers, CRP
meds:
Methylprednisolone 1g IV x3 days then prednisone 1mg/kg daily taper
Rituximab 375mg/m2 IV weekly x4 (induction)
Cyclophosphamide 2mg/kg daily or 15mg/kg IV monthly (induction)
Azathioprine 2mg/kg daily (maintenance)
Rituximab 500mg IV q6 months (maintenance)
TMP-SMX DS daily (PCP prophylaxis + may prevent upper airway relapse)
ddx: Microscopic polyangiitis, Eosinophilic granulomatosis (Churg-Strauss), Goodpasture syndrome, Sarcoidosis, Lymphomatoid granulomatosis, Cocaine-induced midline destructive lesion, Relapsing polychondritis
labs:
c-ANCA/PR3 (positive in 90% of generalized GPA)
Urinalysis (RBC casts, hematuria, proteinuria)
BMP (creatinine)
CBC, ESR, CRP
Chest CT (nodules, cavitations, ground-glass)
Sinus CT
Renal biopsy (pauci-immune crescentic GN)
Tissue biopsy (necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis)
notes:
Formerly called Wegener's granulomatosis
Classic triad: upper airway, lower airway, and kidney involvement
c-ANCA/PR3: 90% specific for GPA, titer may correlate with disease activity
Pulmonary-renal syndrome: hemoptysis + rapidly progressive GN → medical emergency
RITUXIVAS and RAVE trials: rituximab noninferior to cyclophosphamide for induction
Relapse common (50% within 5 years) - monitor and maintain immunosuppression
Subglottic stenosis: may need dilation, not responsive to systemic therapy alone
description: A rare autoimmune disease causing inflammation of blood vessels (vasculitis) that primarily affects the sinuses, lungs, and kidneys, formerly known as Wegener's granulomatosis.

name: Carotid Artery Stenosis
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found on screening)
Carotid bruit
TIA symptoms (amaurosis fugax, hemiparesis, aphasia)
Stroke
Hollenhorst plaque on fundoscopy
treatment:
1. Medical optimization for all (antiplatelet, statin, BP control)
2. Symptomatic >=50-70% stenosis: carotid endarterectomy (CEA) within 2 weeks of event
3. Asymptomatic >=70-80% stenosis: CEA if low surgical risk
4. Carotid artery stenting (CAS) if high surgical risk
5. Risk factor modification
6. Surveillance duplex for moderate stenosis
meds:
Aspirin 81-325mg daily
Clopidogrel 75mg daily (with aspirin perioperatively)
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily (BP control)
Dual antiplatelet for CAS (aspirin + clopidogrel x1 month)
ddx: Vertebrobasilar insufficiency, Cardiac embolism (AFib), Aortic arch atheroma, Subclavian steal, Intracranial stenosis, Fibromuscular dysplasia, Carotid dissection
labs:
Carotid duplex ultrasound (first-line screening)
CTA head/neck (surgical planning)
MRA head/neck
Conventional angiography (gold standard, rarely needed)
Brain MRI/CT (completed stroke)
ECG (AFib screening)
Lipid panel, HbA1c, BMP
notes:
NASCET trial: CEA benefit greatest for symptomatic 70-99% stenosis (NNT=6 over 2 years)
ASymptomatic carotid stenosis: CEA benefit modest (NNT=17), medical therapy has improved
CEA should be performed within 2 weeks of TIA/stroke for maximum benefit
CAS preferred if: high surgical risk, previous neck radiation, recurrent stenosis after CEA, high carotid bifurcation
Near-occlusion and complete occlusion: medical management (CEA not beneficial)
Contralateral carotid occlusion increases CEA risk
description: Narrowing of the carotid arteries in the neck due to atherosclerotic plaque, which can reduce blood flow to the brain and increase the risk of stroke.

name: Psoriasis
signs:
Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales
Extensor surfaces (elbows, knees)
Scalp involvement
Nail changes (pitting, onycholysis, oil spots)
Koebner phenomenon (lesions at trauma sites)
Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding with scale removal)
Inverse psoriasis (flexural areas)
treatment:
1. Mild: topical corticosteroids + vitamin D analogs
2. Moderate: phototherapy (NB-UVB)
3. Severe: systemic therapy (methotrexate, biologics)
4. Biologic agents for moderate-severe plaque psoriasis
5. Moisturizers and skin care
6. Screen for psoriatic arthritis and cardiovascular risk
meds:
Betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% cream BID (topical)
Calcipotriene 0.005% cream BID (vitamin D analog)
Methotrexate 7.5-25mg weekly
Adalimumab 40mg SC q2 weeks
Secukinumab 300mg SC monthly (after loading)
Apremilast 30mg BID
Guselkumab 100mg SC q8 weeks (IL-23)
Deucravacitinib 6mg daily (TYK2 inhibitor)
ddx: Eczema, Seborrheic dermatitis, Pityriasis rosea, Tinea corporis, Lichen planus, Secondary syphilis, Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (biopsy rarely needed)
Skin biopsy if atypical: Munro microabscesses, parakeratosis, acanthosis
CBC, LFTs, hepatitis panel before methotrexate
TB screening before biologics
Lipid panel, glucose (cardiovascular risk screening)
Pregnancy test before systemic therapy
notes:
Chronic autoimmune, affects 2-3% of population
PASI score measures severity
Psoriatic arthritis develops in 30% of psoriasis patients
Increased cardiovascular risk: metabolic syndrome, obesity
Methotrexate: requires folate supplementation, monitor LFTs, avoid alcohol
Biologics: screen for TB, hepatitis B before starting
Koebner: new lesions appear at sites of skin trauma
description: A chronic autoimmune skin condition that speeds up the growth cycle of skin cells, causing thick, red, scaly patches that can be itchy and painful.

name: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome
signs:
Prodromal fever, malaise, URI symptoms
Targetoid skin lesions
Painful blistering and erosions
Mucous membrane involvement (oral, ocular, genital)
Epidermal detachment <10% BSA (SJS) or >30% (TEN)
Nikolsky sign positive
Photophobia and eye pain
treatment:
1. Immediate discontinuation of offending drug
2. Transfer to burn unit or ICU for severe cases (TEN)
3. Wound care (non-adherent dressings, avoid silver sulfadiazine)
4. Ophthalmology consultation (urgent)
5. IV fluids and nutritional support
6. Cyclosporine may reduce progression
7. Avoid systemic corticosteroids (controversial)
meds:
IV fluids (guide by Parkland formula-like estimate)
Cyclosporine 3-5mg/kg/day (may reduce progression)
IVIG 2-3g/kg over 3-4 days (controversial)
Pain management (acetaminophen, opioids)
Mouthwash: magic mouthwash for oral erosions
Artificial tears, cycloplegics (eye care)
Avoid: corticosteroids (may worsen, debated)
ddx: Erythema multiforme, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Pemphigus vulgaris, Linear IgA bullous disease, Drug reaction with eosinophilia (DRESS), Acute GVHD
labs:
Skin biopsy (full-thickness epidermal necrosis)
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Blood cultures (rule out secondary infection)
Chest XR
Bronchoscopy if respiratory symptoms
Genetic testing: HLA-B*5801 (allopurinol), HLA-B*1502 (carbamazepine in Asian descent)
SCORTEN score (prognosis)
notes:
SJS: <10% BSA detachment, SJS/TEN overlap: 10-30%, TEN: >30%
Most common drugs: allopurinol, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin), sulfonamides, NSAIDs (oxicam), antibiotics
Latency: 1-3 weeks after drug initiation (4-28 days)
SCORTEN: age >40, malignancy, HR >120, BUN >28, BSA >10%, bicarb <20, glucose >252
Ocular sequelae: symblepharon, dry eye, corneal scarring (lifelong follow-up)
Do NOT rechallenge with offending drug (or related drugs) - can be fatal
Pharmacogenomic testing recommended before high-risk drugs in susceptible populations
description: A rare, serious immune reaction to medication causing painful blistering and peeling of the skin and mucous membranes, requiring urgent care and drug discontinuation.

name: Major Depressive Disorder
signs:
Depressed mood most of the day
Anhedonia (loss of interest/pleasure)
Weight/appetite changes
Insomnia or hypersomnia
Psychomotor agitation or retardation
Fatigue
Worthlessness or excessive guilt
Difficulty concentrating
Suicidal ideation
treatment:
1. Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) - effective alone for mild-moderate
2. Antidepressant medication for moderate-severe
3. Combination therapy (medication + psychotherapy) most effective
4. ECT for treatment-resistant or severe with psychosis/suicidality
5. TMS for treatment-resistant
6. Ketamine/esketamine for treatment-resistant
7. Safety planning if suicidal ideation
meds:
Sertraline 50-200mg daily (SSRI, first-line)
Escitalopram 10-20mg daily
Bupropion 150-450mg daily (no sexual side effects, helps smoking)
Venlafaxine 75-225mg daily (SNRI)
Duloxetine 60-120mg daily (SNRI, comorbid pain)
Mirtazapine 15-45mg at bedtime (helps sleep, appetite)
Esketamine nasal spray (treatment-resistant)
Lithium augmentation 300-600mg daily
ddx: Bipolar disorder (screen for mania), Hypothyroidism, Anemia, Adjustment disorder, Grief, Substance use, Medical illness, Medication side effects
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (DSM-5 criteria)
PHQ-9 screening tool
TSH (rule out hypothyroidism)
CBC (anemia)
Vitamin D level
B12, folate
BMP
Urine drug screen
Alcohol use screening (AUDIT-C)
Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale
notes:
DSM-5: >=5 symptoms for >=2 weeks, must include depressed mood or anhedonia (SIG E CAPS)
SSRIs: 4-6 weeks for full effect, start low and titrate
Black box warning: increased suicidality in <25yo (monitor closely first 4 weeks)
Serotonin syndrome risk with SSRI + MAOI, tramadol, or other serotonergic agents
Treatment-resistant: failed >=2 adequate antidepressant trials
Maintenance: continue antidepressant >=6-12 months after remission (>=2 years if recurrent)
Postpartum depression: sertraline safe in breastfeeding, brexanolone IV for severe
description: A common and serious mental health condition characterized by persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest that significantly impact daily functioning.

name: Schizophrenia
signs:
Positive symptoms: hallucinations (auditory most common), delusions, disorganized speech/behavior
Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, social withdrawal
Cognitive symptoms: impaired attention, working memory, executive function
Onset typically late teens to early 30s
treatment:
1. Second-generation antipsychotics (first-line)
2. First-generation antipsychotics (effective but more EPS)
3. Clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia
4. Psychosocial interventions: CBT, social skills training, supported employment
5. Family psychoeducation
6. Long-acting injectable antipsychotics for adherence
7. Early intervention programs
meds:
Risperidone 2-6mg daily
Olanzapine 10-20mg daily (weight gain risk)
Aripiprazole 10-30mg daily (less metabolic effects)
Quetiapine 400-800mg daily
Paliperidone palmitate 234mg IM then 156mg monthly (LAI)
Clozapine 300-450mg daily (treatment-resistant - requires ANC monitoring)
Haloperidol 5-20mg daily (first-gen)
Benztropine 1-2mg BID (EPS)
ddx: Schizoaffective disorder, Bipolar disorder with psychosis, Brief psychotic disorder, Substance-induced psychosis (meth, PCP, cannabis), Delusional disorder, Major depression with psychotic features, Medical causes (seizures, delirium)
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (DSM-5 criteria)
CBC, BMP, LFTs, lipid panel, HbA1c (baseline metabolic screening)
TSH
Urine drug screen (rule out substance-induced)
RPR/FTA (neurosyphilis)
Brain MRI (rule out organic cause, especially first episode)
ECG (QTc before antipsychotics)
Fasting glucose and lipids q3-6 months on antipsychotics
Clozapine: ANC weekly x6 months, then biweekly
notes:
DSM-5: >=2 symptoms for >=6 months: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized/catatonic behavior, negative symptoms (>=1 must be from first 3)
Clozapine: only drug proven effective for treatment-resistant (failed >=2 adequate trials), requires REMS (agranulocytosis risk 1%)
Metabolic syndrome: monitor weight, glucose, lipids on all SGAs (especially olanzapine, clozapine)
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: rigidity, fever, AMS, elevated CPK - medical emergency
Tardive dyskinesia: involuntary movements, treat with valbenazine or deutetrabenazine
Suicide risk: ~5% lifetime risk, clozapine reduces suicidal behavior
description: A chronic brain disorder causing distorted thinking, hallucinations, delusions, and impaired daily functioning, typically emerging in early adulthood and requiring lifelong treatment.

name: Bipolar Disorder
signs:
Manic episodes: elevated/irritable mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, flight of ideas, increased goal-directed activity, risky behavior
Depressive episodes: same as MDD
Mixed features: simultaneous manic and depressive symptoms
Psychotic features possible in severe mania
treatment:
1. Mood stabilizer is foundation (lithium, valproate)
2. Second-generation antipsychotics (acute mania and maintenance)
3. Lamotrigine for bipolar depression prevention
4. Avoid antidepressant monotherapy (can trigger mania)
5. Psychoeducation and psychotherapy (CBT, IPSRT)
6. ECT for refractory mania or depression
7. Sleep hygiene and lifestyle regularity
meds:
Lithium 600-1200mg daily (target level 0.6-1.0 mEq/L)
Valproate 500-1500mg daily (level 50-100mcg/mL)
Lamotrigine 200mg daily (bipolar depression prevention)
Quetiapine 300-600mg daily (mania and depression)
Aripiprazole 15-30mg daily (mania)
Lurasidone 20-120mg daily (bipolar depression)
Carbamazepine 400-1600mg daily
ddx: Major depressive disorder, Schizoaffective disorder, ADHD, Borderline personality disorder, Substance-induced mood disorder, Cyclothymia, Hyperthyroidism
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (DSM-5)
Lithium level q3-6 months (target 0.6-1.0, toxicity >1.5)
TSH, BMP, calcium q6 months on lithium (nephrotoxicity, hypothyroidism)
Valproate level, CBC, LFTs
Pregnancy test before valproate (teratogenic - neural tube defects)
Urine drug screen
TSH (baseline)
ECG if indicated
notes:
Bipolar I: at least one manic episode (depression not required for diagnosis)
Bipolar II: hypomania + major depressive episodes (never full mania)
Lithium: narrow therapeutic index, teratogenic (Ebstein anomaly), renal/thyroid toxicity
Lithium toxicity: tremor, diarrhea, ataxia, confusion, seizures (check level, hydrate)
Valproate: teratogenic (NTD), hepatotoxic, PCOS association in women
Lamotrigine: slow titration required (SJS risk), main role is bipolar depression prevention
Antidepressants: use only with mood stabilizer, risk of manic switch
description: A mental health condition causing extreme mood swings between emotional highs (mania or hypomania) and lows (depression), requiring mood-stabilizing medications.

name: Delirium
signs:
Acute onset and fluctuating course
Inattention (cardinal feature)
Disorganized thinking
Altered level of consciousness
Perceptual disturbances (hallucinations, illusions)
Psychomotor agitation (hyperactive) or withdrawal (hypoactive)
Sleep-wake cycle disruption
Disorientation
treatment:
1. Identify and treat underlying cause(s)
2. Non-pharmacologic strategies first: reorientation, sleep hygiene, mobilization, sensory aids
3. Remove offending medications (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids)
4. Avoid physical restraints
5. Low-dose antipsychotics only for severe agitation/safety
6. Treat pain, constipation, urinary retention
meds:
Haloperidol 0.5-1mg PO/IV/IM PRN (agitation, avoid in Parkinson's/LBD)
Quetiapine 12.5-50mg PO (preferred in Parkinson's)
Avoid benzodiazepines (worsen delirium, EXCEPT alcohol withdrawal)
Acetaminophen 1g q6h (pain control)
Melatonin 0.5-3mg at bedtime (sleep)
Remove: anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, antihistamines
ddx: Dementia (chronic, not acute), Depression, Psychosis, Sundowning, Nonconvulsive status epilepticus, Stroke, Encephalitis, Medication effects
labs:
CBC, BMP, calcium, magnesium
Urinalysis and urine culture
CXR
Blood cultures if febrile
LFTs, ammonia
TSH, B12
Blood glucose
Urine drug screen
ECG
CT/MRI head (if focal neuro signs or no clear cause)
EEG (if seizures suspected)
notes:
CAM (Confusion Assessment Method): acute onset + fluctuating + inattention + (disorganized thinking OR altered consciousness)
Hypoactive delirium: often missed, worse prognosis
I WATCH DEATH mnemonic: Infection, Withdrawal, Acute metabolic, Trauma, CNS, Hypoxia, Deficiencies, Endocrine, Acute vascular, Toxins/drugs, Heavy metals
Prevention (HELP program): orientation, early mobilization, sleep protocol, vision/hearing aids, hydration
Delirium increases mortality, length of stay, risk of dementia
Avoid: anticholinergics, benzodiazepines (except alcohol withdrawal), restraints
description: A sudden, fluctuating state of mental confusion and inattention usually caused by an underlying medical condition, medication, or infection, most common in hospitalized elderly patients.

name: Preeclampsia
signs:
New-onset hypertension after 20 weeks (>=140/90)
Proteinuria
Headache (severe, not relieved by analgesics)
Visual disturbances (scotomata, blurred vision)
RUQ/epigastric pain
Edema (facial, hands)
Hyperreflexia and clonus
Pulmonary edema
treatment:
1. Delivery is the only definitive cure
2. Severe features or >=37 weeks: deliver
3. <37 weeks without severe features: expectant management with close monitoring
4. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (severe features)
5. Antihypertensives for severe-range BP (>=160/110)
6. Betamethasone for fetal lung maturity if <37 weeks
7. Postpartum monitoring x72h (can worsen postpartum)
meds:
Magnesium sulfate 4-6g IV bolus then 1-2g/hr (seizure prevention)
Labetalol 20mg IV, double q10min (max 300mg) for acute severe HTN
Nifedipine 10-20mg PO q20min (acute severe HTN)
Hydralazine 5-10mg IV q20min
Betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses 24h apart (<37 weeks)
Aspirin 81mg daily (prevention in high-risk women from 12 weeks)
ddx: Gestational hypertension, Chronic hypertension, HELLP syndrome, Eclampsia, TTP/HUS, Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, SLE flare
labs:
BP monitoring (>=140/90 on 2 occasions 4h apart)
Urinalysis and spot urine protein:creatinine ratio (>=0.3)
24h urine protein (>=300mg)
CBC with platelets (<100K = severe)
BMP (creatinine >1.1 = severe)
LFTs (AST/ALT >2x normal = severe)
LDH
Uric acid
Coagulation studies
Fetal assessment: NST, BPP, umbilical artery Doppler
notes:
Severe features: SBP >=160 or DBP >=110, platelets <100K, creatinine >1.1, liver transaminases >2x, pulmonary edema, cerebral/visual symptoms
Eclampsia: seizures in preeclampsia patient - magnesium sulfate is treatment AND prevention
Magnesium toxicity: loss of DTRs → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest (treat with calcium gluconate)
Risk factors: nulliparity, preeclampsia history, chronic HTN, obesity, diabetes, multiple gestation, AMA
Low-dose aspirin prophylaxis: start 12-16 weeks in high-risk women (ACOG recommendation)
Postpartum preeclampsia: can occur up to 6 weeks postpartum
description: A pregnancy complication characterized by high blood pressure and protein in the urine after 20 weeks of gestation, which can progress to life-threatening seizures (eclampsia) if untreated.

name: Ectopic Pregnancy
signs:
Abdominal/pelvic pain (usually unilateral)
Vaginal bleeding (often spotty)
Amenorrhea (missed period)
Adnexal tenderness
Adnexal mass
Shoulder pain (diaphragmatic irritation from hemoperitoneum)
Hemodynamic instability if ruptured
Cervical motion tenderness
treatment:
1. Hemodynamically unstable/ruptured: emergent surgery (laparoscopy/laparotomy)
2. Methotrexate (medical management) if unruptured, stable, and meets criteria
3. Expectant management if hCG declining and low levels
4. Serial hCG monitoring until <5 mIU/mL
5. RhoGAM if Rh-negative
6. Contraception counseling (future ectopic risk 10-15%)
meds:
Methotrexate 50mg/m2 IM (single dose protocol)
Methotrexate two-dose or multi-dose protocols for higher hCG
Rh(D) immune globulin 300mcg IM (if Rh-negative)
Analgesics (avoid NSAIDs with methotrexate)
Folate supplementation NOT given during methotrexate treatment
ddx: Threatened abortion, Inevitable abortion, Ovarian cyst rupture, Ovarian torsion, Appendicitis, PID, Corpus luteum cyst, Heterotopic pregnancy (rare)
labs:
Quantitative beta-hCG (serial levels)
Transvaginal ultrasound (empty uterus with adnexal mass or free fluid)
CBC (H&H for bleeding)
Type and screen/crossmatch
BMP
LFTs (before methotrexate)
Progesterone (low levels <5 suggests nonviable pregnancy)
notes:
Fallopian tube: 95% of ectopic pregnancies (ampulla most common)
Risk factors: prior ectopic, PID/STI history, tubal surgery, IUD, ART/IVF
Discriminatory zone: hCG 1500-2000 → should see IUP on TVUS; absence suggests ectopic
Normal hCG rise: doubles q48-72h in early pregnancy
Methotrexate criteria: hemodynamically stable, unruptured, hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity, size <4cm
Ruptured ectopic: leading cause of first-trimester maternal death
Future fertility: ~60% intrauterine pregnancy rate after ectopic
description: A pregnancy that implants outside the uterus, most commonly in a fallopian tube, which cannot support normal development and can rupture causing life-threatening internal bleeding.

name: Ovarian Torsion
signs:
Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain
Nausea/vomiting
Adnexal tenderness with palpable mass
Fever (late)
Peritoneal signs (late)
Pain may wax and wane (intermittent torsion)
Adnexal mass on exam
treatment:
1. Emergent surgical exploration (laparoscopy preferred)
2. Ovarian detorsion with preservation (first choice, especially reproductive age)
3. Oophorectomy if nonviable or postmenopausal
4. Cystectomy if cyst present
5. Do NOT delay surgery for imaging if high clinical suspicion
6. Pain management while awaiting surgery
meds:
Ketorolac 30mg IV (pain)
Morphine 2-4mg IV PRN
Ondansetron 4mg IV (nausea)
IV fluids
Antibiotics if concern for abscess or necrotic tissue
ddx: Ruptured ovarian cyst, Ectopic pregnancy, Appendicitis, PID/tubo-ovarian abscess, Endometriosis, Renal colic, Ovarian hyperstimulation
labs:
Pelvic ultrasound with Doppler (enlarged ovary, absent/reduced flow)
Pregnancy test (always in reproductive-age women)
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP
Urinalysis
Lactate
notes:
Most common in reproductive age, 20% in prepubertal girls
Risk factors: ovarian cyst/mass >5cm, pregnancy, ovarian hyperstimulation, prior torsion
Doppler may show normal flow (doesn't rule out torsion - dual blood supply)
Whirl sign on US: twisted vascular pedicle
Detorsion successful even with dusky/black ovary - ovarian recovery >90%
Recurrence: consider oophoropexy (fixation)
Right side > left (sigmoid colon may limit left-sided torsion)
description: A gynecological emergency where the ovary twists on its supporting ligaments, cutting off blood supply and causing sudden severe pelvic pain requiring urgent surgery.

name: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma
signs:
Sudden severe eye pain
Blurred vision with halos around lights
Red eye (conjunctival injection)
Fixed, mid-dilated pupil
Rock-hard eye on palpation
Nausea/vomiting
Headache
Corneal edema (hazy cornea)
Markedly elevated IOP (>40mmHg)
treatment:
1. Medical emergency - lower IOP immediately
2. Topical and systemic IOP-lowering agents
3. Laser peripheral iridotomy (definitive treatment)
4. Treat fellow eye prophylactically (iridotomy)
5. Avoid mydriatic agents
6. Ophthalmology consultation emergently
meds:
Timolol 0.5% 1 drop (beta-blocker)
Pilocarpine 2% 1 drop q15min x3 (miotic)
Apraclonidine 1% 1 drop (alpha-agonist)
Acetazolamide 500mg IV then 250mg PO QID
Mannitol 1-2g/kg IV (refractory)
Prednisolone acetate 1% 1 drop q15min x4 then q1h (inflammation)
Analgesics and antiemetics
ddx: Open-angle glaucoma, Uveitis, Conjunctivitis, Corneal ulcer, Endophthalmitis, Cluster headache, Intracranial pathology
labs:
Tonometry (IOP markedly elevated, often >40mmHg)
Slit lamp exam (shallow anterior chamber, corneal edema, cells/flare)
Gonioscopy (closed angle)
Pupil exam (fixed, mid-dilated)
Fundoscopy (optic disc edema)
Anterior segment OCT
Ultrasound biomicroscopy
notes:
Anatomic risk: hyperopia (farsighted), shallow anterior chamber, thick lens, Asian/Inuit descent
Triggers: dim lighting, sympathomimetics, anticholinergics, topical mydriatics
Pilocarpine works once IOP starts to decrease (iris sphincter ischemic at very high pressures)
Laser peripheral iridotomy: creates hole in iris for aqueous humor flow
Prophylactic iridotomy to contralateral eye (50% risk of developing AACG)
Medications to AVOID in narrow angles: anticholinergics, sympathomimetics, topiramate, sulfonamides
description: A sudden blockage of fluid drainage in the eye causing rapidly rising eye pressure, severe pain, and potential permanent vision loss if not treated within hours.

name: Retinal Detachment
signs:
Sudden onset of floaters
Flashing lights (photopsia)
Curtain or shadow descending across visual field
Painless progressive vision loss
Reduced visual acuity (if macula involved)
Relative afferent pupillary defect (large detachment)
treatment:
1. Urgent ophthalmology referral
2. Macula-on: urgent surgery within 24h
3. Macula-off: surgery within days (less urgent)
4. Pneumatic retinopexy (gas bubble injection)
5. Scleral buckle
6. Pars plana vitrectomy
7. Laser or cryopexy for retinal tears without detachment
8. Positioning post-surgery (face-down for gas bubble)
meds:
No primary medical treatment (surgical condition)
Post-operative: topical antibiotics + steroids
Prednisolone acetate 1% q2h post-op
Cyclopentolate 1% BID post-op
Analgesics PRN
ddx: Posterior vitreous detachment (floaters without detachment), Vitreous hemorrhage, Central retinal vein occlusion, Choroidal detachment, Retinoschisis, Migraine aura
labs:
Dilated fundus exam (detached retina, retinal tears)
B-scan ultrasound (if media opacity prevents visualization)
OCT (macular involvement assessment)
Visual field testing
Visual acuity
notes:
Types: rhegmatogenous (most common - retinal break), tractional (diabetes), exudative (inflammation/tumor)
Risk factors: myopia, prior eye surgery, trauma, family history, lattice degeneration
Macula-on vs macula-off: determines urgency and visual prognosis
PVD symptoms (new floaters/flashes): examine urgently to rule out retinal tear
Gas bubble: avoid air travel and nitrous oxide anesthesia until absorbed
Recurrence: 5-10% after primary repair
Lifetime risk: ~1 in 300
description: A serious eye condition where the retina pulls away from its underlying support tissue, causing flashes, floaters, and progressive vision loss that requires urgent surgical repair.

name: Peritonsillar Abscess
signs:
Severe sore throat (usually unilateral)
Fever
Trismus (difficulty opening mouth)
Muffled/hot potato voice
Drooling
Deviated uvula (away from affected side)
Unilateral tonsillar bulging
Odynophagia
Cervical lymphadenopathy
Ear pain (referred)
treatment:
1. Needle aspiration or incision and drainage (first-line)
2. IV antibiotics
3. Pain management
4. IV fluids if unable to swallow
5. Tonsillectomy for recurrent PTA (interval or quinsy tonsillectomy)
6. Reassess if no improvement after drainage
meds:
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h (penicillin allergy)
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125mg PO BID (step-down)
Dexamethasone 10mg IV x1 (may reduce pain/swelling)
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen alternating
Viscous lidocaine gargle PRN
ddx: Peritonsillar cellulitis, Retropharyngeal abscess, Parapharyngeal abscess, Epiglottitis, Ludwig's angina, Infectious mononucleosis, Tonsillar lymphoma
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (usually no imaging needed)
CT neck with contrast (if deep space infection suspected)
CBC (leukocytosis)
Monospot (rule out EBV)
Culture of aspirate
Blood cultures if septic
notes:
Most common deep space infection of the head and neck
Usually polymicrobial: Group A Strep, Fusobacterium, anaerobes
Needle aspiration: 90% success rate, can be done in ED
Urgent airway concern if bilateral or extending to parapharyngeal space
Recurrence rate: 10-15%
Absolute tonsillectomy indication: recurrent PTA (>=2 episodes)
Danger space extension: can lead to mediastinitis (life-threatening)
description: A collection of pus forming behind the tonsil, usually as a complication of tonsillitis, causing severe throat pain, difficulty swallowing, and a characteristic muffled voice.

name: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo
signs:
Brief episodes of vertigo (seconds to <1 minute)
Triggered by head position changes (rolling in bed, looking up)
Nausea
Positive Dix-Hallpike test (upbeat/torsional nystagmus)
No hearing loss
No neurological deficits
Symptom-free between episodes
treatment:
1. Canalith repositioning maneuvers (first-line)
2. Epley maneuver for posterior canal BPPV (most common)
3. BBQ roll for horizontal canal BPPV
4. Vestibular rehabilitation exercises
5. Avoid meclizine for maintenance (delays compensation)
6. Surgery rarely needed (canal plugging for refractory)
meds:
No medications for BPPV itself
Meclizine 25mg PRN (only for acute severe symptoms, short-term)
Dimenhydrinate 50mg PRN (short-term)
Ondansetron 4mg (nausea)
Avoid chronic vestibular suppressants (delay central compensation)
ddx: Meniere's disease, Vestibular neuritis, Labyrinthitis, Central vertigo (stroke, MS), Vestibular migraine, Cervicogenic dizziness, Orthostatic hypotension
labs:
Clinical diagnosis based on Dix-Hallpike and supine roll tests
No imaging needed for typical presentation
Brain MRI if: central features (vertical nystagmus, direction-changing without position change, neurological deficits), or refractory to repositioning
Audiometry (normal in BPPV)
VidENG if diagnostic uncertainty
notes:
Most common cause of vertigo (17-42% of all vertigo)
Posterior canal: 80% of BPPV (Dix-Hallpike positive)
Horizontal canal: 15% (supine roll test positive)
Epley maneuver: 80% resolution after 1-2 treatments
Otoconia (calcium carbonate crystals) dislodged from utricle into semicircular canal
Recurrence: 15-20% per year
Risk factors: head trauma, prolonged bed rest, osteoporosis, older age
Brandt-Daroff exercises: home-based habituation therapy
description: The most common cause of vertigo, caused by tiny calcium crystals dislodged in the inner ear's semicircular canals, triggering brief spinning sensations with head position changes.

name: Testicular Torsion
signs:
Sudden severe unilateral testicular pain
Swollen, tender testicle
High-riding testicle
Absent cremasteric reflex
Transverse lie of testicle
Nausea/vomiting
Negative Prehn sign (elevation does NOT relieve pain)
Diffuse testicular tenderness (vs point tenderness in epididymitis)
treatment:
1. Emergent surgical exploration (do NOT delay for imaging if high suspicion)
2. Manual detorsion (temporizing): open the book (outward rotation)
3. Orchidopexy (fixation) of affected AND contralateral testis
4. Orchiectomy if nonviable
5. Time is critical: >6h = increasing risk of testicular loss
6. Surgery should occur within 4-6h of symptom onset
meds:
IV analgesics (morphine, ketorolac)
Antiemetics (ondansetron 4mg IV)
IV fluids
Cefazolin 2g IV (surgical prophylaxis)
No medical treatment is substitute for surgery
ddx: Epididymitis, Torsion of appendix testis, Inguinal hernia (incarcerated), Testicular tumor, Orchitis, Hydrocele, Varicocele, Trauma
labs:
Clinical diagnosis - do NOT delay surgery for imaging
Color Doppler ultrasound (absent/decreased blood flow) - ONLY if diagnosis uncertain and won't delay surgery
Urinalysis (typically normal in torsion, pyuria suggests epididymitis)
CBC
notes:
Peak incidence: neonatal and 12-18 years (bell-clapper deformity)
Salvage rates: <6h = >90%, 6-12h = 50%, >24h = <10%
Bilateral orchidopexy mandatory (prevent contralateral torsion)
Manual detorsion: rotate affected testicle outward (like opening a book) - requires surgical exploration regardless
Intermittent torsion: recurrent episodes of pain that resolve - still needs orchidopexy
Torsion of appendix testis: blue dot sign, self-limited, treated conservatively
description: A urological emergency where the spermatic cord twists, cutting off blood supply to the testicle, requiring surgery within hours to prevent permanent damage or loss of the testicle.

name: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
signs:
Urinary frequency
Nocturia
Weak urinary stream
Hesitancy
Incomplete emptying
Terminal dribbling
Urgency
Enlarged prostate on DRE (smooth, firm, elastic)
treatment:
1. Watchful waiting for mild symptoms (AUA score <8)
2. Alpha-blockers for moderate-severe symptoms (first-line)
3. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors for large prostates (>40g)
4. Combination therapy for significant symptoms + large prostate
5. Minimally invasive procedures: UroLift, Rezum, TURP
6. TURP (transurethral resection) for refractory cases
meds:
Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (alpha-blocker)
Finasteride 5mg daily (5-ARI, prostate >40g)
Dutasteride 0.5mg daily (5-ARI)
Tadalafil 5mg daily (PDE5i, concurrent ED)
Combination: tamsulosin + dutasteride (CombAT trial)
Mirabegron 25-50mg daily (add-on for storage symptoms)
ddx: Prostate cancer, Urethral stricture, Bladder cancer, Neurogenic bladder, UTI, Prostatitis, Overactive bladder, Medications (anticholinergics, decongestants)
labs:
AUA Symptom Score (IPSS)
DRE
PSA (elevated in BPH, rule out cancer)
Urinalysis
Post-void residual (bladder scan)
Uroflowmetry (peak flow rate)
Renal ultrasound (if renal insufficiency suspected)
Cystoscopy if hematuria or diagnostic uncertainty
Transrectal ultrasound (prostate volume)
notes:
Prevalence increases with age: 50% at age 50, 80% at age 80
AUA score: 0-7 mild, 8-19 moderate, 20-35 severe
Alpha-blockers: rapid symptom relief (days), retrograde ejaculation side effect
5-ARIs: shrink prostate over 6-12 months, reduce retention/surgery risk, lower PSA by ~50%
Acute urinary retention: catheterize, start alpha-blocker, trial of void in 1-3 days
Intraoperative floppy iris syndrome: tamsulosin use complicates cataract surgery
TURP: gold standard surgery, risk of retrograde ejaculation (75%)
description: A non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland that commonly occurs with aging, causing difficulty urinating due to compression of the urethra.

name: Kawasaki Disease
signs:
Fever >=5 days (required criterion)
Bilateral nonexudative conjunctival injection
Oral changes (strawberry tongue, cracked lips)
Polymorphous rash
Cervical lymphadenopathy (>=1.5cm, usually unilateral)
Extremity changes (edema, erythema → desquamation)
Irritability
Perianal desquamation
treatment:
1. IVIG 2g/kg as single infusion (within 10 days of fever onset)
2. High-dose aspirin during acute phase
3. Low-dose aspirin for 6-8 weeks (antiplatelet)
4. Repeat IVIG if persistent/recurrent fever
5. Corticosteroids for refractory cases
6. Serial echocardiograms (baseline, 2 weeks, 6-8 weeks)
7. Long-term cardiology follow-up if coronary involvement
meds:
IVIG 2g/kg IV over 10-12 hours
Aspirin 80-100mg/kg/day divided QID (acute febrile phase)
Aspirin 3-5mg/kg/day (after afebrile, for 6-8 weeks minimum)
Methylprednisolone 30mg/kg IV (refractory, max 1g)
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV (second refractory)
Avoid live vaccines for 11 months after IVIG
ddx: Scarlet fever, Measles, Toxic shock syndrome, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, Drug reaction, JIA, Adenovirus, EBV/CMV
labs:
CBC (leukocytosis, thrombocytosis week 2-3, anemia)
CRP/ESR markedly elevated
CMP, LFTs (mild hepatitis)
Urinalysis (sterile pyuria)
Echocardiogram (coronary artery aneurysms, pericardial effusion)
Lipid panel
BNP
Blood cultures (rule out sepsis)
notes:
Diagnosis: fever >=5 days + >=4 of 5 clinical criteria (CRASH: Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue, Hand/foot changes)
Incomplete Kawasaki: fever + 2-3 criteria with lab/echo support (especially in infants)
Coronary artery aneurysm: main complication, risk 25% without treatment, <5% with IVIG
Leading cause of acquired heart disease in developed countries
Peak age: 6 months - 5 years
Giant coronary aneurysm (>=8mm): lifelong anticoagulation, cardiology follow-up
Delay live vaccines 11 months after IVIG (interferes with immune response)
description: A childhood illness causing inflammation of blood vessels throughout the body, particularly dangerous for the coronary arteries, requiring prompt treatment with IVIG to prevent heart damage.

name: Intussusception
signs:
Colicky abdominal pain (episodic, every 15-20 minutes)
Drawing up of legs during pain episodes
Lethargy between episodes
Currant jelly stools (late)
Palpable sausage-shaped mass (RUQ)
Vomiting
Empty RLQ (Dance sign)
Bloody mucus on rectal exam
treatment:
1. NPO, IV fluids, NG tube if vomiting
2. Air or barium enema reduction (first-line for ileocolic)
3. Surgical reduction if: peritonitis, perforation, failed enema, recurrent, or pathologic lead point
4. Bowel resection if necrosis
5. Observe 24h after successful reduction (recurrence risk 5-10%)
meds:
IV fluids: NS or LR bolus 20mL/kg
Antibiotics if peritonitis (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole)
Analgesics
Atropine 0.01mg/kg IV (may facilitate reduction during enema)
Antiemetics PRN
ddx: Gastroenteritis, Appendicitis, Meckel diverticulum, Volvulus, Henoch-Schonlein purpura, Bowel obstruction, Incarcerated hernia
labs:
Abdominal ultrasound (target/donut sign = pathognomonic)
Abdominal XR (may show mass, obstruction pattern)
Air enema (diagnostic AND therapeutic)
CBC
BMP
Type and screen (if surgery anticipated)
Stool guaiac
notes:
Most common cause of bowel obstruction in children 6 months - 3 years
Ileocolic most common type (ileum telescopes into colon)
No pathologic lead point in most children (viral lymphoid hyperplasia)
Pathologic lead point more likely in: >5 years old (Meckel's, lymphoma, polyp, HSP)
Air enema: 80-95% success rate for reduction
Recurrence: 5-10% after successful reduction
Adults: usually has pathologic lead point (tumor) - CT diagnosis, surgical management
description: A medical emergency where one segment of intestine telescopes into an adjacent segment, most common in infants and toddlers, causing colicky pain and requiring urgent reduction.

name: Bronchiolitis
signs:
Rhinorrhea and cough (prodrome)
Wheezing and crackles
Tachypnea
Retractions (subcostal, intercostal)
Nasal flaring
Poor feeding
Apnea (especially in premature infants)
Hypoxemia
treatment:
1. Supportive care is mainstay (no proven benefit of bronchodilators or steroids)
2. Nasal suctioning
3. Supplemental oxygen if SpO2 <90% (AAP) or <92%
4. IV/NG fluids if unable to feed
5. High-flow nasal cannula for moderate-severe
6. Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk infants
7. Minimize interventions (AAP guidelines)
meds:
Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >=90%
Normal saline nasal drops + suctioning
Hypertonic saline 3% nebulized (hospitalized patients only, modest benefit)
Albuterol trial may be considered but discontinue if no response
Do NOT routinely use: systemic steroids, antibiotics, chest physiotherapy
Palivizumab 15mg/kg IM monthly (prevention in high-risk, RSV season)
ddx: Asthma, Pneumonia, Pertussis, Foreign body aspiration, Croup, Congenital heart disease, Vascular ring, Cystic fibrosis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
RSV rapid antigen test or PCR (cohorting, not management change)
Pulse oximetry
CXR only if diagnostic uncertainty or severe
CBC rarely needed
Blood gas if respiratory failure
No routine labs recommended (AAP)
notes:
RSV most common cause (50-80%), also rhinovirus, hMPV, parainfluenza
Peak age: 2-6 months, most common <2 years
AAP guidelines: supportive care only, no bronchodilators or steroids recommended
High-risk for severe disease: premature (<29 weeks), <3 months, CHD, chronic lung disease, immunodeficiency
Apnea: more common in ex-premature infants and neonates
Hospitalization criteria: unable to feed, persistent hypoxemia, significant respiratory distress, apnea
Discharge when: improving, feeding adequate, SpO2 stable on RA
description: A common lower respiratory infection in infants and young children, usually caused by RSV, causing wheezing, difficulty breathing, and feeding problems, managed primarily with supportive care.

name: Lung Cancer
signs:
Chronic cough (new or changed)
Hemoptysis
Dyspnea
Chest pain
Weight loss
Hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve)
Superior vena cava syndrome
Pancoast syndrome (shoulder pain, Horner syndrome)
Paraneoplastic syndromes
Clubbing
treatment:
1. Stage I-II NSCLC: surgical resection (lobectomy)
2. Stage III: chemoradiation +/- immunotherapy
3. Stage IV: systemic therapy (targeted therapy, immunotherapy, chemotherapy)
4. SCLC limited stage: chemoradiation
5. SCLC extensive: chemotherapy + immunotherapy
6. Palliative care integration for advanced disease
7. Molecular testing for targetable mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, etc.)
meds:
Cisplatin/carboplatin + pemetrexed (non-squamous NSCLC)
Carboplatin + paclitaxel (squamous NSCLC)
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk (PD-L1 >=50% or with chemo)
Osimertinib 80mg daily (EGFR mutation)
Alectinib 600mg BID (ALK rearrangement)
Cisplatin/carboplatin + etoposide (SCLC)
Atezolizumab (SCLC, with chemo)
ddx: Pulmonary metastases, Pneumonia, Tuberculosis, Sarcoidosis, Pulmonary abscess, Benign pulmonary nodule, Mesothelioma
labs:
CT chest (nodule/mass characterization)
PET-CT (staging)
Tissue biopsy (bronchoscopy, CT-guided, surgical)
Brain MRI (staging, especially SCLC)
CBC, BMP, LFTs, calcium
Molecular testing: EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, KRAS, NTRK
PD-L1 expression (immunotherapy eligibility)
Bone scan
PFTs (surgical candidacy)
notes:
Leading cause of cancer death worldwide
NSCLC (85%): adenocarcinoma (most common), squamous cell, large cell
SCLC (15%): highly aggressive, often disseminated at diagnosis, initially chemo-sensitive
LDCT screening: ages 50-80, >=20 pack-year history, current or quit within 15 years
Paraneoplastic: SIADH, Cushing (SCLC), hypercalcemia (squamous), Lambert-Eaton (SCLC)
Superior sulcus (Pancoast): shoulder pain, Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis)
Targeted therapy has transformed outcomes for driver-mutation positive NSCLC
description: Cancer originating in the lungs, the leading cause of cancer death, classified as non-small cell (85%) or small cell (15%), with treatment depending on type, stage, and molecular markers.

name: Breast Cancer
signs:
Painless breast mass (hard, fixed, irregular borders)
Skin changes (dimpling, peau d'orange)
Nipple retraction or discharge (bloody)
Axillary lymphadenopathy
Breast asymmetry
Inflammatory breast cancer: red, warm, swollen breast
Often detected by screening mammography (no symptoms)
treatment:
1. Surgery: lumpectomy + radiation or mastectomy
2. Sentinel lymph node biopsy
3. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (locally advanced, HER2+, triple negative)
4. Adjuvant therapy based on receptor status
5. Radiation after lumpectomy
6. Endocrine therapy for ER+ (5-10 years)
7. Anti-HER2 therapy for HER2+ (trastuzumab)
8. Genetic testing and counseling (BRCA1/2)
meds:
Tamoxifen 20mg daily x5-10 years (premenopausal, ER+)
Anastrozole 1mg daily (postmenopausal, ER+)
Trastuzumab 6mg/kg IV q3wk x1 year (HER2+)
Pertuzumab + trastuzumab (HER2+ neoadjuvant/adjuvant)
Doxorubicin + cyclophosphamide then paclitaxel (AC-T, chemotherapy)
Abemaciclib 150mg BID (CDK4/6 inhibitor, high-risk ER+)
Olaparib 300mg BID (BRCA-mutated)
ddx: Fibroadenoma, Fibrocystic changes, Breast cyst, Fat necrosis, Phyllodes tumor, Paget disease of the nipple, Inflammatory breast conditions
labs:
Mammography (screening and diagnostic)
Breast ultrasound
Breast MRI (high-risk screening, extent of disease)
Core needle biopsy (tissue diagnosis)
ER/PR receptor status
HER2 status (IHC, FISH)
Ki-67 proliferation index
Oncotype DX or MammaPrint (genomic assays for ER+ early-stage)
BRCA1/2 and genetic panel testing
Staging: CT, bone scan, PET-CT for advanced
notes:
Screening: mammography q1-2 years starting age 40-50 (guidelines vary)
ER+/HER2-: most common subtype (~70%), endocrine therapy is backbone
HER2+: trastuzumab revolutionized outcomes (previously poor prognosis)
Triple negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-): chemotherapy + immunotherapy, often BRCA-associated
BRCA1/2: 60-80% lifetime breast cancer risk, consider risk-reducing mastectomy
Oncotype DX: RS <26 → may omit chemotherapy in node-negative ER+ (TAILORx trial)
Inflammatory breast cancer: clinical diagnosis (T4d), neoadjuvant chemotherapy first
description: The most common cancer in women, originating in breast tissue, with treatment and prognosis depending on the tumor's hormone receptor status, HER2 status, and stage at diagnosis.

name: Colorectal Cancer
signs:
Change in bowel habits
Rectal bleeding or melena
Iron deficiency anemia (right-sided)
Abdominal pain
Weight loss
Tenesmus (rectal cancer)
Palpable abdominal mass
Bowel obstruction (late)
Often asymptomatic (detected by screening)
treatment:
1. Surgical resection (colectomy) with lymph node dissection
2. Stage III: adjuvant chemotherapy (FOLFOX)
3. Stage II high-risk: consider adjuvant chemotherapy
4. Rectal cancer: neoadjuvant chemoradiation then surgery (TME)
5. Stage IV: systemic therapy +/- surgical resection of metastases
6. dMMR/MSI-H: immunotherapy (pembrolizumab)
7. Targeted therapy based on molecular profile
meds:
FOLFOX: 5-FU + leucovorin + oxaliplatin (adjuvant, stage III)
FOLFIRI: 5-FU + leucovorin + irinotecan (metastatic)
Capecitabine 1250mg/m2 BID (oral 5-FU)
Bevacizumab 5mg/kg IV q2wk (anti-VEGF, metastatic)
Cetuximab (anti-EGFR, KRAS wild-type, left-sided)
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk (dMMR/MSI-H)
5-FU/capecitabine + radiation (neoadjuvant rectal)
ddx: IBD, Diverticular disease, Hemorrhoids, IBS, Polyps, Infectious colitis, Ischemic colitis, Other GI malignancy
labs:
Colonoscopy with biopsy (diagnostic, gold standard)
CEA (baseline and monitoring, not screening)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
PET-CT (staging if surgery planned for metastases)
CBC (iron deficiency anemia)
LFTs
Molecular testing: KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, MSI/dMMR, HER2
Rectal cancer: MRI pelvis (staging, CRM assessment)
Genetic testing for Lynch syndrome if age <50 or family history
notes:
Third most common cancer, second leading cause of cancer death
Screening: colonoscopy q10 years starting age 45, or FIT annually, or Cologuard q3 years
Adenoma-carcinoma sequence: normal → polyp → dysplasia → carcinoma (takes ~10 years)
Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 mutations, increased CRC risk (80% lifetime)
Right-sided: often anemia, more likely BRAF+, MSI-H
Left-sided: obstruction, bleeding, better response to anti-EGFR therapy
Liver metastases: potentially curable with resection (5-year survival 40-60% if resectable)
description: Cancer of the colon or rectum, typically developing from precancerous polyps over many years, making it highly preventable through screening colonoscopy.

name: Fournier's Gangrene
signs:
Perineal/scrotal pain and swelling
Rapid progression
Crepitus (subcutaneous gas)
Fever and sepsis
Erythema with dusky discoloration
Foul-smelling discharge
Systemic toxicity out of proportion to exam
treatment:
1. Emergent surgical debridement (most important)
2. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
3. Aggressive fluid resuscitation
4. Repeat debridement q24-48h until clean margins
5. ICU admission
6. Wound VAC after debridement
7. Reconstructive surgery after infection controlled
meds:
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Clindamycin 900mg IV q8h (toxin suppression)
Meropenem 1g IV q8h (alternative)
IV fluids aggressive resuscitation
Vasopressors as needed
ddx: Scrotal abscess, Epididymitis, Cellulitis, Incarcerated hernia, Testicular torsion, Necrotizing fasciitis (other sites)
labs:
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP (renal function, acidosis)
Lactate elevated
Blood cultures
CT pelvis (gas tracking in soft tissues)
Wound cultures (polymicrobial)
Coagulation studies
ProCalcitonin
notes:
Polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis of perineum/genitalia
Risk factors: diabetes (most common), immunosuppression, alcoholism, obesity
Mortality 20-40% even with treatment
LFINSI score predicts severity and mortality
Often polymicrobial: E. coli, Bacteroides, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Clostridium
Surgical debridement is definitive - antibiotics alone are insufficient
Fecal diversion (colostomy) may be needed if perianal source
description: A rapidly progressive, life-threatening necrotizing infection of the perineal and genital area requiring emergency surgical debridement and broad-spectrum antibiotics.

name: Epididymitis
signs:
Gradual onset scrotal pain
Swollen, tender epididymis
Fever
Dysuria and urinary frequency
Positive Prehn sign (relief with elevation)
Cremaster reflex intact
Reactive hydrocele
treatment:
1. Antibiotics based on likely pathogen
2. Scrotal elevation and support
3. NSAIDs and ice for pain
4. Rest
5. STI testing and partner treatment if sexually transmitted
6. Urology follow-up if no improvement in 48-72h
meds:
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM x1 + doxycycline 100mg BID x10 days (STI-related, age <35)
Levofloxacin 500mg daily x10 days (non-STI, age >35, enteric organisms)
Ibuprofen 600mg TID
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Scrotal support (jockstrap)
ddx: Testicular torsion (emergency), Torsion of appendix testis, Orchitis, Inguinal hernia, Hydrocele, Testicular tumor, Trauma
labs:
Urinalysis and urine culture
STI testing: GC/chlamydia NAAT
CBC
Color Doppler ultrasound (increased blood flow to epididymis, vs absent in torsion)
Urethral swab if discharge
notes:
Age <35: usually sexually transmitted (N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis)
Age >35: usually enteric organisms (E. coli) from UTI or BPH
Key differentiator from torsion: gradual onset, positive Prehn sign, intact cremaster reflex, pyuria
If uncertain between torsion and epididymitis: surgical exploration (don't miss torsion)
Complications: abscess, chronic pain, infertility
Pediatric epididymitis: usually non-infectious, may be post-viral
description: Inflammation of the epididymis, the coiled tube behind the testicle, usually caused by bacterial infection from sexually transmitted organisms in younger men or urinary bacteria in older men.

name: Urinary Retention
signs:
Inability to void
Suprapubic fullness and discomfort
Overflow incontinence
Palpable/percussible bladder
Lower abdominal distension
Agitation in elderly/demented patients
Renal failure if bilateral obstruction
treatment:
1. Immediate bladder catheterization (Foley or intermittent)
2. Decompress gradually (controversial - drain freely per recent evidence)
3. Identify and treat underlying cause
4. Alpha-blocker trial before catheter removal
5. Trial of void after 1-3 days of catheter drainage
6. Clean intermittent catheterization if chronic
7. Surgery for BPH if refractory
meds:
Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (start before catheter removal trial)
Finasteride 5mg daily (large prostate)
Bethanechol 10-50mg TID (detrusor underactivity, limited evidence)
Oxybutynin AVOID (worsens retention)
Discontinue anticholinergics, antihistamines, decongestants
ddx: BPH, Urethral stricture, Prostate cancer, Neurogenic bladder, Medication-induced, Constipation/fecal impaction, Cauda equina syndrome, Post-operative
labs:
Post-void residual (>200mL significant, >500mL = retention)
BMP (creatinine - renal function)
Urinalysis and culture
PSA
Renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis)
Cystoscopy if stricture suspected
Urodynamic studies (chronic/neurogenic)
notes:
Acute: sudden inability to void, painful, requires immediate catheterization
Chronic: incomplete emptying, overflow incontinence, painless
Common medications causing retention: anticholinergics, antihistamines, opioids, TCAs, alpha-agonists (pseudoephedrine)
Post-operative retention: common after surgery, anesthesia, especially with opioids
Cauda equina: urinary retention + saddle anesthesia + bilateral leg symptoms → emergent MRI
Decompression cystitis: hematuria after drainage (benign, self-limited)
Post-obstructive diuresis: monitor UO and replace fluids if >200mL/hr
description: The inability to completely empty the bladder, which can occur suddenly (acute) or develop gradually (chronic), most commonly caused by prostate enlargement in older men.

name: Renal Cell Carcinoma
signs:
Classic triad (10%): hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass
Most found incidentally on imaging
Weight loss
Fever of unknown origin
Varicocele (left-sided, doesn't decompress when supine)
Paraneoplastic syndromes: polycythemia, hypercalcemia, hypertension
Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction)
treatment:
1. Localized: partial or radical nephrectomy
2. Small tumors (<4cm): active surveillance or ablation option
3. Metastatic: systemic therapy (immunotherapy + TKI)
4. Cytoreductive nephrectomy in select metastatic cases
5. Surveillance protocol after resection
6. Radiation for bone/brain metastases
meds:
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk + axitinib 5mg BID (metastatic, first-line)
Nivolumab 3mg/kg + ipilimumab 1mg/kg IV (metastatic, first-line)
Sunitinib 50mg daily (4wk on/2wk off)
Cabozantinib 60mg daily
Pazopanib 800mg daily
Everolimus 10mg daily (second-line)
Radiation for symptomatic metastases
ddx: Renal cyst, Angiomyolipoma, Oncocytoma, Transitional cell carcinoma, Renal abscess, Adrenal mass, Retroperitoneal sarcoma
labs:
CT abdomen with and without contrast (enhancing renal mass)
Renal mass biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain)
CBC (polycythemia or anemia)
BMP (calcium, creatinine)
LFTs
LDH
Chest CT (staging)
Brain MRI and bone scan (if symptomatic)
Urinalysis
notes:
Clear cell: most common subtype (75%), VHL pathway, responds to TKI/immunotherapy
Papillary and chromophobe: less common, different biology
Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome: bilateral/multifocal RCC, hemangioblastomas, pheochromocytoma
RCC can metastasize to unusual sites: bone, lung, brain, contralateral kidney
RCC is classically resistant to traditional chemotherapy and radiation
Spontaneous regression of metastases: rare but reported (immune-mediated)
IVC tumor thrombus: surgical challenge, may extend to right atrium
description: The most common type of kidney cancer in adults, often found incidentally on imaging, arising from the cells lining the kidney tubules and frequently associated with paraneoplastic syndromes.

name: Prostate Cancer
signs:
Often asymptomatic (detected by PSA screening)
Urinary symptoms (frequency, hesitancy, nocturia)
Bone pain (metastatic)
Hard, irregular nodule on DRE
Hematuria (rare)
Erectile dysfunction
Weight loss (advanced)
treatment:
1. Low-risk: active surveillance (preferred for Gleason <=6)
2. Intermediate-risk: radical prostatectomy or radiation + ADT
3. High-risk: radiation + long-term ADT (2-3 years)
4. Metastatic hormone-sensitive: ADT + docetaxel or novel hormonal agent
5. Castration-resistant: enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, radium-223
6. Genetic testing for BRCA (PARP inhibitor eligibility)
meds:
Leuprolide 22.5mg IM q3 months (LHRH agonist, ADT)
Degarelix 240mg SC then 80mg monthly (GnRH antagonist)
Enzalutamide 160mg daily
Abiraterone 1000mg daily + prednisone 5mg BID
Docetaxel 75mg/m2 IV q3wk
Olaparib 300mg BID (BRCA-mutated)
Radium-223 (bone metastases)
Zoledronic acid 4mg IV q3-4wk (bone protection)
ddx: BPH, Prostatitis, Bladder cancer, UTI, Urethral stricture, Neurogenic bladder
labs:
PSA (elevated, but not specific)
DRE
Transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy or MRI-fusion biopsy
Multiparametric prostate MRI (PI-RADS scoring)
Gleason score/Grade Group (biopsy)
Bone scan (if PSA >20 or high-risk)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
Testosterone level
Genetic testing: BRCA1/2, ATM, MSI
notes:
Most common cancer in men, second leading cause of cancer death
Screening: shared decision-making for PSA starting age 50 (or 40-45 if high-risk: Black race, family history)
Gleason score: sum of two most common patterns (3+3=6 favorable, 4+3=7 intermediate, 4+5=9 high-risk)
Active surveillance for low-risk: PSA q6mo, repeat biopsy q1-2 years
ADT side effects: hot flashes, osteoporosis, metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular risk, cognitive changes
Tumor flare with LHRH agonists: give antiandrogen cover for first 2 weeks
PSMA-PET: most sensitive imaging for recurrent/metastatic disease
description: Cancer of the prostate gland, the most common cancer in men, ranging from slow-growing tumors suitable for monitoring to aggressive disease requiring multimodal treatment.

name: Bladder Cancer
signs:
Painless gross hematuria (most common presenting symptom)
Frequency and urgency
Dysuria
Pelvic pain (advanced)
Weight loss
Flank pain (ureteral obstruction)
Lower extremity edema (pelvic lymphadenopathy)
treatment:
1. Non-muscle invasive (Ta, T1, CIS): TURBT + intravesical therapy
2. BCG intravesical instillation (standard for CIS and high-risk NMIBC)
3. Muscle-invasive (T2+): neoadjuvant cisplatin chemo then radical cystectomy
4. Metastatic: cisplatin-based chemo + immunotherapy
5. Trimodal therapy (TURBT + chemo + radiation) for bladder preservation
6. Urine diversion after cystectomy (ileal conduit or neobladder)
meds:
Intravesical BCG weekly x6 then maintenance
Intravesical gemcitabine 2g (alternative to BCG)
Cisplatin + gemcitabine (neoadjuvant/metastatic)
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk (BCG-unresponsive NMIBC or metastatic)
Avelumab 800mg IV q2wk (maintenance after chemo)
Enfortumab vedotin (metastatic, post-chemo)
Erdafitinib 8mg daily (FGFR-altered)
ddx: UTI, Renal calculi, Glomerulonephritis, Renal cell carcinoma, Prostate cancer (hematuria), BPH, Urothelial carcinoma of upper tract, Radiation cystitis
labs:
Urinalysis (hematuria)
Urine cytology
Cystoscopy with biopsy (diagnostic, gold standard)
CT urogram (upper tract evaluation)
CBC, BMP
LFTs
Chest CT (staging)
PET-CT (metastatic workup)
MRI pelvis (local staging)
