Urine FISH or molecular markers
notes:
Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma: 90% of bladder cancers
Risk factors: smoking (#1), occupational exposures (aromatic amines, dyes, rubber), cyclophosphamide, radiation
Non-muscle invasive: 75% of presentations, but 50-70% recur
BCG: live attenuated Mycobacterium bovis, most effective intravesical therapy for NMIBC
BCG shortage: alternative intravesical agents (gemcitabine + docetaxel)
Smoking cessation: reduces recurrence risk by 40%
CIS (carcinoma in situ): flat, high-grade, aggressive - BCG is standard
description: Cancer of the bladder lining, most commonly presenting as painless blood in the urine, strongly associated with smoking, and treated based on whether it has invaded the bladder muscle.

name: Cystic Fibrosis
signs:
Chronic productive cough
Recurrent pulmonary infections (Pseudomonas)
Failure to thrive
Steatorrhea (fatty, foul-smelling stools)
Digital clubbing
Nasal polyps
Meconium ileus (neonates)
Male infertility
Salty-tasting skin
treatment:
1. Airway clearance techniques (chest PT, vest therapy, PEP devices)
2. Inhaled mucolytics (dornase alfa, hypertonic saline)
3. CFTR modulators (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor - Trikafta)
4. Pancreatic enzyme replacement
5. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K)
6. Aggressive treatment of pulmonary exacerbations
7. Lung transplant for end-stage disease
meds:
Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta) daily (eligible mutations)
Dornase alfa 2.5mg nebulized daily
Hypertonic saline 7% nebulized BID
Albuterol nebulized before airway clearance
Pancrelipase (Creon) with meals and snacks
Azithromycin 500mg MWF (anti-inflammatory)
Tobramycin inhaled 300mg BID alternating months
Vitamins A, D, E, K supplementation
ddx: Primary ciliary dyskinesia, Bronchiectasis (other causes), Asthma, Immunodeficiency, Celiac disease, Shwachman-Diamond syndrome
labs:
Newborn screening (IRT + CFTR mutations)
Sweat chloride test (>=60 mmol/L diagnostic)
CFTR genotyping (>2000 mutations, F508del most common)
Sputum culture (Pseudomonas, S. aureus, B. cepacia)
PFTs (FEV1 monitoring)
Fecal elastase (pancreatic insufficiency)
Fat-soluble vitamin levels
OGTT annually after age 10 (CFRD screening)
Liver function tests
Chest CT
notes:
Autosomal recessive, CFTR gene (chromosome 7), F508del most common mutation (~70%)
Trikafta: revolutionary - eligible for ~90% of CF patients, dramatically improves lung function and outcomes
Median survival now >50 years (was ~30 in 2000s, improving rapidly with CFTR modulators)
Pseudomonas acquisition: chronic infection worsens prognosis
Burkholderia cepacia: poor prognosis, relative contraindication to transplant
CFRD (CF-related diabetes): unique pathophysiology, treat with insulin (not metformin)
Meconium ileus at birth: pathognomonic for CF
description: A genetic disorder affecting the lungs and digestive system, caused by mutations in the CFTR gene that produce thick, sticky mucus leading to chronic lung infections and pancreatic insufficiency.

name: Febrile Seizure
signs:
Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
Associated with fever (usually >100.4°F/38°C)
Age 6 months - 5 years
Seizure typically lasts <15 minutes
Post-ictal drowsiness
Rapid return to baseline
No focal neurological deficits
treatment:
1. Acute seizure: ensure safety, position on side
2. Benzodiazepine if seizure >5 minutes
3. Antipyretics for comfort (do NOT prevent febrile seizures)
4. Identify and treat source of fever
5. Reassurance to parents (benign prognosis)
6. No routine anti-seizure medication for simple febrile seizures
7. LP only if clinical concern for meningitis
meds:
Diazepam 0.5mg/kg rectal (acute seizure >5min)
Midazolam 0.2mg/kg intranasal or buccal
Acetaminophen 15mg/kg q4h PRN (comfort, does NOT prevent recurrence)
Ibuprofen 10mg/kg q6h PRN (comfort)
Antibiotics only if bacterial source identified
Daily anticonvulsants NOT recommended for simple FS
ddx: Meningitis/encephalitis, Epilepsy, Shaking/rigors, Breath-holding spell, Electrolyte disturbance, Intracranial pathology, Non-accidental trauma
labs:
Simple febrile seizure: no routine workup needed
Focus on fever source: UA, CXR, blood culture as indicated
LP: consider if <12 months (especially if unvaccinated), meningeal signs, complex FS, or pretreated with antibiotics
EEG NOT recommended for simple FS
BMP only if clinically indicated
CBC if source unclear
notes:
Simple FS: generalized, <15 min, once in 24h, age 6mo-5yr, normal development
Complex FS: focal, >15 min, recurs within 24h, or abnormal neuro exam
Recurrence risk: ~30% overall (50% if first FS <12 months)
Epilepsy risk after simple FS: 1-2% (same as general population)
Complex FS: slightly higher epilepsy risk but still low
Antipyretics do NOT prevent febrile seizures (multiple RCTs)
Parent education is crucial: most parents fear their child is dying during seizure
description: A seizure occurring in a young child triggered by fever, typically benign and self-limiting, not caused by epilepsy and not requiring long-term anti-seizure medication.

name: Neonatal Jaundice
signs:
Yellow discoloration of skin and sclerae
Progresses cephalocaudally (head → trunk → extremities)
Poor feeding
Lethargy
High-pitched cry (kernicterus)
Hypotonia then hypertonia (kernicterus)
Typically appears after 24h of life (physiologic)
treatment:
1. Phototherapy (first-line based on bilirubin nomogram)
2. Intensive phototherapy for rapid rise or high levels
3. Exchange transfusion for severe cases or failed phototherapy
4. Frequent feeding (breast milk or formula) q2-3h
5. Address underlying cause (hemolysis, infection)
6. IV fluids if dehydrated
7. Monitor bilirubin q4-24h based on level and trajectory
meds:
Phototherapy (blue-green light 430-490nm wavelength)
IVIG 0.5-1g/kg (isoimmune hemolytic disease)
IV fluids D10W if dehydrated
Phenobarbital (rarely, Crigler-Najjar)
Exchange transfusion: double-volume (170mL/kg)
No need for routine supplementation if breastfeeding well
ddx: Physiologic jaundice, Breast milk jaundice, ABO/Rh incompatibility, G6PD deficiency, Hereditary spherocytosis, Sepsis, Biliary atresia, Hypothyroidism, Cephalohematoma reabsorption, Crigler-Najjar
labs:
Total and direct bilirubin (plot on Bhutani nomogram)
Blood type and Coombs test (mother and infant)
CBC with reticulocyte count and peripheral smear
G6PD level
Transcutaneous bilirubinometry (screening)
Direct bilirubin (if elevated: think biliary atresia, TORCH)
Albumin level
TSH (newborn screen)
notes:
Physiologic: appears day 2-3, peaks day 3-5, resolves by 2 weeks
Pathologic: <24h, rate >5mg/dL/day, >95th percentile on nomogram, direct >1mg/dL or >20% of total
Kernicterus: bilirubin encephalopathy, acute (hypotonia, poor feeding) → chronic (choreoathetoid CP, hearing loss, dental enamel dysplasia)
Breast milk jaundice: prolonged unconjugated, peaks week 2, benign
Breastfeeding jaundice: inadequate intake, dehydration, first week
Bhutani nomogram: hour-specific bilirubin risk zones guide management
AAP phototherapy thresholds vary by gestational age and risk factors
description: Yellowing of a newborn's skin and eyes caused by elevated bilirubin levels, usually physiologic and harmless but requiring monitoring and treatment with phototherapy in severe cases to prevent brain damage.

name: Neonatal Sepsis
signs:
Temperature instability (fever or hypothermia)
Poor feeding
Lethargy or irritability
Respiratory distress (tachypnea, grunting, apnea)
Tachycardia or bradycardia
Hypotension
Jaundice
Abdominal distension
Seizures
Poor perfusion (mottling, delayed cap refill)
treatment:
1. Empiric antibiotics immediately after cultures obtained
2. Early-onset (<72h): ampicillin + gentamicin
3. Late-onset (>72h): vancomycin + gentamicin (or cefepime)
4. Respiratory support as needed
5. IV fluids and vasopressors for shock
6. Duration: 7-21 days depending on organism and site
7. LP when clinically stable
meds:
Ampicillin 50mg/kg IV q8-12h (early-onset)
Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg IV q24-48h (based on GA)
Vancomycin 15mg/kg IV q8-12h (late-onset)
Cefotaxime 50mg/kg IV q8h (meningitis doses)
Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV q8h (if HSV suspected)
Fluconazole (Candida prophylaxis in ELBW)
Dopamine/epinephrine for refractory shock
ddx: Transient tachypnea of newborn, RDS, Congenital heart disease, Inborn errors of metabolism, NEC, Congenital viral infection (TORCH), Maternal chorioamnionitis without neonatal infection
labs:
Blood culture (before antibiotics)
CBC with differential (immature:total neutrophil ratio)
CRP (serial, more useful for monitoring)
ProCalcitonin (rising)
UA and urine culture (late-onset)
LP and CSF analysis (cell count, protein, glucose, culture)
Chest XR if respiratory symptoms
BMP, blood gas
HSV PCR if vesicles or maternal history
notes:
Early-onset (<72h): GBS (#1), E. coli (#2), Listeria. Acquired during birth
Late-onset (>72h): CoNS (#1 in NICU), S. aureus, gram-negatives, Candida
GBS prophylaxis: intrapartum penicillin G if maternal GBS+ or risk factors
Rule of 2: if blood culture negative at 36-48h AND well-appearing, can stop antibiotics
HSV: consider if maternal genital herpes, vesicles, seizures, LFT abnormalities, CSF pleocytosis
NEC: bloody stools + abdominal distension + pneumatosis intestinalis
Mortality: 10-25% (higher in preterm, gram-negative, meningitis)
description: A serious bloodstream infection in newborns occurring within the first month of life, caused by bacteria acquired before, during, or after birth, requiring immediate antibiotic treatment.

name: Pertussis
signs:
Catarrhal phase (1-2 weeks): rhinorrhea, mild cough, low-grade fever
Paroxysmal phase (2-8 weeks): severe coughing fits with inspiratory whoop
Post-tussive vomiting
Cyanosis during paroxysms
Apnea (infants - may be only sign)
Subconjunctival hemorrhages from coughing
Convalescent phase: gradual improvement
treatment:
1. Macrolide antibiotics (reduce transmission, may shorten illness if given early)
2. Supportive care: oxygen, suctioning, hydration
3. Hospitalize infants <6 months
4. Post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts
5. Droplet precautions until 5 days of antibiotics
6. DTaP vaccination for prevention
meds:
Azithromycin 10mg/kg day 1 then 5mg/kg days 2-5 (preferred in infants <1 month)
Erythromycin 40mg/kg/day divided QID x14 days
TMP-SMX (macrolide-intolerant)
Supplemental oxygen PRN
IV fluids if unable to feed
Post-exposure prophylaxis: same antibiotics for household contacts
ddx: Viral URI, Bronchiolitis, Croup, Foreign body aspiration, Chlamydia pneumonia (infants), Mycoplasma, Tuberculosis, Reactive airway disease
labs:
Nasopharyngeal PCR (most sensitive, test of choice)
Nasopharyngeal culture on Regan-Lowe or Bordet-Gengou media (gold standard but slow)
CBC: marked lymphocytosis (>10,000-100,000 in infants - associated with mortality)
CXR: perihilar infiltrates or shaggy heart border
Pertussis serology (epidemiologic, not acute diagnosis)
notes:
Bordetella pertussis, 100-day cough
Infants <6 months: highest morbidity/mortality, may not have classic whoop
Leukocytosis with lymphocytosis: hallmark in infants, WBC >100K associated with mortality
Vaccination: DTaP (children), Tdap (adolescents/adults), Tdap in pregnancy (27-36 weeks each pregnancy)
Immunity wanes 5-10 years after vaccination → adolescent/adult cases increasing
Cocooning strategy: vaccinate family members around newborn
Post-exposure prophylaxis within 21 days for household contacts regardless of vaccination status
description: A highly contagious bacterial respiratory infection known as 'whooping cough,' causing severe coughing fits that can be life-threatening in young infants who may not yet be fully vaccinated.

name: Measles
signs:
Prodrome: high fever (often >104°F), cough, coryza, conjunctivitis (3 C's)
Koplik spots (white spots on buccal mucosa - pathognomonic)
Maculopapular rash starting on face, spreads downward
Rash appears ~day 4 of illness
Fever peaks with rash onset then improves
Lymphadenopathy
Pharyngitis
treatment:
1. Supportive care (fluids, antipyretics, rest)
2. Vitamin A supplementation (reduces mortality and complications)
3. Airborne isolation until 4 days after rash onset
4. Report to public health
5. Post-exposure prophylaxis: MMR vaccine within 72h or immunoglobulin within 6 days
6. Treat complications (pneumonia, encephalitis)
meds:
Vitamin A: 200,000 IU PO x2 days (children >12 months)
Vitamin A: 100,000 IU (6-12 months), 50,000 IU (<6 months)
Acetaminophen for fever
IV fluids if dehydrated
Antibiotics only for bacterial superinfection
MMR vaccine for post-exposure prophylaxis (within 72h of exposure)
ddx: Rubella, Roseola, Scarlet fever, Kawasaki disease, Drug eruption, Mononucleosis, Parvovirus B19, Rocky Mountain spotted fever
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (in endemic settings)
Measles IgM antibody (positive 3 days after rash)
Measles RNA PCR (nasopharyngeal, throat, urine)
CBC (leukopenia, lymphopenia)
CXR if pneumonia suspected
Viral culture (reference labs)
notes:
Highly contagious: R0 = 12-18, airborne transmission, infectious 4 days before to 4 days after rash
Complications: pneumonia (#1 cause of death), encephalitis (1/1000), SSPE (rare, years later)
Vaccination: MMR at 12-15 months and 4-6 years (97% effective after 2 doses)
Immunocompromised: giant cell pneumonia, can be fatal, no rash (modified measles)
Reportable disease - immediate notification to public health
Vitamin A deficiency increases measles mortality - supplement all cases
SSPE (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis): progressive neurodegeneration, occurs 7-10 years post-measles
description: A highly contagious viral infection causing high fever, cough, and a characteristic spreading rash, preventable by vaccination but potentially causing serious complications including pneumonia and brain inflammation.

name: Scarlet Fever
signs:
Sandpaper-like erythematous rash
Strawberry tongue (white then red)
Pastia lines (linear petechiae in skin folds)
Circumoral pallor
Fever
Sore throat
Cervical lymphadenopathy
Desquamation of fingertips (late, 1-2 weeks)
treatment:
1. Penicillin or amoxicillin (first-line)
2. Treat for 10 days to prevent rheumatic fever
3. Supportive care: fluids, antipyretics, soft diet
4. May return to school 24h after starting antibiotics
5. Monitor for complications
meds:
Amoxicillin 50mg/kg/day divided BID x10 days
Penicillin V 250mg BID-TID x10 days (weight-based)
Benzathine penicillin G 600,000-1.2M units IM x1
Azithromycin 12mg/kg day 1 then 6mg/kg days 2-5 (penicillin allergy)
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for fever and pain
ddx: Viral exanthem, Kawasaki disease, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Drug eruption, Toxic shock syndrome, Measles, Mononucleosis
labs:
Rapid strep antigen test (throat swab)
Throat culture (gold standard if rapid test negative)
ASO titer and anti-DNase B (if post-strep complications suspected)
CBC
Urinalysis (post-strep glomerulonephritis screening 1-2 weeks after)
notes:
Caused by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) producing erythrogenic toxin
Typically ages 5-15 years
Complication prevention: full antibiotic course prevents acute rheumatic fever (NOT PSGN)
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis: 1-3 weeks after, hematuria, not prevented by antibiotics
Rheumatic fever: 2-4 weeks after, prevented by timely antibiotic treatment
Sandpaper rash: characteristic texture, blanches with pressure
Pastia lines persist after rash fades - helpful late diagnostic clue
description: A bacterial illness caused by Group A Streptococcus that produces a distinctive sandpaper-textured rash, strawberry tongue, and fever, treated with antibiotics to prevent serious complications.

name: Pancreatic Cancer
signs:
Painless jaundice (head of pancreas)
Weight loss
Epigastric pain radiating to back
New-onset diabetes
Steatorrhea
Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder)
Migratory thrombophlebitis (Trousseau syndrome)
Depression
Anorexia
treatment:
1. Surgical resection if resectable (only 15-20% at diagnosis)
2. Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) for head tumors
3. Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (borderline resectable)
4. Adjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX after resection
5. Palliative chemotherapy for metastatic disease
6. Biliary stenting for obstructive jaundice
7. Pain management (celiac plexus block)
8. Palliative care integration
meds:
Modified FOLFIRINOX (5-FU, leucovorin, irinotecan, oxaliplatin)
Gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel (alternative first-line)
Gemcitabine monotherapy (poor performance status)
Olaparib (BRCA-mutated, maintenance)
Pembrolizumab (MSI-H, rare)
Pancreatic enzyme replacement (exocrine insufficiency)
Celiac plexus block or neurolysis for pain
ddx: Cholangiocarcinoma, Ampullary carcinoma, Chronic pancreatitis, Autoimmune pancreatitis, Pancreatic cyst/IPMN, CBD stone, Gastric cancer
labs:
CT abdomen with pancreatic protocol (triple-phase)
CA 19-9 (tumor marker, not screening)
EUS with FNA (tissue diagnosis, staging)
MRI/MRCP (biliary anatomy)
CBC, BMP, LFTs (obstructive pattern)
Bilirubin (elevated with head tumors)
Chest CT (staging)
PET-CT (metastatic workup)
Genetic testing: BRCA1/2, PALB2, MLH1, MSH2
notes:
5-year survival: ~10% overall (worst of common cancers)
Adenocarcinoma: 85% of pancreatic cancers, head location most common (60-70%)
Resectability: borderline if involves SMA/celiac ≤180°, unresectable if >180° or distant mets
CA 19-9: useful for monitoring treatment response, not screening (false-positive in jaundice)
Risk factors: smoking, chronic pancreatitis, family history, obesity, new-onset diabetes after 50
Autoimmune pancreatitis (IgG4): important mimic, responds to steroids
Whipple mortality: <5% at high-volume centers, but high morbidity
description: A highly aggressive cancer of the pancreas with poor prognosis, often diagnosed late due to vague symptoms, with surgical removal possible in only a minority of cases.

name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found on screening in cirrhotics)
RUQ pain or fullness
Weight loss
Hepatomegaly
Ascites (new or worsening)
Jaundice
Paraneoplastic: erythrocytosis, hypercalcemia, hypoglycemia
Bloody ascites
Hepatic bruit
treatment:
1. Surgical resection (non-cirrhotic or well-compensated cirrhosis)
2. Liver transplant (Milan criteria: single ≤5cm or up to 3 lesions each ≤3cm)
3. Locoregional: RFA, TACE, Y-90 radioembolization
4. Systemic: atezolizumab + bevacizumab (first-line advanced)
5. Sorafenib or lenvatinib (alternative first-line)
6. Curative intent transplant cures both cancer and cirrhosis
meds:
Atezolizumab 1200mg + bevacizumab 15mg/kg IV q3wk (first-line advanced)
Sorafenib 400mg BID
Lenvatinib 8-12mg daily
Cabozantinib 60mg daily (second-line)
Regorafenib 160mg daily (second-line after sorafenib)
Nivolumab + ipilimumab (second-line)
ddx: Hepatic metastases, Cholangiocarcinoma, Hepatic adenoma, FNH, Hemangioma, Regenerative/dysplastic nodule
labs:
Ultrasound q6 months (surveillance in cirrhotics)
AFP (elevated but nonspecific)
Triple-phase CT or MRI with contrast (arterial enhancement with washout = diagnostic)
LI-RADS classification
LFTs, AFP, MELD score
Hepatitis B/C serologies
Biopsy only if imaging inconclusive (risk of seeding)
Chest CT (staging)
notes:
Leading cause of cancer death worldwide
Surveillance: US + AFP q6 months in cirrhosis patients (any etiology)
Diagnosis often by imaging alone (no biopsy needed if classic enhancement pattern on CT/MRI)
Milan criteria for transplant: single ≤5cm or 2-3 nodules each ≤3cm, no vascular invasion, no extrahepatic disease
BCLC staging guides treatment: 0/A (curative), B (TACE), C (systemic), D (palliative)
AFP >400 highly suggestive but only 50% sensitive
Hep B: HCC can occur WITHOUT cirrhosis (screen HBV carriers regardless)
Risk: HCV cirrhosis (1-4%/year), HBV, alcohol, NASH cirrhosis (increasing)
description: The most common primary liver cancer, usually developing in the setting of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis, with surveillance enabling early detection and potentially curative treatment.

name: Gastric Cancer
signs:
Epigastric pain
Weight loss
Early satiety
Nausea/vomiting
Dysphagia (proximal tumors)
Melena or hematemesis
Virchow node (left supraclavicular)
Sister Mary Joseph nodule (periumbilical)
Blumer shelf (palpable rectal shelf)
Krukenberg tumor (ovarian metastasis)
treatment:
1. Surgical resection (subtotal or total gastrectomy) for localized disease
2. D2 lymph node dissection
3. Perioperative chemo (FLOT regimen) for locally advanced
4. Adjuvant chemoradiation (MacDonald regimen, alternative)
5. Metastatic: systemic therapy based on molecular profile
6. HER2+: trastuzumab + chemotherapy
7. PD-L1+/MSI-H: immunotherapy
meds:
FLOT: 5-FU + leucovorin + oxaliplatin + docetaxel (perioperative)
Capecitabine + oxaliplatin (XELOX)
Trastuzumab 6mg/kg IV q3wk (HER2+ metastatic)
Pembrolizumab (PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or MSI-H)
Nivolumab (adjuvant after neoadjuvant chemo + surgery)
Ramucirumab + paclitaxel (second-line)
Trastuzumab deruxtecan (HER2+ second-line)
ddx: Peptic ulcer disease, GERD, Functional dyspepsia, Gastric lymphoma (MALT), GIST, Pancreatic cancer, Gastric polyps
labs:
EGD with multiple biopsies (diagnostic)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
EUS (T and N staging)
PET-CT (metastatic workup)
CBC (anemia)
LFTs, albumin
HER2 testing (IHC/FISH)
PD-L1 CPS
MSI/dMMR testing
CEA, CA 19-9
H. pylori testing
notes:
Leading cause of cancer death globally (especially Asia, South America)
Intestinal type: better prognosis, associated with H. pylori, atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia
Diffuse type: worse prognosis, linitis plastica (leather bottle stomach), signet ring cells
H. pylori: WHO class I carcinogen, eradication reduces risk
Hereditary diffuse gastric cancer: CDH1 mutation, prophylactic gastrectomy recommended
Classic metastatic signs: Virchow (left supraclavicular), Krukenberg (ovarian), Sister Mary Joseph (umbilical)
Screening in high-risk countries (Japan, Korea): regular EGD detects early-stage disease
description: Cancer of the stomach lining, often diagnosed at advanced stages in Western countries, strongly associated with H. pylori infection and treated with surgery and chemotherapy.

name: Ovarian Cancer
signs:
Often asymptomatic until advanced (silent killer)
Abdominal bloating and distension
Pelvic/abdominal pain
Early satiety
Urinary frequency
Ascites
Weight loss
Palpable adnexal mass
Bowel obstruction (advanced)
treatment:
1. Surgical staging and debulking (goal: no visible residual disease)
2. Total abdominal hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy + omentectomy
3. Adjuvant carboplatin + paclitaxel x6 cycles
4. PARP inhibitor maintenance (BRCA-mutated or HRD+)
5. Bevacizumab (advanced disease)
6. Neoadjuvant chemo if unresectable at presentation
7. Genetic testing for all epithelial ovarian cancer
meds:
Carboplatin AUC 5-6 + paclitaxel 175mg/m2 IV q3wk x6
Olaparib 300mg BID (PARP inhibitor, BRCA+ maintenance)
Niraparib 200-300mg daily (maintenance, all comers)
Bevacizumab 15mg/kg IV q3wk
Pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (recurrent)
Gemcitabine + carboplatin (platinum-sensitive recurrence)
ddx: Ovarian cyst, Endometriosis, Uterine fibroids, Tubo-ovarian abscess, GI malignancy (peritoneal carcinomatosis), Primary peritoneal cancer, Fallopian tube cancer
labs:
CA-125 (elevated in 80% of advanced, not screening in general population)
Pelvic ultrasound (complex adnexal mass)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
Paracentesis with cytology (if ascites)
CBC, BMP, LFTs
HE4 (supplementary marker)
Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI)
BRCA1/2 and HRD testing (all patients)
Genetic counseling and panel testing
notes:
5th leading cause of cancer death in women, most lethal gynecologic malignancy
70% diagnosed at stage III-IV (lack of effective screening)
Epithelial (90%): high-grade serous most common subtype
BRCA1/2 mutations: 15-20% of epithelial ovarian cancers (PARP inhibitor eligibility)
Risk-reducing BSO recommended for BRCA carriers (by age 35-40 for BRCA1, 40-45 for BRCA2)
CA-125: not recommended for screening (poor specificity), useful for monitoring treatment response
Platinum-free interval: >6 months = platinum-sensitive (better prognosis)
Optimal debulking (<1cm residual) = most important surgical prognostic factor
description: Cancer originating in the ovaries, often called the 'silent killer' because it frequently goes undetected until advanced stages, with BRCA gene mutations significantly increasing risk.

name: Diabetes Mellitus Type 1
signs:
Polyuria and polydipsia
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Fatigue
Blurred vision
DKA at presentation (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing)
Young age at onset
No obesity (typically)
treatment:
1. Insulin therapy (mandatory, lifelong)
2. Basal-bolus regimen or insulin pump
3. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)
4. Carbohydrate counting
5. HbA1c target <7%
6. Screen for complications annually
7. Screen for autoimmune comorbidities (thyroid, celiac)
meds:
Insulin glargine 0.2-0.4 units/kg SC daily (basal)
Insulin lispro/aspart dosed per carb ratio (bolus, ~1:10-1:15)
Insulin pump therapy (continuous SC infusion)
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (severe hypoglycemia)
Automated insulin delivery (hybrid closed-loop systems)
ddx: Type 2 diabetes, LADA, MODY, Secondary diabetes (pancreatitis, CF), DKA from other causes
labs:
Random glucose >=200 + symptoms, FPG >=126, or HbA1c >=6.5%
C-peptide (low/absent)
GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA antibodies (autoimmune markers)
HbA1c q3 months
Annual: urine albumin, lipids, TSH, celiac serologies
Comprehensive metabolic panel
notes:
Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
Autoantibodies positive in 90%+ at diagnosis
Honeymoon period: partial remission after starting insulin (months to 1-2 years)
DKA prevention: never stop basal insulin, sick day rules
Hypoglycemia unawareness: reduced counterregulatory response with recurrent lows
Type 1 screening: now possible (teplizumab can delay onset in stage 2)
Associated autoimmune: thyroiditis (25%), celiac (5-10%), Addison's, vitiligo
description: An autoimmune disease where the immune system destroys insulin-producing cells in the pancreas, requiring lifelong insulin therapy to manage blood sugar levels.

name: Addison's Disease
signs:
Fatigue and weakness
Hyperpigmentation (skin creases, gums, scars)
Weight loss and anorexia
Hypotension and orthostasis
Salt craving
Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia
Adrenal crisis: shock, altered consciousness
treatment:
1. Glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone)
2. Mineralocorticoid replacement (fludrocortisone)
3. Stress dosing during illness/surgery
4. Medical alert bracelet
5. Emergency injectable hydrocortisone for adrenal crisis
6. Patient education on sick day rules
meds:
Hydrocortisone 15-25mg daily (divided: 2/3 AM, 1/3 afternoon)
Fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2mg daily
Stress dosing: hydrocortisone 50-100mg IV/IM q8h
DHEA 25-50mg daily (women, debated)
Emergency kit: hydrocortisone 100mg IM
ddx: Secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary), Chronic fatigue syndrome, Depression, Hypothyroidism, Anorexia nervosa, Hemochromatosis
labs:
Morning cortisol (low <3mcg/dL diagnostic, >18 rules out)
ACTH stimulation test (gold standard: cortisol <18 at 30/60min)
ACTH level (elevated in primary, low in secondary)
BMP (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia)
21-hydroxylase antibodies (autoimmune)
Adrenal CT (hemorrhage, infiltration, calcification)
Renin and aldosterone (elevated renin, low aldo)
TSH, CBC
notes:
Primary (Addison's): autoimmune most common in developed countries (70-90%), also TB, adrenal hemorrhage
Secondary: pituitary/hypothalamic disease, chronic steroid use then abrupt withdrawal
Adrenal crisis: life-threatening - give hydrocortisone 100mg IV immediately, do not wait for labs
Autoimmune polyglandular syndrome: Addison's + thyroid disease + type 1 DM
Hyperpigmentation: ACTH cross-reacts with melanocortin receptors (absent in secondary)
Sick day rules: double or triple oral dose during illness, IM injection if vomiting
Waterhouse-Friderichsen: bilateral adrenal hemorrhage from meningococcemia
description: A rare condition where the adrenal glands don't produce enough cortisol and aldosterone, causing fatigue, low blood pressure, and darkening of the skin, with risk of life-threatening adrenal crisis.

name: Hyperparathyroidism
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found incidentally on labs)
Bones: bone pain, osteoporosis, fractures
Stones: renal calculi
Abdominal groans: nausea, constipation, pancreatitis
Psychiatric moans: depression, confusion, fatigue
Polyuria and polydipsia
treatment:
1. Surgery (parathyroidectomy) for symptomatic or meeting criteria
2. Monitoring for asymptomatic patients not meeting surgical criteria
3. Hydration
4. Calcimimetics for non-surgical candidates
5. Bisphosphonates for osteoporosis
meds:
Cinacalcet 30-90mg BID (calcimimetic, non-surgical candidates)
Alendronate 70mg weekly (osteoporosis)
IV normal saline (hypercalcemic crisis)
Furosemide 20-40mg IV (after adequate hydration)
Calcitonin 4 IU/kg SC q12h (acute hypercalcemia)
ddx: Malignancy-associated hypercalcemia (PTHrP), Vitamin D excess, Granulomatous disease, Thiazide-induced, Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, Milk-alkali syndrome
labs:
Serum calcium elevated
PTH elevated (inappropriately normal = also diagnostic)
24h urine calcium (distinguish from FHH)
Vitamin D level
Phosphorus (low)
Alkaline phosphatase
BMP (creatinine)
DEXA scan
Renal ultrasound (nephrolithiasis)
Sestamibi scan (localize adenoma pre-surgery)
4D CT parathyroid (alternative localization)
notes:
Bones, Stones, Abdominal groans, Psychiatric moans (classic mnemonic)
Primary: single adenoma (85%), hyperplasia (15%), carcinoma (<1%)
Surgical indications: symptomatic, Ca >1mg/dL above normal, age <50, osteoporosis, eGFR <60, renal stones, 24h urine Ca >400
FHH: benign, autosomal dominant, low urine calcium (Ca/Cr clearance ratio <0.01) - do NOT operate
Normocalcemic PHPT: elevated PTH with normal calcium - emerging entity
Secondary hyperparathyroidism: CKD-related, PTH elevated with low/normal calcium
Tertiary: autonomous PTH after long-standing secondary (post-transplant)
description: A condition where one or more parathyroid glands produce too much parathyroid hormone, causing elevated calcium levels that can affect bones, kidneys, and mental function.

name: Diabetes Insipidus
signs:
Polyuria (3-20 L/day of dilute urine)
Polydipsia (intense thirst)
Nocturia
Dehydration if unable to access water
Hypernatremia
Preference for ice-cold water
Dilute urine despite dehydration
treatment:
1. Central DI: desmopressin (DDAVP)
2. Nephrogenic DI: treat underlying cause, low-solute diet, thiazides
3. Ensure adequate fluid access
4. Monitor sodium and urine output
5. Address underlying cause
meds:
Desmopressin (DDAVP) 10-40mcg intranasal or 0.1-0.4mg PO BID-TID (central DI)
Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily (nephrogenic DI - paradoxical antidiuresis)
Amiloride 5-10mg BID (lithium-induced nephrogenic DI)
Indomethacin 25mg TID (adjunct for nephrogenic DI)
Free water replacement
ddx: Primary polydipsia (psychogenic), Diabetes mellitus, Hypercalcemia, Hypokalemia, CKD, Beer potomania
labs:
Serum sodium (elevated or high-normal)
Serum osmolality (>295 mOsm/kg)
Urine osmolality (inappropriately dilute <300 in face of high serum osm)
Urine specific gravity (<1.005)
Water deprivation test (gold standard)
Desmopressin challenge (distinguishes central vs nephrogenic)
Copeptin levels (emerging test)
Brain MRI (pituitary/hypothalamic lesion)
BMP, calcium, potassium
notes:
Central: lack of ADH production (pituitary surgery, tumor, infiltrative, trauma, idiopathic)
Nephrogenic: kidney resistant to ADH (lithium most common drug cause, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, genetic)
Water deprivation test: if urine concentrates with desmopressin = central; if not = nephrogenic
Post-surgical DI: often transient after pituitary surgery (triphasic response)
Bright spot on MRI: posterior pituitary, absent in central DI
Desmopressin: risk of hyponatremia if overcorrected - allow breakthrough polyuria
Gestational DI: placental vasopressinase degrades ADH
description: A condition characterized by excessive urination and intense thirst caused by either insufficient production of antidiuretic hormone (central) or the kidneys' inability to respond to it (nephrogenic).

name: Metabolic Syndrome
signs:
Central obesity (waist >40in men, >35in women)
Elevated blood pressure (>=130/85)
Elevated fasting glucose (>=100)
Elevated triglycerides (>=150)
Low HDL (<40 men, <50 women)
Acanthosis nigricans
Skin tags
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modification (cornerstone): diet, exercise, weight loss
2. Target 7-10% weight loss
3. Mediterranean or DASH diet
4. 150 min/week moderate-intensity exercise
5. Treat individual components as needed
6. GLP-1 RA or bariatric surgery for obesity
7. Cardiovascular risk assessment
meds:
Metformin 500-1000mg BID (if prediabetes/diabetes)
Atorvastatin 10-40mg daily (dyslipidemia)
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily (hypertension)
Semaglutide 2.4mg SC weekly (weight management)
Fenofibrate 145mg daily (hypertriglyceridemia if >500)
Icosapent ethyl 2g BID (elevated TG + CVD risk)
ddx: Cushing syndrome, Hypothyroidism, PCOS, Lipodystrophy, Medication-induced (antipsychotics, steroids)
labs:
Fasting glucose and HbA1c
Fasting lipid panel
Blood pressure measurement
Waist circumference
BMP
LFTs (NAFLD screening)
TSH
Uric acid
notes:
Defined by >=3 of 5 criteria (IDF/NHLBI harmonized definition)
Affects ~35% of US adults
Doubles cardiovascular disease risk, 5x diabetes risk
NAFLD/NASH: hepatic manifestation of metabolic syndrome
Insulin resistance is the underlying pathophysiology
Visceral adiposity more metabolically harmful than subcutaneous
Bariatric surgery: most effective intervention for severe obesity with metabolic syndrome
GLP-1 RA: cardiovascular benefit beyond weight loss (SELECT trial with semaglutide)
description: A cluster of interconnected metabolic abnormalities including central obesity, high blood pressure, high blood sugar, and abnormal cholesterol levels that together significantly increase the risk of heart disease, stroke, and diabetes.

name: Polycystic Kidney Disease
signs:
Bilateral flank masses
Hypertension (often presenting sign)
Flank or abdominal pain
Gross hematuria
Recurrent UTIs
Renal calculi
Hepatic cysts
Intracranial aneurysms
Mitral valve prolapse
treatment:
1. Blood pressure control (ACEi/ARB preferred, target <110/75 if eGFR >60)
2. Tolvaptan (slows cyst growth and eGFR decline)
3. Adequate hydration (>3L/day, suppress vasopressin)
4. Low sodium diet
5. Avoid caffeine (may stimulate cyst growth)
6. Pain management (avoid NSAIDs)
7. Renal replacement therapy/transplant for ESRD
meds:
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily (BP control)
Tolvaptan 45-120mg daily (V2 receptor antagonist, slows progression)
Acetaminophen for pain (avoid NSAIDs)
Antibiotics for cyst infections (fluoroquinolones penetrate cysts)
Statins as needed
ddx: Simple renal cysts, Autosomal recessive PKD, Tuberous sclerosis, Von Hippel-Lindau, Acquired cystic disease (dialysis), Medullary sponge kidney
labs:
Renal ultrasound (bilateral enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts)
Revised Ravine criteria (age-dependent cyst numbers)
CT or MRI (total kidney volume - htTKV for prognosis)
BMP (creatinine, eGFR monitoring)
Urinalysis
Genetic testing (PKD1, PKD2)
MRA brain (screen for intracranial aneurysm if family history)
LFTs
Lipid panel
notes:
ADPKD: PKD1 (85%, worse prognosis, ESRD ~54yo) vs PKD2 (15%, milder, ESRD ~74yo)
Most common inherited kidney disease (1/400-1/1000)
Tolvaptan (TEMPO trial): slows GFR decline 1mL/min/year, monitor LFTs (hepatotoxicity)
Intracranial aneurysm: screen with MRA if family history of SAH/aneurysm, rupture risk 5x general population
Cyst infection: fever + flank pain, CT/PET may localize, fluoroquinolones preferred (cyst penetration)
Total kidney volume (htTKV) >600mL by MRI predicts rapid progression
Native nephrectomy may be needed before transplant (massive kidneys)
description: A genetic disorder causing numerous fluid-filled cysts to develop in the kidneys, progressively enlarging them and eventually leading to kidney failure, most commonly inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.

name: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome
signs:
Triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + acute kidney injury
Bloody diarrhea (prodrome in typical HUS)
Oliguria or anuria
Pallor and jaundice
Petechiae
Hypertension
Seizures (severe)
Edema
treatment:
1. Supportive care is mainstay for typical (STEC-HUS)
2. IV fluids and electrolyte management
3. Dialysis for severe AKI
4. Transfuse PRBCs if symptomatic anemia (Hgb <6-7)
5. Avoid antibiotics for STEC infection (may worsen HUS)
6. Avoid platelet transfusion unless active bleeding
7. Atypical HUS: eculizumab (complement inhibitor)
meds:
IV normal saline (volume expansion early)
PRBC transfusion (Hgb <6-7)
Eculizumab 900mg IV weekly x4 then 1200mg q2wk (atypical HUS)
Ravulizumab (longer-acting complement inhibitor)
Antihypertensives (amlodipine, labetalol)
Dialysis support
Avoid: antibiotics for STEC, anti-motility agents, platelet transfusions
ddx: TTP (more neurologic, ADAMTS13 <10%), DIC, Malignant hypertension, SLE nephritis, Preeclampsia/HELLP, ITP, Scleroderma renal crisis
labs:
CBC (anemia, thrombocytopenia)
Peripheral smear (schistocytes, helmet cells)
BMP (elevated creatinine, electrolyte abnormalities)
LDH elevated
Haptoglobin decreased
Direct Coombs negative
Reticulocyte count elevated
Stool culture and Shiga toxin assay
ADAMTS13 activity (>10% = not TTP)
Complement studies (C3, C4, factor H, factor I, MCP) for atypical
Urinalysis (hematuria, proteinuria)
notes:
Typical (STEC-HUS): 90% of pediatric HUS, E. coli O157:H7 (Shiga toxin), self-limited in most
Atypical HUS: complement-mediated, no diarrheal prodrome, recurrent, treat with complement inhibitor
Antibiotic use in STEC may increase HUS risk by increasing toxin release
STEC-HUS: supportive care, 90% recover renal function
Atypical HUS: without complement inhibition, 50% progress to ESRD
Differentiate from TTP by ADAMTS13 activity: <10% = TTP, >10% = HUS
Meningococcal vaccination required before eculizumab (blocks terminal complement)
description: A condition where damaged red blood cells clog the kidneys' tiny blood vessels, causing kidney failure, most commonly triggered in children by certain strains of E. coli bacteria.

name: Spinal Stenosis
signs:
Neurogenic claudication (leg pain/heaviness with walking)
Improves with sitting or leaning forward (shopping cart sign)
Bilateral leg symptoms (often asymmetric)
Low back pain
Numbness and tingling in legs
Weakness (advanced)
Normal pulses (distinguishes from vascular claudication)
treatment:
1. Conservative first: PT, NSAIDs, activity modification
2. Epidural steroid injections for temporary relief
3. Gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic pain
4. Surgical decompression (laminectomy) for refractory cases
5. Fusion if concurrent instability/spondylolisthesis
6. Weight management and core strengthening
meds:
NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID)
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID
Epidural steroid injection (fluoroscopic-guided)
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Duloxetine 60mg daily (neuropathic pain)
Calcitonin nasal spray 200IU daily (limited evidence)
ddx: Peripheral arterial disease (vascular claudication), Lumbar disc herniation, Peripheral neuropathy, Hip osteoarthritis, Cauda equina syndrome, Trochanteric bursitis
labs:
MRI lumbar spine (gold standard - AP canal diameter, thecal sac area)
CT myelography (if MRI contraindicated)
XR lumbar spine (spondylolisthesis, alignment)
EMG/NCV (differentiate from neuropathy)
ABI (rule out PAD if vascular claudication suspected)
notes:
Most common reason for spine surgery in patients >65
Neurogenic vs vascular claudication: neurogenic improves with flexion/sitting, vascular improves with standing still
Shopping cart sign: patients lean forward on cart (flexion opens spinal canal)
Degenerative: facet hypertrophy, ligamentum flavum thickening, disc bulging
SPORT trial: surgery superior to conservative at 4 years for symptoms, but high crossover rate
Spondylolisthesis (vertebral slippage): may require fusion in addition to decompression
Cauda equina syndrome: emergency - bowel/bladder dysfunction requires urgent decompression
description: Narrowing of the spinal canal in the lower back that compresses the nerves, causing leg pain and weakness when walking that is relieved by sitting or bending forward.

name: Cervical Radiculopathy
signs:
Neck pain radiating to arm
Dermatomal numbness/tingling
Weakness in specific myotome
Decreased deep tendon reflexes
Spurling test positive (head extension + rotation reproduces symptoms)
Relief with arm abduction (shoulder abduction sign)
Neck stiffness
treatment:
1. Conservative management first (80-90% improve)
2. NSAIDs and activity modification
3. Cervical collar for comfort (short-term only)
4. Physical therapy (traction, strengthening)
5. Cervical epidural steroid injection
6. Surgery (ACDF or posterior foraminotomy) for refractory or progressive deficit
meds:
NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID, meloxicam 15mg daily)
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Pregabalin 75-150mg BID
Methylprednisolone dose pack (acute, short course)
Cyclobenzaprine 10mg TID (muscle spasm)
Epidural steroid injection (interlaminar or transforaminal)
ddx: Cervical myelopathy, Thoracic outlet syndrome, Rotator cuff pathology, Carpal tunnel syndrome, Peripheral neuropathy, Brachial plexopathy, Pancoast tumor
labs:
MRI cervical spine (disc herniation, foraminal stenosis)
CT (bony detail if surgery planned)
XR cervical spine (alignment, disc space narrowing)
EMG/NCV (if diagnosis uncertain, >6 weeks)
notes:
C6 root (C5-C6 disc): biceps weakness, decreased biceps reflex, thumb/index numbness
C7 root (C6-C7 disc): most common, triceps weakness, decreased triceps reflex, middle finger numbness
C8 root (C7-T1 disc): grip weakness, finger flexor weakness, ring/pinky numbness
Spurling test: most specific physical exam maneuver
ACDF: anterior cervical discectomy and fusion - most common surgery
Myelopathy signs (cord compression): gait ataxia, hand clumsiness, Hoffmann sign, hyperreflexia → consider surgery
Red flags: progressive weakness, myelopathy, bowel/bladder symptoms → urgent MRI
description: Compression of a nerve root in the neck causing pain, numbness, and weakness radiating down the arm, most commonly from a herniated disc or bone spur.

name: Snakebite Envenomation
signs:
Fang marks
Local pain, swelling, and ecchymosis (progressive)
Bleeding from bite site
Coagulopathy (bleeding from gums, IV sites)
Nausea/vomiting
Metallic taste
Fasciculations (elapid/neurotoxic)
Ptosis and respiratory failure (elapid)
Compartment syndrome (rare)
treatment:
1. Immobilize affected extremity below heart level
2. Remove jewelry/constricting items
3. Antivenom (CroFab or Anavip for pit vipers in US)
4. Serial labs q6h (coagulopathy may recur)
5. Tetanus prophylaxis
6. Do NOT: tourniquet, incision, suction, ice
7. Observe minimum 8-12h even if initially dry bite
meds:
CroFab 4-6 vials IV initial, repeat q1h until control
Anavip 10 vials IV initial (longer half-life)
Maintenance: CroFab 2 vials at 6, 12, 18h
Morphine 2-4mg IV PRN (pain)
Tetanus prophylaxis
Antibiotics only if secondary infection
Avoid: aspirin, NSAIDs (worsen coagulopathy)
ddx: Non-venomous snakebite, Spider bite, Insect sting, Cellulitis, Allergic reaction, DIC (other causes)
labs:
CBC with platelets (serial q6h)
PT/INR, PTT, fibrinogen (coagulopathy)
BMP (rhabdomyolysis, renal function)
CPK
Type and screen
Wound cultures (if infection suspected)
Urine myoglobin
Serial circumference measurements of affected extremity (swelling progression)
notes:
US pit vipers: rattlesnake (most severe), copperhead (usually mild), cottonmouth (water moccasin)
Coral snake (elapid): neurotoxic, delayed onset, may need different antivenom
CroFab: Fab fragments, shorter half-life → recurrent coagulopathy in 50% (need maintenance dosing)
Anavip: F(ab')2, longer half-life, less recurrence
25% of pit viper bites are dry (no envenomation) - still observe
Indications for antivenom: progressive swelling, coagulopathy, systemic symptoms
Compartment syndrome: rare, check pressures before fasciotomy (antivenom often sufficient)
description: Poisoning from a venomous snake bite that can cause progressive tissue damage, blood clotting problems, and potentially life-threatening systemic effects, treated with specific antivenom.

name: Cyanide Poisoning
signs:
Rapid onset altered consciousness
Seizures
Bitter almond odor (not all can detect)
Cherry-red skin (classic but unreliable)
Tachycardia then bradycardia
Hypotension
Lactic acidosis despite normal PaO2
Respiratory failure
Cardiac arrest
treatment:
1. Remove from exposure, decontaminate
2. 100% oxygen
3. Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit) - preferred antidote
4. Sodium thiosulfate
5. Sodium nitrite (alternative, risk of methemoglobinemia)
6. Supportive care: intubation, vasopressors
7. Consider concurrent CO poisoning in fire victims
meds:
Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV over 15min (preferred antidote)
Sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV over 10-20min
Sodium nitrite 300mg (10mL of 3%) IV over 5min (caution if CO co-exposure)
Amyl nitrite pearls inhaled (temporizing)
100% O2 via NRB or intubation
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV (acidosis)
Epinephrine for cardiac arrest/hypotension
ddx: Carbon monoxide poisoning, Hydrogen sulfide, Methemoglobinemia, Sepsis, Metabolic acidosis (other), Seizure disorder, Cardiac arrest (other)
labs:
ABG/VBG: lactic acidosis with narrow A-V O2 difference (venous blood appears arterial)
Lactate markedly elevated
Cyanide level (send but don't wait for results - treat empirically)
CO-oximetry (concurrent CO in fire)
Methemoglobin level (if nitrites given)
BMP (anion gap acidosis)
ECG
notes:
Sources: house fires (combustion of synthetics), industrial (mining, electroplating), cyanogenic plants (cassava), nitroprusside toxicity
Mechanism: inhibits cytochrome c oxidase → blocks cellular respiration → histotoxic hypoxia
Cells can't use oxygen → high venous O2, lactic acidosis
Hydroxocobalamin: binds CN → cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), safe, turns urine/skin red
Nitrite-thiosulfate kit: nitrite creates methemoglobin (binds CN), thiosulfate converts to thiocyanate (renally excreted)
Avoid nitrites if concurrent CO poisoning (worsens oxygen-carrying capacity)
Fire victims: treat empirically for both CN and CO poisoning
description: Poisoning from cyanide exposure that prevents cells from using oxygen, causing rapid collapse and death if not treated immediately with specific antidotes like hydroxocobalamin.

name: Methanol Poisoning
signs:
Inebriation initially (may mimic ethanol)
Visual disturbances (blurred vision, blindness)
Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting
Headache
Altered mental status
Kussmaul breathing (acidosis)
Optic disc hyperemia on fundoscopy
Seizures (severe)
treatment:
1. Fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor) - antidote
2. Correct metabolic acidosis with sodium bicarbonate
3. Hemodialysis for severe poisoning
4. Folinic acid (enhances formate metabolism)
5. Ethanol drip if fomepizole unavailable
6. Supportive care
meds:
Fomepizole 15mg/kg IV loading then 10mg/kg q12h
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV (target pH >7.3)
Folinic acid (leucovorin) 50mg IV q6h
Ethanol drip 10% IV (target level 100-150mg/dL) if fomepizole unavailable
Thiamine 100mg IV (cofactor)
Hemodialysis
ddx: Ethanol intoxication, Ethylene glycol poisoning, DKA, Lactic acidosis, Isopropanol ingestion, Salicylate poisoning
labs:
ABG (severe anion gap metabolic acidosis)
Serum osmolality (elevated osmol gap early)
Methanol level (if available)
BMP (low bicarb, anion gap)
Osmol gap = measured - calculated osmolality (>10 = elevated)
Lactate
VBG
Urinalysis (no oxalate crystals = distinguishes from ethylene glycol)
Formate level (correlates with toxicity)
Fundoscopic exam
notes:
Methanol → formaldehyde → formic acid (toxic metabolite → retinal damage, metabolic acidosis)
Osmol gap elevated early (methanol), anion gap elevated late (formate accumulation)
Hemodialysis indications: visual symptoms, pH <7.25, renal failure, methanol >50mg/dL
Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase → prevents conversion to toxic metabolites
Sources: windshield washer fluid, antifreeze, moonshine, industrial solvents
Visual toxicity: formate damages retinal ganglion cells and optic nerve
Ethylene glycol (antifreeze): similar treatment but causes calcium oxalate crystals in urine
description: Poisoning from ingesting methanol (wood alcohol) that produces toxic metabolites causing severe metabolic acidosis and blindness, requiring urgent treatment with fomepizole and potentially dialysis.

name: Hodgkin Lymphoma
signs:
Painless cervical/supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (most common)
B symptoms: fever, night sweats, weight loss >10% in 6 months
Pruritus
Alcohol-induced lymph node pain (rare but classic)
Mediastinal mass (cough, SVC syndrome)
Hepato/splenomegaly
Pel-Ebstein fever (cyclic)
treatment:
1. Early stage favorable: ABVD x2-4 cycles + involved-site radiation
2. Early stage unfavorable: ABVD x4-6 cycles +/- radiation
3. Advanced stage: ABVD x6 cycles or BV-AVD x6 cycles
4. PET-adapted therapy (escalate or de-escalate based on interim PET)
5. Relapsed/refractory: salvage chemo then autologous transplant
6. Brentuximab vedotin + checkpoint inhibitors for relapsed disease
meds:
ABVD: Adriamycin + Bleomycin + Vinblastine + Dacarbazine q2wk
BV-AVD: Brentuximab vedotin + AVD (omit bleomycin)
Brentuximab vedotin 1.8mg/kg IV q3wk
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk (relapsed)
Nivolumab 3mg/kg IV q2wk (relapsed)
ICE or DHAP (salvage regimens)
G-CSF (filgrastim) for neutropenia
ddx: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma, Reactive lymphadenopathy, Sarcoidosis, Infectious mononucleosis, Tuberculosis, Metastatic carcinoma, Castleman disease
labs:
Excisional lymph node biopsy (Reed-Sternberg cells, CD15+/CD30+)
PET-CT (staging, treatment response)
CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis
CBC, ESR (prognostic)
BMP, LFTs, albumin
LDH
HIV, hepatitis B/C
PFTs and echocardiogram (baseline before chemo)
Bone marrow biopsy (if PET positive in marrow)
Pregnancy test (reproductive-age women)
notes:
Bimodal age distribution: 15-35 and >55 years
Classic HL (95%): Reed-Sternberg cells in inflammatory background
Reed-Sternberg: large binucleated cells, owl-eye appearance, CD15+/CD30+
Subtypes: nodular sclerosis (most common, young adults), mixed cellularity, lymphocyte-rich, lymphocyte-depleted
One of the most curable cancers: >80% cure rate overall
Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity: monitor PFTs, avoid high FiO2
Fertility preservation: discuss sperm/egg banking before treatment
Secondary malignancies: breast cancer, lung cancer, MDS/AML (long-term risk from radiation/chemo)
description: A cancer of the lymphatic system characterized by the presence of distinctive Reed-Sternberg cells, typically presenting as painless lymph node swelling in young adults, with high cure rates.

name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
signs:
Painless lymphadenopathy
B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss)
Splenomegaly
Abdominal pain or fullness
Cytopenias (bone marrow involvement)
Skin lesions (cutaneous lymphoma)
CNS symptoms (primary CNS lymphoma)
Recurrent infections
treatment:
1. Indolent (follicular): watch and wait if asymptomatic, rituximab-based when needed
2. Aggressive (DLBCL): R-CHOP x6 cycles (curative intent)
3. Highly aggressive (Burkitt): intensive chemo (R-CODOX-M/IVAC or similar)
4. Mantle cell: rituximab + bendamustine or intensive chemo + auto-transplant
5. CAR T-cell therapy for relapsed aggressive NHL
6. Radiation for localized disease
meds:
R-CHOP: Rituximab + Cyclophosphamide + Doxorubicin + Vincristine + Prednisone q3wk
Rituximab 375mg/m2 IV
Bendamustine 90mg/m2 IV + rituximab (indolent)
CAR T-cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel, lisocabtagene maraleucel)
Polatuzumab vedotin + R-CHP (DLBCL)
Lenalidomide + rituximab (indolent, maintenance)
Ibrutinib 560mg daily (mantle cell)
Tumor lysis prophylaxis: allopurinol, rasburicase, hydration
ddx: Hodgkin lymphoma, Reactive lymphadenopathy, Metastatic carcinoma, Sarcoidosis, CLL, Infections (TB, HIV, EBV), Castleman disease
labs:
Excisional lymph node biopsy (architecture + immunohistochemistry)
Flow cytometry
PET-CT (staging)
CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis
Bone marrow biopsy
CBC, LDH (prognostic), BMP, LFTs, uric acid
Hepatitis B (reactivation risk with rituximab)
HIV
FISH/cytogenetics (MYC, BCL2, BCL6 rearrangements)
LP if CNS lymphoma suspected
notes:
Most common hematologic malignancy
DLBCL: most common aggressive NHL (~30%), R-CHOP cures 60-70%
Follicular: most common indolent NHL, incurable but long survival (median >15 years)
Burkitt: highly aggressive, starry-sky pattern, MYC translocation t(8;14), cure rate >80% with intensive chemo
Double-hit lymphoma: MYC + BCL2 rearrangements, aggressive, treated with dose-adjusted chemo
Hep B reactivation: screen ALL patients before rituximab, give prophylaxis if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+
Tumor lysis syndrome: high risk with Burkitt, high LDH, bulky disease - prophylax with rasburicase
CAR T: cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity are major complications
description: A diverse group of blood cancers arising from lymphocytes, ranging from slow-growing (indolent) types that may be watched to aggressive types requiring immediate chemotherapy.

name: Von Willebrand Disease
signs:
Easy bruising
Mucocutaneous bleeding (epistaxis, menorrhagia, gingival bleeding)
Excessive bleeding after surgery or dental procedures
Prolonged bleeding time
Family history of bleeding
GI bleeding (from angiodysplasia in type 2)
Postpartum hemorrhage
treatment:
1. Desmopressin (DDAVP) for type 1 and some type 2 (first-line)
2. VWF-containing factor concentrates (Humate-P) for major bleeding or type 3
3. Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid) for mucosal bleeding
4. Hormonal therapy for menorrhagia
5. Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs
6. Pre-procedural prophylaxis based on type and severity
meds:
Desmopressin 0.3mcg/kg IV/SC or 300mcg intranasal (type 1)
Humate-P (VWF:RCo/FVIII) dose based on VWF activity level
Tranexamic acid 1g PO TID or 10mg/kg IV
Aminocaproic acid 50-100mg/kg PO q6h
Oral contraceptives or IUD (menorrhagia)
Topical thrombin for minor wounds
ddx: Hemophilia A/B, Platelet function disorders, Acquired VWD, Liver disease, DIC, Anticoagulant use, ITP
labs:
VWF antigen level
VWF activity (ristocetin cofactor assay, VWF:GPIbM)
Factor VIII activity (reduced, VWF carries/stabilizes FVIII)
PTT (prolonged or normal)
Bleeding time or PFA-100 (prolonged)
CBC (thrombocytopenia in type 2B)
VWF multimer analysis (type classification)
Blood type (type O has lower VWF levels)
DDAVP challenge test
notes:
Most common inherited bleeding disorder (1% prevalence)
Type 1 (75-80%): quantitative deficiency, mild, responds to DDAVP
Type 2 (15-20%): qualitative defects (2A, 2B, 2M, 2N subtypes)
Type 3 (<5%): severe, absent VWF, factor VIII very low, resembles hemophilia A
Type 2B: DDAVP contraindicated (causes thrombocytopenia via platelet aggregation)
Blood type O: naturally lower VWF levels (may be misdiagnosed as type 1)
Pregnancy: VWF levels rise (may normalize), but risk of postpartum hemorrhage
Acquired VWD: associated with aortic stenosis, lymphoproliferative disorders, hypothyroidism
description: The most common inherited bleeding disorder, caused by deficient or defective von Willebrand factor, leading to impaired platelet adhesion and excessive mucocutaneous bleeding.

name: Polycythemia Vera
signs:
Plethoric facies (ruddy complexion)
Splenomegaly
Pruritus (especially after warm bath/shower - aquagenic pruritus)
Headache
Dizziness
Visual disturbances
Erythromelalgia (burning pain in hands/feet)
Thrombosis (stroke, DVT, Budd-Chiari)
Gout
treatment:
1. Phlebotomy to target Hct <45% (cornerstone)
2. Low-dose aspirin 81mg daily
3. Cytoreductive therapy if high-risk: hydroxyurea (first-line)
4. Ruxolitinib for hydroxyurea-intolerant/resistant
5. Manage cardiovascular risk factors
6. Monitor for transformation to myelofibrosis or AML
meds:
Phlebotomy (500mL) to maintain Hct <45%
Aspirin 81mg daily
Hydroxyurea 500-1500mg daily (high-risk: age >60 or prior thrombosis)
Ruxolitinib 10-20mg BID (JAK inhibitor, second-line)
Anagrelide 0.5mg BID (thrombocytosis)
Allopurinol 300mg daily (hyperuricemia)
Antihistamines or SSRIs for pruritus
ddx: Secondary polycythemia (hypoxia, EPO-secreting tumor), Chronic hypoxia (COPD, OSA), Dehydration (relative polycythemia), Other MPNs (ET, PMF), EPO-producing renal tumor
labs:
CBC: elevated Hgb (>16.5 men, >16 women), elevated Hct, often elevated WBC and platelets
JAK2 V617F mutation (positive in 95%)
JAK2 exon 12 (if V617F negative)
EPO level (low in PV, elevated in secondary)
Bone marrow biopsy (hypercellular, panmyelosis, pleomorphic megakaryocytes)
Iron studies (often depleted from phlebotomy)
Uric acid
LDH
Peripheral smear
notes:
JAK2 V617F positive in ~95% - essentially diagnostic
WHO criteria: all 3 major (Hgb >16.5/16 OR Hct >49%/48%, BM biopsy, JAK2) OR first 2 major + 1 minor (low EPO)
Hct <45% target: CYTO-PV trial showed reduced thrombosis
High-risk: age >60 OR prior thrombosis → cytoreduction
Low-risk: phlebotomy + aspirin only
Transformation: 10-20% evolve to myelofibrosis, 5-10% to AML over decades
Aquagenic pruritus: pathognomonic, treat with SSRIs, antihistamines, phototherapy
Budd-Chiari syndrome: hepatic vein thrombosis, think PV in young patients
description: A chronic blood cancer where the bone marrow produces too many red blood cells, increasing blood thickness and the risk of dangerous blood clots, treated primarily with blood removal (phlebotomy).

name: Chronic Venous Insufficiency
signs:
Lower extremity edema (worse at end of day, improves with elevation)
Varicose veins
Skin discoloration (hemosiderin staining - brown)
Venous stasis dermatitis (eczematous changes)
Lipodermatosclerosis (indurated, inverted champagne bottle shape)
Venous ulcers (medial malleolus)
Pain/heaviness in legs with prolonged standing
treatment:
1. Compression therapy (30-40mmHg stockings) - cornerstone
2. Leg elevation above heart level
3. Exercise (calf muscle pump activation)
4. Wound care for venous ulcers (moist dressing, compression)
5. Endovenous ablation (laser or radiofrequency) for saphenous reflux
6. Sclerotherapy for varicose veins
7. Skin care to prevent breakdown
meds:
Compression stockings 30-40mmHg (knee-high or thigh-high)
Multilayer compression bandaging (acute ulcer management)
Topical emollients (skin hydration)
Topical corticosteroids (stasis dermatitis, short-term)
Pentoxifylline 400mg TID (adjunct for venous ulcers)
Aspirin 300mg daily (may improve ulcer healing)
Diuretics NOT routinely recommended
ddx: Lymphedema, DVT, PAD (arterial ulcers), CHF, Cellulitis, Lipoedema, Contact dermatitis, Nephrotic syndrome
labs:
Duplex ultrasound (venous reflux, >0.5sec reflux = significant)
ABI (rule out PAD before compression - ABI must be >0.8)
Wound culture if ulcer appears infected
CBC, BMP, albumin
HbA1c (diabetes impairs healing)
Venous plethysmography (if ultrasound inconclusive)
notes:
CEAP classification: C0-C6 based on clinical severity
C6 = active venous ulcer (medial malleolus is classic location)
ABI MUST be checked before applying compression (PAD + compression = disastrous)
Venous ulcers: shallow, irregular borders, medial malleolus, moist base
Arterial ulcers: deep, punched out, lateral malleolus/toes, painful, dry base
Compression reduces ulcer recurrence from 70% to 20%
Superficial venous ablation improves ulcer healing and reduces recurrence
Lifelong compression is recommended to prevent recurrence
description: A condition where leg veins cannot efficiently return blood to the heart, causing swelling, skin changes, and potentially painful ulcers near the ankles, managed primarily with compression therapy.

name: Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found incidentally on imaging)
Chest or back pain (expansion/rupture)
Hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve compression)
Dysphagia (esophageal compression)
SVC syndrome
Aortic regurgitation murmur
Unequal pulses
Widened mediastinum on CXR
treatment:
1. Blood pressure control (beta-blocker first-line)
2. Surveillance imaging at regular intervals
3. Surgery when size threshold reached or rapid growth
4. Open surgical repair or endovascular (TEVAR)
5. Genetic testing if Marfan or familial TAA
6. Screen first-degree relatives of familial cases
7. Avoid heavy lifting (isometric exercise)
meds:
Losartan 50-100mg daily (Marfan syndrome, preferred over BB)
Atenolol 25-100mg daily (beta-blocker)
Amlodipine 5-10mg daily (add-on BP control)
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Target SBP <130mmHg
Avoid fluoroquinolones (associated with aortic dissection/aneurysm)
ddx: Aortic dissection, Mediastinal mass, Lung cancer, Thymoma, Pericardial effusion, Esophageal disorders
labs:
CT angiography (gold standard for size and morphology)
TTE/TEE (aortic root, ascending aorta, valve)
MRA (surveillance, no radiation)
CXR (widened mediastinum, tracheal deviation)
Genetic testing: FBN1 (Marfan), TGFBR1/2 (Loeys-Dietz), ACTA2, MYH11
Lipid panel, HbA1c
ECG (LVH from aortic regurgitation)
notes:
Surgical thresholds: ascending >5.5cm (5.0cm if Marfan/bicuspid, 4.5cm if Loeys-Dietz), descending >6.0-6.5cm
Rapid growth >0.5cm/year or >1cm total: consider surgery regardless of size
Marfan syndrome: FBN1 mutation, aortic root dilation, lens subluxation, tall habitus
Bicuspid aortic valve: associated ascending aortic aneurysm (lower surgical threshold 5.0cm)
TEVAR: endovascular repair for descending thoracic aneurysms (lower morbidity than open)
Acute rupture: mortality >90% if prehospital, 50% if reaches OR
Fluoroquinolone warning: FDA black box for aortic dissection/aneurysm risk
description: An abnormal widening of the thoracic aorta that usually causes no symptoms but can be life-threatening if it ruptures or dissects, monitored with imaging and repaired surgically when large enough.

name: Arteriovenous Malformation
signs:
Often asymptomatic until hemorrhage
Intracranial hemorrhage (50% present with bleeding)
Seizures
Headache
Focal neurological deficits
Pulsatile mass (peripheral AVM)
Bruit over lesion
Heart failure (large AVMs with high-flow shunting)
treatment:
1. Multidisciplinary evaluation (neurosurgery, interventional neuroradiology)
2. Surgical resection (definitive for accessible AVMs)
3. Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) for deep/small AVMs
4. Endovascular embolization (pre-surgical or standalone)
5. Observation for unruptured AVMs in some patients (ARUBA trial)
6. Manage seizures if present
7. Hemorrhage: standard ICH management, consider AVM-directed treatment
meds:
Levetiracetam 500-1500mg BID (seizures)
Labetalol IV (BP control during hemorrhage)
Mannitol 1g/kg IV (elevated ICP)
Dexamethasone (peritumoral edema from radiosurgery)
Analgesics for headache
Anticoagulants generally AVOIDED
ddx: Brain tumor, Cavernous malformation, Dural AV fistula, Aneurysm, Arteriovenous fistula, Hemorrhagic stroke (other causes), Developmental venous anomaly
labs:
CT head (acute hemorrhage)
CT angiography (identify AVM)
Brain MRI with MRA (characterize nidus, feeding arteries, draining veins)
Conventional cerebral angiography (gold standard, shows angioarchitecture)
EEG if seizures
Spetzler-Martin grading (size, eloquence, venous drainage → surgical risk)
notes:
Spetzler-Martin grade: I-II low risk (surgery favored), III (individualized), IV-V high risk (observation or radiosurgery)
ARUBA trial: unruptured AVM observation had better outcomes than intervention (controversial, criticized)
Ruptured AVMs: higher rebleed risk (2-4%/year), more likely to benefit from treatment
Radiosurgery: takes 2-3 years for obliteration, hemorrhage risk persists until obliterated
Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT/Osler-Weber-Rendu): multiple AVMs in lungs, brain, liver, GI
Annual hemorrhage risk: 2-4% for unruptured, higher for ruptured
Pediatric AVMs: more likely to present with hemorrhage, consider treatment
description: An abnormal tangle of blood vessels connecting arteries directly to veins without normal capillaries in between, most dangerous when occurring in the brain where rupture can cause hemorrhagic stroke.

name: Hepatitis B
signs:
Fatigue and malaise
Jaundice
RUQ pain
Nausea, vomiting, anorexia
Dark urine and pale stools
Arthralgia
Urticaria
Fever
Hepatosplenomegaly
May be asymptomatic
treatment:
1. Acute: supportive care (most adults clear spontaneously, 95%)
2. Chronic: antiviral therapy if active disease
3. Monitor ALT, HBV DNA, HBeAg status
4. Screen for HCC: US + AFP q6 months
5. Vaccination for prevention (universal)
6. Screen household/sexual contacts
meds:
Tenofovir disoproxil 300mg daily (chronic, first-line)
Entecavir 0.5mg daily (chronic, first-line)
Tenofovir alafenamide 25mg daily (renal/bone safety)
Peg-interferon alfa-2a 180mcg SC weekly x48wk (finite therapy option)
HBIG + vaccine (neonatal prophylaxis for HBsAg+ mothers)
ddx: Hepatitis A, C, D, E, Alcoholic hepatitis, Drug-induced hepatitis, Autoimmune hepatitis, EBV/CMV hepatitis, Wilson disease
labs:
HBsAg (current infection)
Anti-HBs (immunity)
Anti-HBc IgM (acute) / IgG (prior exposure)
HBeAg and anti-HBe (replication status)
HBV DNA viral load
ALT/AST
CBC, BMP
Fibroscan or APRI/FIB-4 (fibrosis staging)
HCC screening: US + AFP q6 months
HDV co-infection testing
notes:
Serologic patterns: acute (HBsAg+, IgM anti-HBc+), chronic (HBsAg+ >6mo), immune (anti-HBs+, anti-HBc+), vaccinated (anti-HBs+ only)
Chronic infection risk inversely related to age at acquisition: 90% in neonates, 5% in adults
Treat if: ALT elevated + HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL, cirrhosis with any detectable DNA, immunosuppression
Reactivation risk: rituximab, chemotherapy, steroids → screen and prophylax
Vertical transmission prevention: HBIG + vaccine at birth, maternal tenofovir if high viral load
HDV (delta) co-infection: only with HBV, accelerates liver disease
description: A viral infection of the liver transmitted through blood and body fluids that can become chronic, leading to cirrhosis and liver cancer, preventable through vaccination.

name: Hepatitis C
signs:
Often asymptomatic for decades
Fatigue
Jaundice (acute, rare)
Arthralgia
Cirrhosis complications (late)
Cryoglobulinemia: purpura, arthralgia, glomerulonephritis
Porphyria cutanea tarda
Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (associated)
treatment:
1. Direct-acting antiviral (DAA) therapy for ALL patients with chronic HCV
2. Pangenotypic regimens preferred
3. Treatment is curative in >95% of cases
4. Screen for HCC if cirrhotic (continue even after SVR)
5. Screen for co-infections (HIV, HBV)
6. No vaccine available
meds:
Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) 400/100mg daily x12 weeks (pangenotypic, first-line)
Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret) 300/120mg daily x8-12 weeks (pangenotypic)
Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir/voxilaprevir x12 weeks (DAA-experienced)
Ribavirin 1000-1200mg daily (add-on for some genotypes/cirrhosis)
Avoid: protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis
ddx: Hepatitis B, Alcoholic liver disease, NAFLD/NASH, Autoimmune hepatitis, Drug-induced liver injury, Hemochromatosis, Wilson disease
labs:
HCV antibody (screening)
HCV RNA viral load (confirmatory, quantitative)
HCV genotype (guides some treatment decisions)
CBC, CMP, INR
Fibroscan or FIB-4/APRI (fibrosis assessment)
HIV, HBV testing
HCC screening if cirrhotic: US + AFP q6 months
SVR12: undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks after treatment = cure
notes:
Leading cause of liver transplantation in US (declining with DAA era)
Screening: universal one-time screening for all adults >=18 (CDC/USPSTF)
SVR (sustained virologic response) = cure, achieved in >95% with DAAs
Genotype 1 most common in US (70%)
Risk factors: IVDU (#1), blood transfusion before 1992, needle stick, intranasal drug use
Cirrhosis patients: continue HCC screening even after SVR (risk persists)
HBV reactivation: can occur during HCV treatment, screen HBV before starting DAAs
Acute HCV: 15-25% spontaneously clear, monitor 12-16 weeks before treating
description: A viral liver infection spread primarily through blood contact that can silently damage the liver over decades, now curable in over 95% of cases with modern antiviral medications.

name: Dengue Fever
signs:
High fever (104°F/40°C)
Severe headache (retro-orbital)
Myalgia and arthralgia (breakbone fever)
Maculopapular rash (islands of white in sea of red)
Nausea/vomiting
Petechiae
Warning signs: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Supportive care: IV fluids, analgesics
2. Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin (bleeding risk)
3. Monitor for severe dengue (dengue hemorrhagic fever/shock)
4. Fluid resuscitation for dengue shock syndrome
5. Platelet transfusion only for severe bleeding
6. Mosquito avoidance (prevention)
meds:
Acetaminophen 1g q6h (fever/pain, AVOID NSAIDs/aspirin)
IV NS or LR for dehydration
Oral rehydration solution
Platelet transfusion only if active significant bleeding
No antiviral treatment available
Dengue vaccine (Dengvaxia) for seropositive individuals in endemic areas
ddx: Chikungunya, Zika, Malaria, Typhoid fever, Leptospirosis, Influenza, Measles, Rickettsial infection
labs:
Dengue NS1 antigen (positive days 1-5)
Dengue IgM/IgG serology (positive after day 5)
CBC: thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, hemoconcentration (rising Hct)
BMP, LFTs (elevated transaminases)
Coagulation studies
Ultrasound (pleural effusion, ascites in severe dengue)
Cross-reactive with Zika serology
notes:
Aedes aegypti mosquito vector (also transmits Zika, chikungunya, yellow fever)
4 serotypes (DENV-1 to 4): infection with one provides lifelong immunity to that serotype
Second infection with different serotype: higher risk of severe dengue (antibody-dependent enhancement)
Severe dengue: plasma leakage (hemoconcentration, effusions), severe bleeding, organ impairment
Critical phase: defervescence (day 3-7) when complications typically occur
Dengvaxia vaccine: only for previously infected (seropositive) - worsens outcomes in seronegative
Tourniquet test: >=20 petechiae in 1 inch square after BP cuff inflation = positive
description: A mosquito-borne viral infection causing high fever, severe body aches, and rash, with risk of progressing to life-threatening hemorrhagic fever, especially upon second infection with a different strain.

name: Botulism
signs:
Descending flaccid paralysis
Cranial nerve involvement first: diplopia, ptosis, dysarthria, dysphagia
Dry mouth
Fixed dilated pupils
Symmetric weakness
Respiratory failure
Constipation
No fever
Infant: floppy baby, poor feeding, weak cry, constipation
treatment:
1. Antitoxin immediately (do not wait for lab confirmation)
2. Respiratory monitoring and intubation if needed
3. Supportive ICU care
4. Wound debridement (wound botulism)
5. Report to public health (potential bioterrorism agent)
6. BabyBIG (botulism immune globulin) for infant botulism
meds:
Heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (BAT) IV (adults, from CDC)
BabyBIG (botulism immune globulin IV) for infant botulism
Metronidazole or penicillin (wound botulism adjunct)
No antibiotics needed for foodborne botulism
Avoid aminoglycosides (worsen neuromuscular blockade)
Mechanical ventilation support
ddx: Guillain-Barré syndrome (ascending, not descending), Myasthenia gravis, Stroke, Tick paralysis, Organophosphate poisoning, Lambert-Eaton syndrome, Poliomyelitis
labs:
Botulinum toxin assay (serum, stool, wound, or food - send to CDC/state lab)
Stool culture for C. botulinum
EMG: incremental response to rapid repetitive stimulation (opposite of MG)
Nerve conduction studies
CBC, BMP (usually normal)
Lumbar puncture: normal CSF (helps exclude GBS)
Brain imaging: normal
notes:
Descending paralysis distinguishes from GBS (ascending)
Types: foodborne (preformed toxin in food), wound (IVDU, especially black tar heroin), infant (honey, ingestion of spores), iatrogenic (Botox overdose)
Clostridium botulinum toxin: blocks acetylcholine release at NMJ
Bioterrorism category A agent
Infant botulism: most common form in US, <12 months, constipation first symptom, avoid honey
Antitoxin prevents progression but does not reverse existing paralysis
Recovery: weeks to months, may need prolonged ventilatory support
Call CDC Emergency Operations Center (770-488-7100) for antitoxin
description: A rare but serious paralytic illness caused by a nerve toxin produced by Clostridium botulinum bacteria, causing descending muscle weakness that can lead to respiratory failure.

name: Candidiasis (Invasive)
signs:
Persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibiotics
Hypotension
Endophthalmitis (eye exam finding)
Skin lesions (pink papular/nodular)
Hepatic/splenic abscesses
Often in ICU patients with central lines
Renal involvement
No specific signs (high index of suspicion needed)
treatment:
1. Echinocandin first-line (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin)
2. Remove all intravascular catheters
3. Blood cultures q48h until negative
4. Dilated fundoscopic exam (rule out endophthalmitis)
5. Step-down to fluconazole once susceptibilities known and clinically stable
6. Duration: 14 days after first negative blood culture
meds:
Caspofungin 70mg IV day 1 then 50mg daily
Micafungin 100mg IV daily
Anidulafungin 200mg IV day 1 then 100mg daily
Fluconazole 800mg then 400mg daily (step-down, susceptible species)
Amphotericin B lipid formulation 3-5mg/kg IV daily (salvage)
Flucytosine 25mg/kg PO QID (adjunct for endophthalmitis/CNS)
ddx: Bacterial sepsis, Aspergillosis, Endocarditis, Drug fever, Central line infection (bacterial), Disseminated histoplasmosis
labs:
Blood cultures (positive in ~50% of invasive candidiasis)
Beta-D-glucan (elevated, >80pg/mL suggestive)
T2Candida panel (rapid molecular detection)
Dilated fundoscopic exam (all patients)
Echocardiogram if persistent candidemia
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Candida species identification and susceptibility testing
CT abdomen if hepatosplenic candidiasis suspected
notes:
Candida albicans most common species, but non-albicans increasing (C. glabrata, C. auris)
C. auris: multidrug-resistant, often resistant to fluconazole and sometimes echinocandins, infection control challenge
Catheter removal: essential, reduces mortality by 40%
Echinocandins: preferred empiric therapy (active against most Candida including C. glabrata)
Fluconazole step-down: appropriate for susceptible species after clinical improvement
Chronic disseminated (hepatosplenic): treat until lesions resolve, often needs months
Mortality: 40-60% attributable mortality in candidemia
description: A serious fungal bloodstream infection that occurs primarily in hospitalized and immunocompromised patients, requiring prompt antifungal treatment and removal of infected catheters.

name: Achalasia
signs:
Dysphagia to both solids AND liquids (simultaneously)
Regurgitation of undigested food
Chest pain
Weight loss
Heartburn (paradoxical, from fermentation)
Nocturnal cough (aspiration)
Halitosis
Difficulty belching
treatment:
1. Pneumatic dilation (graded balloon dilation)
2. Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication
3. Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM)
4. Botulinum toxin injection into LES (temporizing)
5. Medical therapy (nitrates, CCBs) rarely adequate
6. Rule out pseudoachalasia (malignancy) in older patients
meds:
Nifedipine 10-30mg SL before meals (temporizing, poor efficacy)
Isosorbide dinitrate 5mg SL before meals
Botulinum toxin 100 units endoscopic injection into LES (temporary 6-12 months)
Sildenafil 50mg before meals (limited evidence)
Medical therapy: generally disappointing, used as bridge
ddx: Esophageal cancer (pseudoachalasia), Chagas disease, Esophageal stricture, GERD, Esophageal spasm, Eosinophilic esophagitis, Scleroderma esophagus
labs:
Barium swallow (bird's beak sign, dilated esophagus, absent peristalsis)
Esophageal manometry (gold standard: absent peristalsis + failed LES relaxation)
EGD (rule out mechanical obstruction and pseudoachalasia)
CT chest (rule out mass/pseudoachalasia in patients >60 or rapid onset)
Chicago classification (HRM subtypes: Type I, II, III)
notes:
Type II achalasia: best response to all treatments (panesophageal pressurization)
Type III: spastic, worst response to pneumatic dilation, may respond better to POEM
Bird's beak: smooth tapering of distal esophagus on barium swallow
Pseudoachalasia: malignancy (especially GEJ adenocarcinoma) mimicking achalasia - suspect if age >60, rapid onset, weight loss
Chagas disease: Trypanosoma cruzi causes secondary achalasia (endemic in Latin America)
POEM: endoscopic myotomy, excellent efficacy, higher GERD rates post-procedure
Esophageal cancer risk: slightly increased in longstanding achalasia (squamous cell carcinoma)
description: A rare swallowing disorder where the esophagus loses its ability to squeeze food down and the valve into the stomach doesn't relax properly, causing difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids.

name: Esophageal Varices
signs:
Hematemesis (large-volume, bright red or coffee ground)
Melena
Hypotension and tachycardia
Pallor
Signs of cirrhosis (jaundice, ascites, spider angiomata)
Hepatic encephalopathy (with acute bleed)
Shock in massive hemorrhage
treatment:
1. Resuscitation: IV access, fluids, blood products
2. IV octreotide/somatostatin analog (reduce portal pressure)
3. IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone, reduces mortality in acute variceal bleed)
4. Emergent EGD with band ligation (within 12h)
5. Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore) as bridge to definitive therapy
6. TIPS for refractory bleeding
7. Primary prophylaxis: beta-blocker or band ligation
meds:
Octreotide 50mcg IV bolus then 50mcg/hr infusion x3-5 days
Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily x7 days
Pantoprazole 40mg IV BID
Nadolol 20-80mg daily (primary/secondary prophylaxis)
Propranolol 20-40mg BID (prophylaxis)
Target HR reduction 25% or HR <55
Carvedilol 6.25-12.5mg daily (alternative, may be superior)
Rifaximin 550mg BID (if concurrent encephalopathy)
ddx: Peptic ulcer bleeding, Mallory-Weiss tear, Gastric varices, Portal hypertensive gastropathy, Esophageal cancer, Dieulafoy lesion, Aortoenteric fistula
labs:
CBC (hemoglobin, platelets)
BMP, LFTs
INR/PT
Type and crossmatch (prepare 4-6 units PRBCs)
Lactate
Blood cultures (SBP risk during variceal bleed)
EGD (diagnostic and therapeutic)
MELD score
Child-Pugh score
notes:
Screen for varices at diagnosis of cirrhosis (EGD)
Small varices: beta-blocker prophylaxis
Medium/large varices: beta-blocker OR band ligation for primary prophylaxis
Transfusion target: Hgb 7-8 g/dL (over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding)
TIPS: reduces portal pressure, risk of encephalopathy (30-50%), used for refractory bleeding
Beta-blocker: target resting HR 55-60 or 25% reduction from baseline
Baveno VII consensus: noninvasive screening (liver stiffness + platelet count) can avoid EGD in compensated cirrhosis
6-week mortality after variceal bleed: 15-25% (improving with modern management)
description: Enlarged veins in the esophagus caused by increased pressure in the portal vein from liver disease, which can rupture and cause life-threatening bleeding.

name: Lower GI Bleed
signs:
Hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum)
Maroon stools
Abdominal cramping
Hemodynamic instability (large volume)
Rectal bleeding
Melena (if proximal source with slow transit)
Signs of shock in severe cases
treatment:
1. Resuscitation: IV access, fluids, blood products
2. Risk stratification (Oakland score)
3. Colonoscopy within 24h (after bowel prep) for diagnosis and therapy
4. CT angiography for brisk bleeding (>0.5mL/min)
5. Angiographic embolization for active bleeding on CTA
6. Surgery for refractory/massive bleeding
7. Treat underlying cause
meds:
IV crystalloid resuscitation
PRBC transfusion (target Hgb 7-8)
Reverse anticoagulation if applicable: vitamin K, PCC, idarucizumab
GoLYTELY 1L/hr prep for urgent colonoscopy
